column3: The nickel foam was ultrasonicated in acetone, 10% HCl aqueous solution, and DI water successively and then vacuum-dried at 60 °C. A previous method of preparation of Fe-doped nickel sulfide (FeNi2S4)  was suitably modified to support dual borate/Fe doping and catalyst deposition on a nickel foam. Briefly, 1.5 mmol FeSO4 7H2O, 2 mmol NiSO4·6H2O, 1.5 mmol K2B4O7·4H2O, and 8 mmol l-cysteine were dissolved in 40 mL of a glycol–DI water mixture (1:1 v/v) and rigorously stirred for 30 min. This mixture and a pretreated nickel foam (3 cm × 2 cm) were transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the nickel foam was removed, washed with water several times, and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum. For comparative studies, Fe-free borate-doped Ni3S2 was also prepared by the same procedure sans the use of iron sulfate. Likewise, borate/Fe-free undoped Ni3S2 was also prepared without Fe and borate precursors. The catalyst loading on NF was in the range of 4–6 mg cm–2.

Ligand L1 (10 mg, 0.03 mmol), sodium terephthalate (6.2 mg, 0.03 mmol), and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (8.6 mg, 0.03 mmol) were taken in a glass tube. To this, 3 mL of methanol and 7 mL of deionized water were added and the glass tube was sealed and heated at 100 °C for 2 days and then slowly cooled to room temperature, resulting in red-colored single crystals of MOF 1. Yield 70–75%.

Ligand L1 (10 mg, 0.03 mmol), sodium terephthalate (6.2 mg, 0.03 mmol), and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (8.7 mg, 0.03 mmol) were taken in a glass tube. To this, 5 mL of methanol and 5 mL of deionized water were added and the glass tube was sealed and heated at 100 °C for 2 days and then slowly cooled to room temperature. Green-colored single crystals of MOF 2 were obtained in 75–80% yield.

Ligand L2 (10 mg, 0.03 mmol) and sodium terephthalate (6.2 mg, 0.03 mmol) were taken in a glass tube. To this, 3 mL of methanol and 7 mL of deionized water were added. Co(SO4)·7H2O (8.4 mg, 0.03 mmol) was dissolved in 2 mL of methanol and added to it. The glass tube was sealed and heated at 100 °C for 3 days and then slowly cooled down to room temperature. Bright-red-colored single crystals of MOF 3 were obtained in 65–70% yield.

Ligand L2 (10 mg, 0.03 mmol) and sodium terephthalate (6.2 mg, 0.03 mmol) were taken in a glass tube. To this, 3 mL of methanol and 7 mL of deionized water were added. A 2 mL methanolic solution of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (8.7 mg, 0.03 mmol) was added to the above-mentioned mixture. The glass tube was sealed and heated at 100 °C for 3 days and then slowly cooled to room temperature. Deep green-colored single crystals of MOF 4 were obtained in 70–75% yield.

Before the synthesis of materials, a piece of nickel foam (the geometric size is 4 cm × 2 cm) was carefully treated with diluted hydrochloric acid (3 M), acetone, and deionized water in sequential order under ultrasonic conditions for 30 min each time. Next, 32 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 2 mL of deionized water, and 2 mL of ethanol were mixed in a glass container. Subsequently, 0.1246 g (0.75 mmol) of BDC was dissolved in the mixed solution under continuous stirring. Then, 0.1188 g (0.5 mmol) of NiCl2·6H2O and 0.0320 g (0.25 mmol) of VCl2 were added. A green solution formed under continuous magnetic stirring for 30 min. After that, the mother solution was ultrasonically shocked (40 kHz) with a piece of pretreated nickel foam for 8 h at room temperature. Afterward, the mother solution and nickel foam were diverted into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE) autoclave, which was then sealed and heated at 140 °C for 24 h. After being cooled down to room temperature, nickel foam was taken out and washed with ethanol (3–4 times), while the powder deposited on the bottom of the autoclave was obtained through centrifugation. Finally, the products were vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 12 h and were denoted Ni2V-MOFs@NF. Three other MOF@NF electrocatalysts (e.g., NiV-MOFs@NF, NiV2-MOFs@NF, and Ni-MOFs@NF) were prepared according to the same procedure except for the atomic ratios of NiCl2·6H2O to VCl2.

column2: {'PRECURSOR': 'Ni(NO3)2·6H2O'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'NaMoO4·2H2O'}, {'SOLVENT': 'H2O'}, {'SUBSTRATE': 'Ni(OH)2-grown nickel foam'}, {'TARGET': 'NiO@NiMoO4 nanosheets grown on nickel foam'}
column3: A homogeneous solution consisting of 0.197 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.125 mmol of NaMoO4·2H2O in 15 mL of H2O was poured into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After the addition of the above Ni(OH)2-grown nickel foam, the autoclave was maintained for 6 h at 110 °C. The nickel foam was dried at 50 °C overnight after rinsing with water and ethanol. NiO@NiMoO4 nanosheets grown on nickel foam were obtained after being calcined at 350 °C for 2 h.

Co(OH)F/NF was synthesized according to a previously reported method.  Typically, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (1 mmol), NH4F (4 mmol), and urea (5 mmol) were dissolved in 18 mL of dihydrogen oxide at room temperature. NF (3 × 1) cm2 was ultrasonically soaked in acetone, 2 M hydrochloric acid, and absolute ethanol, respectively, for 10 min. Then, the NF and reaction mixture were placed into a 25 mL Teflon-lined flask and heated at 100 °C for 6 h, and then the mixture was cooled to ambient temperature within 15 min using ice water. The resulting Co(OH)F/NF was ultrasonically washed with distilled water and ethanol and vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 8 h.

To grow Co-MOF crystals on NF, the Co(OH)F/NF precursor was immersed into a mixed solution of dihydrogen oxide and acetonitrile (Vacetonitrile:VH2O = 1:1) in which were dissolved different concentrations of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5 mmol), Hatz (0.1 mmol), and H4bta (0.1 mmol) in a 25 mL autoclave, and the product was obtained by the same procedure used for preparation of 1, denoted Cox(Hatz)0.1(H4bta)0.1/NF (x = 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5). The resulting Co-MOF/NFs were cleaned and vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 8 h.

Co/Mn-MOF was synthesized by a solvothermal method reported previously, which is shown in Scheme 1. Briefly, 0.08 g of cobalt(II) acetate tetrahydrate, 0.08 g of manganese acetate, and 0.16 g of BDC were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF, 10 mL of ethanol, and 10 mL of distilled water. The mixture was ultrasonicated for a few minutes and stirred at room temperature. The solution was then kept under continuous magnetic stirring for 2 h till it formed a clear solution. Then, the mixture solution was poured into a 100 mL polymer-coated steel autoclave and kept in an oven at 150 °C for 24 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool completely at room temperature, and the product solution was centrifuged with DMF and ethanol several times and dried in an oven at 90 °C for 12 h. The substance was then transferred to a sample vial and stored for further characterizations.

The composite was prepared using a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis method (Scheme 2). First, 0.7 g of terephthalic acid and 0.3 g of cobalt acetate were dissolved in a beaker containing 20 mL of ethanol solution, and the solution was marked as A. Second, in another beaker, 0.3 g of manganese acetate was dissolved in 40 mL of DI water, and it was marked as B. Solution B was then poured rapidly into solution A, which was a TPA (terephthalic acid)–ethanolic acid solution under magnetic stirring; without any delay, 1 mL of CND solution was added to the above mixture and allowed to stir for 1 h. The rest of the methodology was the same as the MOF synthesis described above. Then, the precipitate was centrifuged three times with ethanol and distilled water and dried at 60 °C for 6 h. The obtained dried compound was stored for analysis, and the catalytic activity (OER) was examined.

To prepare Ni-BDC-modified NiSO hollow nanoparticles, 15 mg of NiSO hollow nanoparticles was dispersed in 18 mL of DMF by sonication for 20 min then 31 mg of terephthalic acid and 50 mg of PVP were added. Finally, the solution was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for a solvothermal reaction at 120 °C for 24 h. The final product was washed and dried using the same method as described in Section 2.3.

NiCl2 (45 mg) was dissolved in 18 mL of DMF by sonication for 5 min then 31 mg of terephthalic acid and 50 mg of PVP were added. Finally, the solution was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for a solvothermal reaction at 120 °C for 24 h. The final product was washed and dried using the same method as described in Section 2.3.

By a re-solvothermal treatment, the above Ni-BDC could be converted to a reported MOF, Ni3(OH)2(BDC)2(H2O)2; thus, we inferred the Ni-BDC to be a precursor of Ni3(OH)2(BDC)2(H2O)2. Briefly, in this transformative synthesis, 15 mg of Ni-BDC nanoparticles was dispersed in 18 mL of a cosolvent of DMF, ethanol, and deionized water (DMF/ethanol/water = 16:1:1), and then the suspension was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave for the second solvothermal treatment at 120 °C for 24 h, noting that the Ni-BDC precursor here was prepared from the first solvothermal reaction (Section 2.7). Finally, the product was collected by centrifuging and was washed with anhydrous ethanol several times and finally air-dried at 60 °C.

In this process, briefly, 4.0 mL of the above obtained suspension of Ni nanoparticles, 20 mL of toluene, and 1.0 mL of 1-dodecanethiol were mixed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 30 mL and then kept inside an electric oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, it was then cooled naturally. The product was collected by washing and centrifuging with anhydrous ethanol three times and finally air-dried at 60 °C.

The NXXX-Co3O4 were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal reaction. Briefly, 58 mg of Co(NO)3·H2O and 4 mg of NaNO3 were dissolved in 10 mL of water to form a homogeneous solution. A piece of carbon paper (CP) (1 cm × 4 cm) was immersed in the above solution, and we then added 9 mL of ammonium hydroxide. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 140 °C for 12 h. After being washed with deionized water and ethanol several times, the product was dried at room temperature. This NXXX-Co3O4 product was designated as N1-Co3O4. When the amounts of ammonium hydroxide were 18 and 27 mL, the Co3O4 products were denoted as N2-Co3O4 and N3-Co3O4, respectively.

CFP was cut into 10 mm × 60 mm pieces and ultrasonically cleaned by ethanol, H2SO4 solution, and water in sequence, resulting in a hydrophilic surface. Then, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.87 g), CO(NH2)2 (0.90 g), and NH4F (0.22 g) were dissolved in 80 mL of water, and the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. One piece of CFP was immersed in the solution. The autoclave was then heated in an electric oven at 120 °C for 7 h, and naturally cooled to room temperature. The products (CoCH NW) were rinsed ultrasonically several times with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 80 °C in air.

Herein, a series of LnaIrbOxOHy (Ln = lanthanides) with the ratio of a/b equal to 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 2, and 3 were hydrothermally synthesized following the 300 °C calcination process. A fixed volume of 3.75 mL of 36.417 mM H2IrCl6·xH2O was added in the Teflon-lined vessel, followed by the addition of the specific volume of 0.1 M Ln(NO3)3·6H2O according to the a/b value in this work. 3 mL of 1 M NaOH was added to the solution and naturally reacted for several minutes. 17 mL of deionized water was subsequently added, and the Teflon-lined pressure vessel was encased using a stainless steel reactor and transferred to the oven. The hydrothermal synthesis condition was set to 180° for 12 h. The raw catalyst was calcined in the furnace at 300 °C, lasting for 6 h with natural cooling. The synthesis of pyrochlore Lu2Ir2O7 and IrO2 was done following the method used in our previous studies.  The low-crystallinity IrOx was synthesized at 300 °C following the above-mentioned step without the addition of lanthanides.

Ni(OH)2@IF was synthesized by the hydrothermal method. First of all, the purchased commercial iron foam (IF) is cut into pieces of 1 × 2 cm2 and washed with acetone and absolute ethyl alcohol for 20 min, respectively. The dried IF was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave containing 0.281 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.3 g of urea, and 36 mL of deionized water. After heating for 12 h at 120 °C, IF was taken out and rinsed with deionized water several times and dried under vacuum.

The precursor solutions were added to a mixed solution of deionized water (30 mL) and dimethylformamide (DMF) (16 mL) in an autoclave liner and sonicated for 5 min. The solvothermal reaction was then performed at 130 °C for 16 h, followed by additional solvothermal reaction at 170 °C for 2 h. The autoclave was naturally cooled down, and the obtained suspension was ultrasonicated and washed with a centrifuge 2 times in deionized water and ethanol mixture, followed by 2 times in pure deionized water. The sample was then freeze-dried overnight. A yellowish-orange powder was obtained.

Ni(OH)2 nanosheets were synthesized by a modified solvothermal reaction method.  For the synthesis of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets, 0.9 mmol of nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate and 1.8 mmol of urea were dissolved in a mixed solution of DI water (12 mL) and ethylene glycol (EG, 28 mL). After it was stirred for 1 h, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for solvothermal reaction at 120 °C for 12 h. After the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed with DI water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C in an oven for 10 h. For the synthesis of Co(OH)2 nanosheets, 0.9 mmol of cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate and 1.8 mmol of hexamethylenetetramine (HMT) were dissolved in a mixed solution of DI water (16 mL) and EG (24 mL). Then, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for solvothermal reaction at 120 °C for 10 h. The products were collected by centrifugation, washed three times with DI water and ethanol, and eventually dried at 60 °C in an oven for 10 h.

The synthesis procedure of Ni0.85Fe0.15(OH)2 LDH nanosheets is similar to that of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets. A 0.75 mmol portion of nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate, 0.15 mmol of iron(III) nitrate nonahydrate, and 1.8 mmol of urea were dissolved in a mixed solvent of DI water (12 mL) and EG (28 mL). After it was stirred for 1 h, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for solvothermal reaction at 120 °C for 12 h. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed three times with DI water and ethanol, and eventually dried in an oven at 60 °C for 10 h.

260 mg of Al(NO3)3·9H2O, 260 mg of PVP was dissolved in the mixture of 10.0 mL of ethanol, 10.0 mL of H2O, and 5.0 mL of acetone, followed by addition of 0.15 mL of Ni(NO3)2 (0.5 M). The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave which was then kept at 180 °C for 24 h. Thus-resulting precipitate was washed twice with ethanol and dried for further use.

For synthesizing both Cu3Sn and selenized Cu3Sn over Cu foam catalysts, we have followed a double hydrothermal treatment. At first, to prepare Cu3Sn over Cu foam, acid pretreated Cu foams (4 × 0.5) were cut down and to which 0.5 M of SnCl2 is added along with 3 M KOH in 30 mL of H2O and kept in a 50 mL autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. After this, the as formed Cu3Sn@Cu foam which is gray white color was again subjected to another hydrothermal condition. In second, Cu3Sn@Cu foam is taken along with 0.1 M NaBH4 and 0.02 M Se and was added in 30 mL of H2O in a 50 mL autoclave and again subjected to hydrothermal condition at 180 °C for 12 h. Similarly, Cu7Se4@Cu foam was prepared by following similar protocols except the addition of SnCl2 into the reaction mixture. After which, the as-formed black selenized Cu3Sn@Cu foam was washed with DI water and ethanol several times and dried for further studies. Details of the materials used and sample preparation techniques for characterization have been given in the Supporting Information.

In a typical fabrication procedure, 2 mmol of Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O and 1 mmol of Al(NO)3·9H2O were completely dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. Then, 8 mmol of NH4F and 12 mmol of CO(NH2)2 were added to the above solution with vigorous stirring. After 30 min, the resulting solution and a piece of CC were transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 120 °C for 800 min. After cooling down, the Co2Al1 LDH/CC was obtained and rinsed with deionized water. A similar method as described above was used for the preparation of CoxAl3–x LDH/CC (x = 1, 1.5, and 2.5), where x represents the moles of Co in the original reaction solution. The loading amount of catalysts on CC was calculated as 1.02–1.27 mg cm–2. To study the effect of reaction time on the electrocatalytic performance of catalysts, we prepared Co2Al1 LDH/CC under different reaction times, such as 400, 1200, and 1600 min. In addition, the Co LDH/CC and AlF2OH/CC were also synthesized by a method similar to that of the Co2Al1 LDH/CC without adding Al(NO)3·9H2O and Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O, respectively. Note that the total molar quantity of metal ions was 3 mmol.

In a typical synthetic process, CoCl2·6H2O (1.19 g, 5 mmol) and urea (1.1 g 18.3 mmol) were mixed with mild heating (80 °C) and stirring for obtaining the DES. When the dark-blue DES is formed, the DES was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Then, the autoclave was put in an oven at 180 °C for 21 h. After this, the sealed autoclave was immersed in water to cool to room temperature rapidly. The DES was totally transformed to rufous products. After being washed with ethanol for three times, the products were dried at room temperature in a vacuum drying oven for a day.

The NiCo-LDHs/CP electrode was prepared via a facile hydrothermal method. First, 0.65 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.325 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (the molar radio of Ni:Co = 2:1), 7.5 mmol of hexamethylenetetramine (HMT), and the CP were added into a mixed solution of water (80 mL) in order to undergo ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Then, the solution and substrate were transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the electrode was ultrasonicated with ethanol to remove the weak bonds of NiCo-LDHs on CP. Subsequently, the as-made sample was rinsed with deionized water and ethanol, respectively, and then dried at 80 °C.

All the catalysts were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis in Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves. For m-CoVOx, an aqueous solution containing 30 mM of cobalt(II) chloride (CoCl2, anhydrous, > 98%, Fluka), 10 mM of vanadium(III) chloride (VCl3, anhydrous, 97%, Sigma), and 63 mM urea (99%, ACS reagent) was loaded into the autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 14 h. The control samples (CoOx and VOx), based on only one of the two metals, were prepared by the same procedure except only the desired metal salt (CoCl2 or VCl3) was used with a concentration of 40 mM to keep the total amount of metal cations constant. After the hydrothermal step, the powders were separated by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min and washed three times with a 50:50 mixture of H2O and ethanol, with centrifugation between each washing step. The powders were then collected and dried overnight under vacuum at room temperature.

99.4 mg ferrous chloride (FeCl2·4H2O, 0.5 mmol) and 17.1 mg molybdenum(V) chloride (MoCl5, 0.063 mmol) were dissolved in 15 mL ethanol. The liquor was stirred continuously for 30 min and transferred to a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, then dried Ni foam was also placed in the autoclave. The reactor was kept at 125 °C for 12 h and was cooled down to the room temperature. Ni foam with amorphous Fe–Mo–O nanostructures was removed, cleaned for a few times with deionized ethanol/water, and then dried overnight at 50 °C before use.

A piece of Cu foam was cut into 4 × 2 cm specification, cleaned by 3 M HCl solution for 10 min to remove the oxide on the surface, and then washed ultrasonically with ethanol and deionized water, separately. The cleaned Cu foam was immersed into a 60 mL aqueous solution containing 3 M NaOH and 0.15 M (NH4)2S2O8 for 20 min to obtain Cu(OH)2 NWs. After washing, the Cu(OH)2 NWs was dried in air and then calcined at 180 °C for 1 h to obtain CuO NWs. Finally, 0.01 g of CH4N2S was dispersed ultrasonically in 30 mL of ethylene glycol. The CuO NWs and ethylene glycol solution of thiourea were heated at 180 °C in a 40 mL Teflon-lined pressure vessel for 12 h. Then, the prepared amorphous CuS substrate was washed with deionized water and then dried in vacuum conditions.

In a typical synthesis, CoCl2·6H2O (13 mmol, 50 mg) and excess thiourea (65 mmol, 150 mg) were taken in a 100 mL beaker and dissolved in a DMF–water mixture (1:1) under constant stirring. In another beaker, required amounts of SeO2 (13 mmol, 22 mg) and N2H4·H2O (0.5 mL) were added into a freshly prepared aqueous solution of 0.5 M NaOH (5 mL). The thus-prepared solution was added dropwise to the mixture of Co2+ and thiourea, and the solution was stirred for another 30 min. The concentration (in mol) of Co2+, thiourea, and SeO2 was kept at a ratio of 1:5:1 in the final reaction mixture. This mixture was then transferred to a 15 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The temperature of the autoclave was slowly raised to 180 °C in an oven and kept for 24 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the black colored product was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with water and ethanol. The as-synthesized product was dried under vacuum overnight before subjecting to further analysis. To understand the role of Se in the growth, the heterostructures were synthesized at different molar ratios of Co2+ and Se (2:1 and 1:2). The catalyst obtained at the two Se molar ratio is referred to as SCoSe-I and SCoSe-II. The synthesis procedure of pristine Co9S8 and CoSe2 is discussed in the Supporting Information.

First, the core Co3O4 nanostructure was hydrothermally synthesized using the method with modification as previously reported.  Co (NO3)2·6H2O was added into a Teflon bottle followed by the addition of NH4F and urea. All these chemicals were added in proportion 1:2:2, respectively. Then, after addition of 20 mL of deionized water into the respective solution, the mixture was autoclaved for hydrothermal treatment of 6 h at 150 °C. All the chemicals used were of analytical grade and required no further purification. After suction filtering, the product was obtained and the retentate was then dehydrated in an oven at 80 °C for 1 h. Finally, the dried product was transferred to a furnace for 2 h at 400 °C in order to achieve a highly crystalline product after the removal of the water of crystallization.

On weighing a fixed mass of the prepared Co3O4 powder, 10 mL of deionized water was added to the samples along with the addition of different amounts of iridium chloride precursor solution (IrCl3·6H2O). The volume of the iridium precursor solution was taken according to the different required molar ratios of both metallic constituents in composites. The samples were then ultrasonicated for 15–20 min to form homogeneous ink. Later, 15 mL of 0.1 M NaOH was added followed by addition of 0.4 mL of H2O2. Then, the samples were placed in autoclaves for hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 6 h. The obtained product was then suction filtered, and the retentate was preceded for calcination in a muffle furnace for 6 h at 400 °C.

Preparation of iridium dioxide was carried out hydrothermally in a single step, followed by a dwelling elapse of 10 h at 400 °C to ensure good crystallinity. IrCl3·3H2O at 56.7 mmol/lit was used as the precursor for the process. A 15 mL portion of 0.5 M NaOH and 0.4 mL of H2O2 were further mixed with a precursor solution in 40 mL Teflon-lined pressure vessels. An oxidizing agent was added to avoid the emergence of unwanted metal species.  The vessel containing the solution was then loaded in the oven to be heated for 10 h at 200 °C, and then was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The obtained product was suction-filtered and thoroughly washed approximately five times to remove unwanted and excess sodium ions. Then, the retentate was dehydrated in an oven at 80 °C for 1 h. Finally, the dried product was transferred to the furnace to obtain a good crystalline product after the water of crystallization was removed. It was treated there for 6 h at temperature of 400 °C.

First, 4 mmol of nickel nitrate, 20 mmol of red phosphorus, and 2.5 mmol of melamine were individually dissolved using 20 mL of deionized water. Next, the dissolved solutions were transferred to a beaker and mixed homogeneously for about 15 min using a magnetic stirrer. The homogeneous mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and held at 160 °C for 12 h. The obtained product was washed thoroughly with deionized water and ethanol and dried at 80 °C for 12 h in a vacuum. Finally, an annealing treatment was carried out at 800 °C for 3 h under a N2 atmosphere. The same procedure was repeated with 2.5, 5, and 10 mmol of melamine concentration and named Ni12P5@N,P-C(2.5), Ni12P5@N,P-C(5), and Ni12P5@N,P-C(10). Then, the obtained black powders were collected and characterized.

On the basis of the above DFT hypothesis, Ni3S2|P was prepared using a facial two-step method. In brief, nickel chloride and thiourea were dissolved in deionized water with one piece of tailored nickel foam (NF) immerged in the solution. After the hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C, Ni3S2 nanosheets were grown on the NF surface to make up a 3D array configuration. Then, postcalcination with sodium hypophosphite as the anion precursor was conducted to introduce P into the Ni3S2 matrix while keeping its original 3D array structure. The detailed preparation can be found in the Experimental Section, and the fabrication process was illustrated in Figure S2 in the Supporting Information.

A piece of NF (1 cm × 3 cm) was carefully sonicated in concentrated HCl solution for 10 min to remove the oxides and impurity on surface and then washed with water, acetone, and ethanol several times to ensure its surface was clean. After that, it was immersed into a 20 mL solution containing 1 mM nickel chloride and 1.5 mM thiourea. After it was stirred for 10 min, the solution with NF was transferred into a 25 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 20 h. The system was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally after the reaction. The final product was washed with deionized water and ethanol several times and dried under vacuum overnight.

Cobalt acetate (Co(Ac)2·4H2O, 2.0 mmol) was dissolved in 44.0 mL of ethanol under continuous stirring for 20 min. Next, 6.0 mL of 1, 3-propanediol was added into the above solution. After stirring for another 15 min, the as-mixed solution was transferred into a 100.0 mL-Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated up to 160 °C and kept at this temperature for 12 h. Then, the solution was naturally cooled down to room temperature and the resulting Co based precursors were washed with ethanol three times and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. Finally, Co3O4 catalyst was obtained by calcining Co based precursors at 400 °C in air for 3 h.

In a typical synthesis of a nickel–iron alkoxide precursor, 8 mL of glycerol was dissolved in 42 mL of isopropyl alcohol (IPA) to form a transparent colorless solution. Then 0.125 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.25 mmol of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O were added to the above solution under vigorous stirring. After that, the obtained solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 140 °C for 12 h. Finally, the autoclave was cooled naturally, and then the faint yellow precipitate was collected by centrifugation, alternatively washed with absolute ethanol and water, and dried. For the preparation of Ni-FeS2-0.5, the precursor and S powder were placed in a quartz tube furnace, with the precursor in the center of the tube, while the S powder was upstream. With a heating rate of 5 °C min–1, the sample was then annealed at 370 °C and maintained for 4 h under a N2 atmosphere.

The NiCo-CH NW/NF precursor was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. First, a piece of Ni foam (2 × 3 cm2) was soaked in 3.0 M HCl for 4 min to remove the surface oxide layer and then washed with ethanol and deionized water. At the same time, 2 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 5 mmol of NH4F, and 10 mmol of urea were dissolved in 40 mL of distilled water and then sonicated for 15 min to obtain a light-pink transparent solution. Then, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and the pretreated Ni foam was immersed into the above solution. The autoclave was sealed and maintained at 120 °C for 6 h. When the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, NiCo-CH NW/NF was washed with water and ethanol several times and then dried at room temperature.

α-Ni(OH)2 NS/NF was synthesized by a hydrothermal growth method. In detail, 4 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 20 mmol of urea, and 8 mmol of NH4F were dissolved in 40 mL of distilled water to form a homogeneous solution under sonication for 15 min; then, the light green solution was poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing a piece of pretreated Ni foam (2 × 3 cm2); and the sealed autoclave was heated in an electric oven at 120 °C for 6 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the sample was taken out and dried at room temperature.

4-Pyridinecarboxylic acid (0.1 mmol, 12 mg) and nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 0.1 mmol, 24 mg) were immersed in 5 mL of DMF (dry) solvent. Triethylamine (acting as a base that abstracts a proton from the ligand to stimulate ligand–metal bond formation) was added dropwise to control the overall pH of the entire solution at 8. The mixture was transferred to a 30 mL sealed container. The comprehensive solution was heated to 80 °C for 2 days. After slow cooling to room temperature, the solvent was decanted off and green hexagonal-shaped crystals (Scheme S1) were obtained (yield 87%), which were suitable for single crystal diffraction. Elemental analysis of C30H50Cl4N8Ni3O10: calcd C 36.01, H 5.04, N 11.2; found C 36.40, H 5.16, N 10.98.

Nickel foam was soaked in diluted HCl solution (about 3.0 M) for 5 min to remove the surface NiO layer, and then washed with deionized water, absolute ethanol and water, successively. To prepare NiFe LDH/Ni foam, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.5 mmol), Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (0.5 mmol), and CO(NH2)2 (5 mmol) were dissolved in 36 mL distilled water and stirred for at least 10 min to form a clear solution. A piece of Ni foam (2 × 3 cm2, 0.5 mm in thickness) was inserted into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with above solution, sealed and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. The sample was then withdrawn from the solution, thoroughly rinsed with distilled water, and dried at 80 °C for 3 h in a vacuum oven. Note that because the Ni foam might have dissolved during hydrothermal treatment due to the decomposition of urea in aqueous environment, accurate measurement of the catalyst loading on Ni foam was challenging.

CoPi@CoCi was synthesized according to the literature with modification.  In a typical procedure, 0.1254 g of CoSO4·7H2O (0.4 mmol), 0.5 mL of H3PO4 (50 mM), and 0.1442 g of sodium dodecylsulfonate (SDS, 0.5 mmol) were mixed with 50 mL of urea aqueous solution (1M) under stirring. After that, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 80 °C for 180 min. After reaction, the product was collected by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min and washed with water and ethanol several times to remove the excess of the remaining reagents. Finally, the washed particles were dried at 60 °C in the vacuum oven.

Benzimidazole (472.6 mg, 4 mmol) was added into a 100 mL screw cap bottle containing 40 mL of deionized water and subjected to magnetic stirring for 15 min, followed by ultrasonic dispersion for 1 min. Co(NO3)2·6H2O (144.5 mg, 0.5 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and added to the benzimidazole suspension solution, which quickly turned blue-violet. The mixture was sealed and magnetically stirred for another 1 min and then maintained at 80 °C for 36 h. After having been cooled to room temperature, the blue-violet product was washed twice with methanol and collected by centrifuging (8000 rpm, 5 min) and overnight drying at 80 °C. The Co content in PCB was calculated to be 19.5 wt % by ICP-OES.

Benzimidazole (472.6 mg, 4 mmol) was added to a 100 mL screw cap bottle containing 40 mL of deionized water and subjected to magnetic stirring for 15 min, followed by ultrasonic dispersion for 1 min. Co(NO3)2·6H2O (144.5 mg, 0.5 mmol) and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (40.4 mg, 0.1 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and added to the benzimidazole suspension solution, which quickly turned orange. The mixture was sealed and magnetically stirred for another 1 min and then maintained at 80 °C for 36 h. After it had cooled to room temperature, the purple product was washed with methanol several times until the supernatant was clean and collected by centrifuging (8000 rpm, 5 min) and drying at 80 °C overnight. The Co and Fe contents in M-PCB were calculated to be 19.3 and 1.59 wt %, respectively, by ICP-OES.

Benzimidazole (472.6 mg, 4 mmol) was added to a 100 mL screw cap bottle containing 40 mL of deionized water and subjected to magnetic stirring for 15 min, followed by ultrasonic dispersion for 1 min. Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (202 mg, 0.5 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and then added to the benzimidazole suspension solution. The mixture was sealed (transferred into a 125 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave) and magnetically stirred for another 1 min before being maintained at 80 °C (or 110 °C) for 36 h. After having cooled to room temperature, the final product was washed and dispersed into methanol for investigation.

First, the Co foam (2 × 3 cm) was successively treated with acetone and 3.0 mol L–1 of HCl by sonication for 30 min. Second, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.439 g) was dissolved in 30 mL of DI water and stirred vigorously for 30 min. Subsequently, the as-cleaned Co foam and the mixture were added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After being heated for 20 h at 180 °C, the foam was cooled down naturally. The foam was washed three times with DI water and ethanol, individually, and dried at 60 °C for overnight. For comparison, a bare Co foam electrode (denoted as Co), bare Ni foam electrode (denoted as NF), and Co3O4/NF electrode were also fabricated with the same procedure. The benchmark Pt/C electrocatalyst on a cobalt foam was also prepared. Please refer to the Supporting Information for the details of experimental measurements.

The Co dendrites were prepared on Ni foam (NF) via a hydrothermal reduction pathway. CoCl2·6H2O (1 mmol) and sodium citrate (4 mmol) were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL) and stirred for about 5 min. Next, a portion of 0.5 g of NaOH was dissolved in 15 mL of water, and the above two solutions were mixed into a uniform solution. After 6.25 mL of N2H4·H2O (80%) was mixed in the purple solution, a piece of pretreated NF was immersed and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave together. The system was heated at 160 °C for 2 h. Finally, the material was taken out after the autoclave cooled to ambient temperature and washed with water and ethanol.

The Ni(OH)2/CNT/CFP electrode was prepared by a facile solvothermal method based on the as-prepared CNT/CFP electrode. First, 1 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 16 mL of a mixed solvent of DMF/EtOH (volume ratio of 1:3). Then, the mixed solution and CNT/CFP electrode were transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for solvothermal reaction at 120 °C for 12 h. Afterward, it was cooled down to room temperature. The as-obtained Ni(OH)2/CNT/CFP electrode was washed with deionized water and dried in vacuum. For comparison, the Ni(OH)2/CFP electrode was synthesized following the same procedure except that the CNT/CFP substrate was replaced by CFP.

column2: {'TARGET': 'pristine NiO'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'nickel nitrate hexahydrate'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'ammonium fluoride'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'urea'}, {'SOLVENT': 'deionized water'}, {'SUBSTRATE': 'nickel foam'}
column3: The pristine NiO was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method and subsequent annealing at low temperature. In detail, 0.436 g of nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 1.5 mmol), 0.111 g of ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 3.0 mmol), and 0.45 g of urea ((NH2)2CO, 7.5 mmol) were first dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring to obtain a transparent green solution. The solution with a clean 2 × 2 cm nickel foam was transferred into a 100 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave and tightening the cover to ensure sealing. Then, the sealed autoclave was heated to 110 °C and maintained for 5 h. The nickel foam substrate with a light green surface was removed after free cooling down, where the green material on the substrate surface is the precursor of NiO. Washing the substrate covered by precursor several times with water and ethanol and drying it in an oven at 60 °C for 30 min. Second, the as-prepared precursor was put into a muffle furnace and annealed at 400 °C for 1 h in air. When the temperature control procedure finished, we shut down the power of the muffle furnace and waited it to be naturally cooled down. The end product is NiO.

The simple solvothermal method was carried to prepare pure Co3O4; 0.25 g of cobalt acetate tetrahydrate (Co(Ac)2·4H2O, 1 mmol) was added into 60 mL of mixed homogeneous solution of deionized water and alcohol with a volume ratio of 1:1, and stirred under a magnetic stirrer until it is dissolved. At this point, we created a clear solution of rose color. Then, 6 mL of ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 25–28 wt %) was accurately taken through the pipette gun and injected into the above solution in a fume hood. After being mixed evenly, the solution became dark and no longer bright. Then, the solution was poured into a 100 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave and a clean 2 × 2 cm nickel foam was added. Finally, the autoclave was sealed and reacted at 85 °C for 10 h. After cooling, the nickel foam was taken out, rinsed several times with deionized water as well as alcohol, and dried. When Co3O4 was ready, it adhered to the nickel foam substrate.

The NiCo-LDH was fabricated on Ni foam through a facile hydrothermal method. First, a piece of commercial Ni foam (2 × 4 cm2) was sequentially ultrasonicated using 3 M HCl, ethanol, and deionized water for 15, 10, and 5 min, respectively, so as to remove the oxide from Ni foam surface and improve surface wettability. Second, a homogeneous solution of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.1616 g), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.1617 g), NH4F (0.1646 g), and CO(NH2)2 (0.3337 g) in 20 mL deionized water with the cleaned Ni foam were transferred into a polystyrene-lined stainless-steel autoclave (20 mL), and then the autoclave was sealed and maintained at 100 °C for 8 h. When the temperature dropped to room temperature, the as-obtained precursor samples were fetched out for further cleaning in deionized water and dried in the air.

Prior to the synthesis, NF substrate was soaked in 3 M HCl for 30 min to remove the oxide layer of the surface, then rinsed with ethanol, acetone, and deionized water by ultrasonication for 30 min each, and dried at 60 °C overnight. CoMoO4 was prepared by a facile hydrothermal reaction, according to the previously reported method.  Typically, 0.363 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.302 g of Na2MoO4·2H2O were mixed in 40 mL of deionized water by vigorous stirring for 20 min. The NF (1 × 3 cm) was placed standing against the wall of a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The obtained solution was transferred into the autoclave to immerse NF and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. When the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, NF coated with purple product was washed by deionized water and ethanol 3 times and dried in vacuum at 50 °C overnight. Then NF with the as-grown hydrate precursors was annealed in N2 atmosphere at 300 °C for 1 h with a ramp rate of 1 °C·min–1.

A mixture of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (24 mg) and H2BTTA ligand (30 mg) in deionized water (4 mL) was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (23 mL) and then heated at 120 °C for 3 days. After being filtered and washed with 20 mL of pure water three times, blue single crystals of FJI-H14 were obtained.

In the typical preparation, 1 mmol of Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O was dissolved in 10 mL of absolute alcohol to obtain 0.1 M solution, while 1 mmol of oxalic acid was dissolved in 1 mL of absolute alcohol to obtain 1.0 M solution. After the complete dissolution, the oxalic acid solution was added dropwise into the Co(CH3COO)2 solution. After stirring for half an hour, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave followed by heating at 150 °C for 6 h. Then it was cooled to room temperature naturally. The precipitates were collected, washed with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water, and then dried at 60 °C overnight. The collected precursor was subsequently calcined in air with a ramp rate of 5 °C min–1 to 400 °C and maintained for 2 h. For the preparation of V-doped Co3O4 nanoparticles with various dopant concentrations, different molar ratios of Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O to VCl3 (i.e., 2:1, 3:1, 4:1, 9:1, and 19:1) were added into 10 mL of alcohol with a total concentration of 0.1 M followed by the same procedures above.

In a typical process, 10 mg of Pd(acac)2 and 32 mg of PVP were dissolved into the mixture solution of 4 mL of deionized water and 2 mL of DMA under an atmosphere of CO and magnetic stirring. The mixture solution was heated at 100 °C for 2 h to synthesize Pd nanosheets, following the previously reported methods.  Then, the obtained Pd nanosheets were homogeneously dispersed in benzyl alcohol, and ruthenium(III) acetylacetonate and PVP were then added into the predispersed solution and sealed with argon. The mixed solution was stirred for 1 h at room temperature, then was sealed into an autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained products were collected by centrifuging and further purified by a deionized water/acetone mixture several times. Finally, the Pd@Ru NSs samples were re-dispersed into deionized water for another use.

The NiFe LDH/CP was prepared by a hydrothermal process. A mixture of NiCl2·6H2O (0.75 mmol, 0.1783 g), FeCl3·6H2O (0.25 mmol, 0.0675 g), CH4N2O (5 mmol), NH4F (2 mmol), and PVP (0.05 g) was dissolved in 18 mL DIW and magnetically stirred for 30 min to form a clear solution. Then, the obtained solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor with the pretreated CP placed in it. The autoclave was placed in a preheated oven at 120 °C and kept for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the CP coated with the as-synthesized material (NiFe LDH) was taken out and washed with DIW. The nontight attachments on the CP were carefully removed by a clean tweezer. Then, the CP was washed with ethanol and dried at 60 °C in a preheated oven.

In@CuCo2O4/CF nanosheets on the CF were synthesized by a typical hydrothermal method. Typically, the pre-treated CF was immersed in a 50 mL vial containing 45 mL deionized water, 1.75 mol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 1.25 mol Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 0.15 mmol VCl3, 6 mmol NH4F, and 8 mmol urea. The vial was sealed, and a reaction was allowed to occur at 120 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the CF with the precursor was washed with water and ethanol several times, and vacuum dried at 60 °C for 6 h. Successively, the precursors were heated at 350 °C for 2 h under the continuous N2 flow with a flow rate of 20 sccm. After that, it was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the product was denoted as In@CuCo2O4/CF.

The synthesis of NiFe LDH/NF was performed according to the method reported previously with modification.  In brief, a given amount of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 4 mmol NH4F, and 10 mmol urea was added into 40 mL water. After complete dissolution, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and two pieces of treated NF were put into the solution. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 120 °C for 6 h. After natural cooling, the NiFe LDH loaded NF was taken out, washed by water, and dried at 60 °C under vacuum. The feed ratio of Fe and Ni salts as well as the name of NiFe LDH/NF obtained correspondingly is listed in Table 1. If the Fe salt was removed in the synthesis, Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O/NF will be obtained at last.

For the sulfuration of NiFe LDH/NF and Ni(OH)2·0.75H2O/NF, a 0.2 mol L–1 Na2S aqueous solution was first prepared. Subsequently, the 40 mL Na2S solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and the NiFe LDH/NF was put into this solution. Then the sealed autoclave was heated at 100 °C for 8 h. After natural cooling, the sulfide loaded NF was taken out, thoroughly washed by water and dried at 60 °C under vacuum. The loading of sulfide catalyst on NF was in the range of 4–6 mg cm–2 calculated from the mass change of NF before and after the growth of catalyst. The Fe/Ni atomic ratio in the final sulfide was detected by EDS, and the sulfated electrode was denoted according to the Fe content in the catalyst (see Table 1 for details).

α-MnO2 nanowires were synthesized by a typical hydrothermal method according to the reported procedure.  0.02 mol of MnSO4·H2O, 0.02 mol of (NH4)2S2O8 and 0.08 mol of (NH4)2SO4 were added to 70 mL of deionized water with vigorous stirring to form a clear transparent precursor solution. The mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL Telfon-lined stainless autoclave, and then heated at 140 °C for 12 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the obtained precipitate was filtered and washed with a large amount of water until the pH value of wastewater reached around 7. MnO2 nanowire powder was obtained after drying the precipitate in the oven at 120 °C for 16 h. To demonstrate the α-MnO2 owning the best OER activity, nanostructured MnO2 with other crystalline structures including β-, γ- and δ- MnO2 were also synthesized for comparison (preparation details seen in the Supporting Information).

Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (3.0 mmol, 1.2 g) and Na4P2O7·10H2O (1.5 mmol, 0.67 g) were dissolved in 15 and 20 mL of deionized water separately. The Na4P2O7 solution was added dropwise into the Fe(NO3)3·9H2O solution under continuous stirring. The mixed solution was stirred for another 30 min and then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the precipitates were collected by centrifugation and washed with water several times. The light-yellow powder of ferric(III) hydroxyphosphates (Fe4(PO4)3(OH)3, FeHP) was obtained after drying at 60 °C overnight. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern of FeHP is shown in Figure S1.

A hydrothermal method was employed for the synthesis of VS2. In a typical procedure, the mixture of sodium orthovanadate (0.55 g) and thioacetamide (1.12 g) was added to a beaker containing 40 mL of deionized water and stirred for 1 h, yielding a clear transparent solution. The mixture was then poured to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (volume of autoclave: 50 mL) and placed in a hot air oven at 160 °C for 24 h. The resultant black-colored precipitate was collected, washed repeatedly with deionized water and ethanol, dried properly, and stored in a cool and dry place prior to further use.

In a typical preparation, CP (2 cm × 1.5 cm) underwent ultrasound in 10 mL of methanol solution containing 0.1 mL of n-butylamine for about 0.5 h. Next, 25.6 mg of Ni(acac)2 and different amounts of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (i.e., 5.4, 10.8, and 21.6 mg) were added into the above solution and ultrasonicated for 0.5 h at room temperature. The mixture was then transferred into the autocalve and maintained at 170 °C for 10 h. The products were collected and washed with ethanol several times and then dried under ambient conditions for further use. For the synthesis of Ni@CP, the method was similar to that of NixCey@CP without adding the Ce(NO3)3·6H2O into the above solution.

In a typical preparation, 10.8 mg of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O was added into 10 mL of methanol and 0.1 mL of n-butylamine and ultrasonicated for 0.5 h. The mixture was then transferred into the autocalve and maintained at 170 °C for 10 h. The obtained products were named CeO2. For the synthesis of CeO2&Ni@CP, the obtained CeO2 was dispersed and ultrasonicated in isopropyl alcohol for 0.5 h. Then the CeO2 solution was added on the previously synthesized Ni@CP and dried naturally for further use.

First, 0.3030 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.0218 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.1833 g of NH4F, and 0.594 g of urea were dissolved in 30.0 mL of deionized water in turn to obtain a clarified solution. Then, after 30 min of stirring, the solution was transferred to a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then the NF was joined in. Third, the autoclave was put in an oven at 120 °C for 12 h. At the end of the reaction, the acquired materials were washed three times by ethanol and water, respectively. At last, the product was dried for 12 h in a vacuum drying chamber at 60 °C. The product was called Fe–Ni–NF.

First, 0.0218 g of Ni(NO3)3·6H2O, 0.0167 g of NH4F, and 0.054 g of urea were dissolved in 30.0 mL of deionized water in turn to achieve a clarified solution. Second, after 30 min of stirring, the solution is transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Then the Fe–NF precursor was added in. Third, the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave was put in an oven at 120 °C for 12 h. At the end of the reaction, the acquired materials were washed three times by ethanol and water, respectively. Finally, the product was dried for 12 h in a vacuum drying chamber at 60 °C. The sample was named FeNi@FeNi.

STS Ni-BTC was synthesized via a solvothermal process reported previously.  Briefly, 0.43 g of Ni(NO3)·6H2O, 0.205 g of 1,3,5-trimesic acid (BTC), 0.055 g of 2-methyl-imidazole, and 15 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were put into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 2 days. After allowing to room temperature, the crystals were washed with DMF and ethanol subsequently for 3 times, and finally dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h. STS Fe-BTC was synthesized via a solvothermal process reported previously with corresponding alterations:  1.1999 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.4129 g of BTC, 0.2976 mL of HF solution (40 wt %), and 15 mL of H2O were put into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 84 h. After allowing to room temperature, the orange crystals were centrifuged. A treatment in hot deionized water (80 °C) for 3 h and followed by hot ethanol (60 °C) for 1h was applied to decrease the amount of residual BTC. The final products were washed with ethanol and dried vacuum at 80 °C for 24 h. STS FeNi-BTC was synthesized with the identical method of STS Ni-BTC in the presence of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O with the Fe/Ni molar ratio of 1/10.

FeNi-MOF-74 was synthesized according to a previous report.  First, Fe(CH3COO)2 (62.61 mg, 0.36 mmol), Ni(NO3)2.6H2O (104.7 mg, 0.36 mmol), and H4DOT (90 mg, 0.45 mmol) were dissolved into a mixed solvent containing 30 mL of DMF, 1.8 mL of ethanol, and 1.8 mL of H2O. The mixed solution was subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min. Then the obtained solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was thermally treated at 120 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the products were obtained by centrifugation, washing with ethanol 3 times and drying in vacuum oven at 30 °C. By adjustment of the mole ratio of Fe and Ni in the precursor, MOF-74 with different Fe:Ni ratios was synthesized.

As demonstrated in Scheme 1, the fabrication of FeNi3–Fe3O4 NPs/MOF-CNT involves mainly two steps, i.e., construction of a MOF/CNT composite by a hydrothermal method followed by a controlled calcination under a N2 atmosphere. First, the Ni-/Fe-based MOF/CNT composite (NiFe0.5-MOF/CNT) was synthesized by mixing metal salts (Ni2+, Fe3+), 1,4-BDC, and CNT paste in a mixed solution of DMF, ethanol, and water and was stirred at ambient temperature for a uniform distribution followed by hydrothermal treatment at 150 °C for 12 h. Afterward, the as-prepared MOF materials (with/without CNT) were annealed at 300, 400, and 500 °C for 1 h under a N2 atmosphere, during which the pristine MOF materials convert to their respective metal oxide nanoparticle-implanted MOF architectures.

In a typical synthesis of NiFe0.5-MOF, 1,4-BDC (1 mmol, 166 mg), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1 mmol, 290 mg), and FeCl3·6H2O (0.5 mmol, 135 mg) were added to 36 mL of a mixed solution (32 mL of dimethylformamide (DMF) + 2 mL of EtOH + 2 mL of H2O) in a Teflon vessel (100 mL) in sequence. The resulting reaction mixture was allowed to stir for several minutes at ambient temperature to attain a dark-yellow clear solution. Subsequently, the Teflon vessel was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave, placed upright in an oven, and subjected to heat at 150 °C for 6 h, and then it was allowed to cool down naturally. The as-obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation and was washed several times with ethanol and water followed by freeze-drying for 12 h. A similar procedure was adopted for the preparation of NiFe0.75-MOF and NiFe0.25-MOF, except for using different molar ratios of FeCl3·6H2O (0.75 and 0.25 mmol) at the beginning.

In a typical synthesis of NiFe0.5-MOF/CNT, 1,4-BDC (1 mmol, 166 mg), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1 mmol, 290 mg), FeCl3·6H2O (0.5 mmol, 135 mg), and 1 g of carbon nanotube (5 wt % in NMP) conductive paste were added to 36 mL of a mixed solution (32 mL of DMF + 2 mL of EtOH + 2 mL of H2O) in a Teflon vessel (100 mL) in sequence. The resulting reaction mixture was allowed to stir for 30 min at ambient temperature to attain a homogeneous reaction mixture. Subsequently, the Teflon vessel was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave, placed upright in an oven, and subjected to heat at 150 °C for 12 h, and then it was allowed to cool down naturally. The as-obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation and was washed several times with ethanol and water followed by freeze-drying for 24 h.

Iron nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.2424 g, 0.05 mol) and tannic acid (C76H52O46, 0.50 g) were dissolved into a mixed solution (12 mL) containing of N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMF), ethanol, and deionized water in a volume ratio of 14:1:1. The above mixture was stirred for 1 h and then transferred into a Teflon autoclave reactor (25 mL) together with the cleaned NF for a solvothermal treatment at 125 °C. After 12 h, indigo (FeNi)–Tan/NF was obtained, and the bottom precipitates were collected by centrifugation under 16000 rpm for 3 min. Then the (FeNi)–Tan/NF and (FeNi)–Tan precipitates were washed three times with deionized H2O, ethanol, and N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMF) and finally dried at 80 °C, obtaining (FeNi)–Tan coordination crystals. By comparing the weight increase of (FeNi)–Tan/NF samples and bare NF, the loading of the as-prepared (FeNi)–Tan samples on the bare NF was obtained as approximately 0.0378 g cm–2.

Few-layered Mo(1–x)WxS2@Ni3S2 heterostructures were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal method. In a typical process, 45 mg of Na2MoO4·2H2O, 90 mg of thioacetamide, and some amount of Na2WO4·2H2O (30, 60, 120 mg) were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water to obtain a clear solution under vigorous stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Then, the obtained homogeneous solution and a piece of Ni foam cleaned with acetone, ethanol, and water were transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the samples were washed with deionized water three times and dried at 50 °C. The as-prepared samples were denoted as Mo-W-S-1@Ni3S2, Mo-W-S-2@Ni3S2, and Mo-W-S-3@Ni3S2, respectively. For comparison, the MoS2@Ni3S2 or WS2@Ni3S2 sample was synthesized by the same procedure when Na2WO4·2H2O or Na2MoO4·2H2O was absent, respectively. In addition, Ni3S2-modified Ni foam (denoted as Ni3S2@NF) was achieved by direct sulfuration of Ni foam in thioacetamide solution at 200 °C for 24 h.

Co(NO3)2·6H2O (2.377 g) was dissolved in a mixed solution containing distilled water (65 mL), ethanol (65 mL), and DMF (65 mL). Then, 2-5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (H4dobdc, 0.482 g) was added during continuous stirring. The as-obtained solution was transferred into a 500 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and then heated in an oven at 100 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, Co-MOF-74 was obtained and collected by centrifugation. The product was washed by methanol for four times, and thereafter, dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 24 h.

First, NiCl2·6H2O (0.25 mmol), FeCl3·6H2O (0.25 mmol), CrCl3·6H2O (0.25 mmol), and MoCl5 (0.25 mmol) were added to 17.5 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Then 1.2 mmol of urea was dissolved in the above mixture and treated with ultrasonic wave for 30 min. Then 2 cm × 3 cm nickel foam was washed with hydrochloric acid, deionized water, and ethanol in turn. After the Ni2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, and Mo5+ salts were dissolved, the mixed solution was transferred to a 45 mL Teflon vessel, and the pretreated nickel foam was quickly placed into the solution. The Teflon vessel was maintained in sealed conditions at 120 °C for 12 h. Finally, the obtained nickel foam was processed by ultrasound, washed three times with deionized water and ethanol, and dried under a vacuum. The other catalysts, NiFeCr–OH and NiFe–OH, were prepared similarly at the beginning with different metal ratios.

(NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (0.25 mmol), CrCl3·6H2O (0.02 mmol), and NH4F (0.27 mmol) (pH is ∼6.24) were added to 10 mL of Milli-Q water and 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol. It is worth noting that the role of NH4F is to adjust the solution pH to close to 6.24. After ultrasonic treatment for 20 min, the mixture was evenly placed into a 45 mL Teflon vessel, and the pretreated nickel foam (3 cm × 4 cm) was quickly put into the solution. The Teflon vessel was maintained in sealed conditions at 150 °C for 8 h. The product was washed several times with water and then dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain a Cr-doped NiMoO4 nanorod array on Ni foam as a precursor. The precursor was then pyrolyzed in a H2/Ar (v/v, 10/90) reducing atmosphere at 500 °C for 1.5 h to obtain sample r-NiCrMo. Another catalyst, r-NiMo, was prepared following a similar procedure without Cr salts being added at the beginning.

The PNCF is transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with deionized water (25 mL) and maintained at a reactive temperature of 200 °C for 24 h. After the reactor cools down to room temperature, the sample is taken out and cleaned three times using deionized water and ethanol, sequentially. Then, the sample is dried in an oven at 50 °C for 2 h to obtain MOx–M(OH)2/PNCF. Note that the mass of MOx–M(OH)2 contained on the PNCF surface is approximately 2.5 mg cm–2. For comparison, the M(OH)x/NCF is also fabricated with the same process as that of MOx–M(OH)2 but without any plasma pretreatment of NCF.

First, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (30 mL), ethanol (2.5 mL), water (2.5 mL), terephthalic acid (TPA) (0.8 mmol), and a mixture of NiCl2·6H2O and FeCl3·6H2O (fixed at 0.8 mmol), where the feeding proportion of Fe ions is larger than Ni ions, are mixed and transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Subsequently, 10 pieces of NF are placed into the above Teflon-lined autoclave. Then, the autoclave is sealed and maintained at 125 °C for 12 h in an oven. Finally, NiFe-MOF is successfully grown on the surface of Ni foam, washed with ethanol and water three times, respectively, and dried at 50 °C for 24 h. NiFe-MOF@NF-I, NiFe-MOF@NF-II, and NiFe-MOF@NF-III correspond to the final products of Ni/Fe feeding molar ratio 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4. The loading mass of the catalyst is precisely calculated according to the increment of weight obtained by the catalyst before and after the loading. The loading mass of the MOF catalyst is around 0.3 mg cm–2 in most cases.

Thirty-two milliliters of N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), 2 mL of ethanol, and 2 mL of deionized water were added into a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene tube. Then, 0.75 mmol of benzenedicarboxylic acid (BDC) was dispersed into the above solution, followed with the addition of 0.375 mmol of CoCl2·6H2O and 0.375 mmol of FeCl2·4H2O. After stirring for 5 min, 0.8 mL of triethylamine (TEA) was injected into the solution quickly and then stirred for 10 min. After that, the solution was ultrasonically shocked (40 kHz) for 8 h. Then, the solution was transferred into a specialized pressure bottle, followed by a solvothermal treatment at 140 °C for 48 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the resulting powder was obtained by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and deionized water three times, and then freeze-dried for 24 h. The produced sample was marked as (U + S)-CoFe-MOF. By comparison, the U-CoFe-MOF was made based on only using an ultrasonically shocked (40 kHz) reaction for 8 h with the same raw materials ratio, while S-CoFe-MOF was made from a direct solvothermal reaction of the raw materials in a stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave at 140 °C for 48 h.

Ni-MOF-74 was prepared by following the previous reported procedures. A solid mixture of H4DOBDC (0.2216 g, 1.12 mmol) and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1.077 g, 3.70 mmol) was added to a 75 mL solution of DMF, EtOH, and H2O in a 1/1/1 ratio. The above mixture was stirred at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous solution and was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon liner and heated to 120 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the solids were separated from the solvent via centrifugation and washed with fresh DMF and methanol. After that, the sample was treated under vacuum at 180 °C for 5 h to obtain the final Ni-MOF-74. A similar procedure was applied in the preparation of Co-MOF-74 except that Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was replaced with Co(NO3)2·6H2O.

The preparation of Co/Ni-MOF-74 was carried out under reaction conditions similar to those employed for Ni-MOF-74. Typically, a mixture of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Co(NO3)2·6H2O with different molar ratios was used as the metal source to react with H4DOBDC (0.2216 g, 1.12 mmol) in DMF/EtOH/H2O (1/1/1) at 120 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the solids were separated from the solvent via centrifugation, washed with fresh DMF and methanol, and dried under vacuum at 180 °C for 5 h to obtain Co/Ni-MOF-74.

Briefly, CoCl2·6H2O (0.5 mmol) was fully dispersed in D.I. water (20 mL), and then it was transferred into a sealed glass bottle. A piece of clean Ni foam (NF; 2.0 cm× 4.0 cm in size) was immersed into the prepared solution. The sealed glass bottle was heated under 90 °C for 10 h and then cooled to ambient temperature. The samples were taken out and washed with distilled water and ethyl alcohol several times. Finally, the samples were dried at 60 °C to obtain the Ni-doped Co3O4 nanosheets array on Ni foam (Ni–Co3O4 NS\NF). The loading amount of Ni–Co3O4 NS on Ni foam was about 0.62 mg cm–2, which was measured by the inductively coupled plasma (ICP) method. A piece of clean Ti mesh or carbon cloth fiber instead of Ni foam was immersed into the same reaction solution. The surface of Ti mesh and carbon cloth fiber did not appear to have Co3O4 precipitates. Moreover, this method for the formation of Co3O4 nanosheet arrays can be easily extended to other metal materials, which can be achieved by replacing the CoCl2 solution with FeCl3 and MnCl2.

Briefly, 2.0 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 8.0 mmol of NH4F, and 10 mmol of CO(NH2)2 were added into 36 mL of distilled water to obtain uniform solution, which was transferred into Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave liners with a piece of clean Ni foam (2.0 cm× 4.0 cm in size) immersed in the prepared solution. The liner was sealed in the stainless steel autoclave, was heated under 100 °C for 10 h, and then was cooled to ambient temperature. The samples were taken out and washed with distilled water and ethyl alcohol several times. Finally, the samples were annealed at 350 °C in air for 2 h to obtain the Co3O4 nanosheets array on Ni foam (Co3O4 NS\NF).

Briefly, NiCl2·6H2O (0.5 mmol) was fully dispersed in D.I. water (20 mL) and then it was transferred into a sealed glass bottle. A piece of clean Ni foam (2.0 cm× 4.0 cm in size) was immersed into the prepared solution. The sealed glass bottle was heated under 90 °C for 10 h and then cooled to ambient temperature. The samples were washed with distilled water several times and then dried under 60 °C to obtain the Ni(OH)2 nanosheets array on Ni foam (Ni(OH)2 NS\NF).

First, a piece of iron foam (IF) (IF, Kunshan Jiayisheng Electronics Co., Ltd., 20.0 mm × 20.0 mm × 1.2 mm) was successively sonicated in HCl (1 M) for 0.5 h and then washed with deionized (DI) water until pH 7. Second, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.1000 mmol), (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (0.0015 mmol), and SDS (0.4000 mmol) were added into 60 mL of DI water and ultrasonic treated for 30 min. Third, the as-treated IF was put into the resulting solution and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After heat-treating was performed at 150 °C for 6 h and natural cooling down to room temperature, the sample was washed thoroughly by DI water as well as ethanol several times and then dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. Finally, the resulting complex was annealed at 400 °C in a N2 atmosphere for 2 h.

column2: {'TARGET': 'MNFO NS/IF'}, {'SUBSTRATE': 'iron foam'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'Nickel nitrate hexahydrate'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate'}
column3: The synthetic process of MNFO NS/IF (the atomic ratio of Mo:Ni is 1.05:10) involves a hydrothermal process and subsequent annealing treatment, as depicted in Figure 1a. Porous iron foam (IF) was not only used as precursor of Fe, but also as the conductive substrates (Figure S1). Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O) as well as ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O) served as precursors of Ni and Mo, respectively. The phase of spinel oxides was produced during the hydrothermal process (Figure S2). Subsequently, Mo cations were oxidized during the annealing in N2 atmosphere along with the formation of Fe vacancies. Meanwhile, foreign Fe cations from IF were filled into the unoccupied octahedral interstice along the channels consisting of defects.  Besides, IF-supported NiFe2O4 nanocubes (NFO NC/IF) and MoFe2O4 nanosheets (MFO NS/IF) were synthesized as comparisons (see the Experimental Section for more details).

In a typical process, NaH2PO2 (0.06 g, 99%) was placed at the center of the tube furnace and the Co(OH)2, CoxNi1–x(OH)y, and Ni(OH)2 textiles were placed at the downstream side of the furnace. After the reaction at 300 °C for 1 h, the CoP, Co0.8Ni0.2P, Co0.6Ni0.4P, Co0.4Ni0.6P, Co0.2Ni0.8P, and NiP arrays with a wide range of Co/Ni ratios were obtained. Then Co0.8Ni0.2P array was placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave reactor with the tunable graphene dots (GDs) solution (0, 5, 15, 25, and 50 mL). After the hydrothermal reaction at 110 °C, the GDs/Co–Ni-based phosphide array was obtained. In comparison, IrO2 and Pt/C (20 wt % Pt) as well as Nafion (5 wt %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

In a typical synthetic procedure, Co3O4/MoO3/g-C3N4 was prepared via the hydrothermal method. In brief, 320 mg of as-prepared Co3O4/MoO3 was dispersed in 40 mL of distilled water under continuous stirring for 20 min. Next, g-C3N4 solution (20 mg in 15 mL of distilled water, followed by 2 h of sonication to get good dispersion as g-C3N4 is almost insoluble in water) was added into the Co3O4/MoO3 solution, and the whole mixture was continued to stir for another 30 min (Scheme 1). After that, the reaction mixture was transferred in a Teflon line autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 12 h. The sample was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with distilled water and ethanol, respectively, to remove undesired species, and the product was placed in an oven for drying at 90 °C for 12 h. Finally, the obtained black powder was calcinated at 420 °C with a rising heating rate of 10 °C min–1, and the temperature was maintained at 420 °C for 3 h, followed by natural cooling. The light ceramic color Co3O4/MoO3/g-C3N4 was obtained which was used for further studies.

The synthesis followed a simple hydrothermal method using ascorbic acid as a size reduction agent. In brief, 1.5 mg of graphite was dispersed in 15 mL of water via ultrasonication. Following this, ascorbic acid (200 mM) was dissolved and the dispersion was placed into a hydrothermal vessel. The vessel was sealed, and the solution underwent hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 2 h. The vessel was then allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The resulting solution was then collected and centrifuged; color change was observed from a black solution to an orange-yellow solution.

Iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (0.272 g, 1 mmol) was dissolved in 30 mL of ethylene glycol followed by the addition of 0.228 g of solid thiourea (3 mmol) to the solution. The mixture was further stirred for another 10 min and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL capacity) and kept in an oven for 10 h at 200 °C. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the black solid was isolated by centrifugation at 5000 RPM of the reaction mixture. The black precipitate was washed thoroughly with deionized water and ethanol. Black fine powder was obtained after drying the precipitate.

Iron(II) sulfate heptahydrate (1.4 g, 5 mmol) was dissolved in 25 mL of milli Q water under constant stirring and a light green solution was obtained. After complete dissolution of the ferrous salt, 10 mL of 80 wt % N2H4·H2O was added dropwise. PVP (0.22 g) was added to the resultant mixture under constant stirring. The pH of the mixture was 9.2, and the solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 14 h. The Teflon-lined autoclave was cooled and the resultant precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 5000 rpm followed by washing with deionized water and ethanol. The black powder was dried and used as a reference material.

RuNPs-CNx composite was synthesized by sodium borohydride-mediated hydrothermal reduction of ruthenium chloride (RuCl3·xH2O) in the presence of g-C3N4. In brief, 5 mg of solid g-C3N4 was dispersed in 2 mL of DI water in a 30 mL sample glass vial and then 4 mL of 0.12 mmol of aqueous solution of RuCl3·xH2O was poured into an aqueous suspension of the g-CNx. The mixed suspension was sonicated in a bath sonicator operating at 60 Hz for proper mixing. After that the suspension was transferred into a 100 mL sealed tube and heated in an oil bath. When the temperature of the oil bath reached 65 °C, 2 mL of 1.58 mmol of NaBH4 was added to this suspension and reamined heating at 120 °C for 8 h with continuous stirring. After thecompletion of the reaction, the sealed tube was cooled to room temperature. The suspension was then centrifuged at 16 000 rpm for 20 min and dried in a vacuum. A solid black product was obtained after drying and named as RuNPs-CNx composite.

A piece of NF (1 × 1 × 0.15 cm) was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave containing 1.4 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 7 mmol (NH4)2CO, 7 mmol NH4F, and 35 mL of H2O. The autoclave was then heated at 20 °C min–1 up to 150 °C and then maintained at 150 °C for 6 h. The obtained Ni(OH)2/NF product was then heated in a tube furnace at 5 °C min–1 to a specific temperature in the range of 300–600 °C for 1 h, where the sample were kept under a reducing atmosphere composed of 20% NH3 and 80% N2 (100 sccm/min). The samples obtained at temperatures of 300, 400, 500, and 600 °C are denoted herein as Ni/Ni-N0.08, Ni/Ni-N0.28, Ni/Ni-N0.22, and Ni/Ni-N0.12, respectively. The sample denoted herein simply as Ni was obtained by reducing Ni(OH)2 at 400 °C in an atmosphere composed of 10% H2 and 90% N2 (100 sccm/min).

In a typical experiment, 3 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 3 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.33 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 3 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, 18 mmol urea, and 1 mmol trisodium citrate dihydrate were dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water with continuous stirring for 30 min to obtain a shallow pink uniform solution. Later, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Afterward, the autoclave was heated at 120 °C for 12 h and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. The samples at the bottom were collected, washed three times with deionized water, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 8 h for further use. The resulting powder was named Fe–NiCo–LDH–MoO3. For comparison, Fe–NiCo–LDH, NiFe–LDH–MoO3, NiCo–LDH–MoO3, CoFe–LDH–MoO3, Fe–NiCo–LDH–MoO3, Fe–NiCo–LDH–VO3, and Fe–NiCo–LDH–WO3 prepared by a similar process but with the addition of different amounts of metal salt solutions were also investigated (Figure 1).

Preparation of CoMnLax-MOF/CF: In 40 mL of DMF solution with 2 mmol TPA, 10 mL of a mixture of 1 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 1 mmol MnCl2·4H2O, and 0.2 mmol La(NO3)3·6H2O was added under stirring. CF (electrode size: 10 × 15 × 1 mm) cleaned for 10 min in 3 M HCl, deionized water, and ethyl alcohol by ultrasonication in turns was put into the reaction mixture, and then, 50 mL of the mixture was placed in a 100 mL Teflon liner. Then, the Teflon liner was placed in a stainless-steel jacket (Wuhan Youmai Technology) and sealed and reacted at 120 °C for 6 h in an oven. After that, the CF was washed with water and alcohol repeatedly and dried at 60 °C in air for 8 h. The synthesis process is depicted in Scheme 1. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed with alcohol, and dried in the oven for 8 h. CoMn-MOF/CF without La was prepared as the control.

MOF-74-Co/Fe was synthesized according to the previous reported method with slightly modification.  Typically, 0.32 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.16 mmol FeCl2·4H2O, and 0.20 mmol H2DOBDC were first dissolved into a mixture solvent containing 28.0 mL of DMF, 2.0 mL of CH3OH, and 2.0 mL of DI water. After magnetic stirring for 45 min, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 130 °C for 24 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the brown red precipitate in the autoclave was collected by centrifugation. As-obtained MOF-74-Co/Fe was sequentially washed with DMF, ethanol, and distilled water several times. Finally, the MOF-74-Co/Fe was dried at 60 °C under vacuum overnight. Single metal MOFs including MOF-74-Co and MOF-74-Fe were synthesized with the same procedure but only one metal source was added.

Ni3N–NiMoN nanowires grown on CC (Ni3N–NiMoN/CC) electrocatalysts were fabricated by two steps, that is, fabrication of NiMoO4 nanowires located on CC and then heat treatment in NH3. First, to get NiMoO4 nanowires on CC by the hydrothermal method, 2 mmol of Na2MoO4, 2 mmol of Ni(NO3)2, and 0.059 g of NH4F were dissolved into 30 mL of deionized water and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Thereafter, a piece of clean carbon cloth (1 cm × 2 cm), which was pretreated in a mixed solution of HNO3 and H2SO4 with a volume ratio of 1:2 to create some functional groups on CC, was put into the autoclave vertically. The autoclave was heated at 150 °C for 6 h, and after being naturally cooled to room temperature, the sample was taken out, washed with deionized water, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h under vacuum. During the second step, the NiMoO4 precursor was heat-treated at 400 °C in NH3 atmosphere for 2 h. In this work, three samples with different Ni/Mo molar ratios of 1:0.5, 1:0.75, and 1:2 were prepared. To investigate the role of NH4F, the samples with and without adding NH4F were synthesized, which were denoted as Ni3N–NiMoN/CC and NiMoN–WNF/CC, respectively. Additionally, the effects of various nitriding temperatures of 200, 400, 600, and 800 °C were investigated, and these four samples were denoted as Ni3N–NiMoN-200/CC, Ni3N–NiMoN-400/CC, Ni3N–NiMoN-600/CC, and Ni3N–NiMoN-800/CC, respectively.

10 millimoles of CO(NH2)2 (urea), 4 mmol of NH4F, and 1 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water and transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave containing acid-pretreated 3 × 5 cm2 NF. Then, this autoclave was put into an oven to remain at 120 °C for 10 h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was slowly cooled to room temperature. The as-prepared catalyst was dried at 80 °C for 8 h after washing several times with deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the dried catalyst, Ni(OH)2/NF, was obtained.

Different electrocatalysts proposed in this work were prepared by the solvothermal method. In brief, 0.007 M Pluronic F-127 and 0.024 M iron hexacyanoferrate were dissolved in 1:1 ratio of dimethyl formamide (DMF) and acetonitrile under stirring. Then, the mixture was stirred for 1 h. The obtained solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. Afterward, the mixture was centrifuged and washed 3–4 times thoroughly with water and ethanol to remove the impurities. Later, the resultant suspension was dried in an hot air oven at 80 °C for 24 h. Then, the obtained product was collected and calcinated at 350 °C for 3 h in an alumina crucible, and after cooling to room temperature, the reddish brown sample was collected and used for subsequent studies.

Pluronic F-127 (0.007 M), 0.020 M K3[Fe(CN)6], and 0.010 M Co[Fe(CN)6] were dissolved in 1:1 ratio of DMF and acetonitrile under stirring for 1 h. The above solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the whole mixture was centrifuged and washed 3–4 times with water and ethanol and dried in an hot air oven at 80 °C for 24 h. The collected powder was calcinated at 350 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace, and after cooling to room temperature, the products were collected and ground well in an agate mortar. As a result, a cobalt ferrite nanostructure (CoFe2O4) was obtained. Similarly, nickel ferrite (NiFe2O4) and nickel ferrite/cobalt ferrite heterostructures (Ni/Co-HSs) were prepared by following the aforementioned protocol. In the synthesis of nickel ferrite, instead of cobalt hexacyanoferrate, nickel hexacyanoferrate should be added, whereas in the case of nickel ferrite/cobalt ferrite heterostructure (Ni/Co-HS) synthesis, both nickel hexacyanoferrate (0.024 M) and cobalt hexacyanoferrate (0.024 M) were added consecutively. The schematic representation of metal hexacyanoferrate-complex-derived heterostructure (Ni/Co-HS) synthesis is shown in Figure 1. Finally, 10% MWCNTs were blended at the time of introduction of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 heterostructures into the autoclave, which results in the functionalization of MWCNTs on the heterostructures (C-Ni/Co-HS) as composites.
