Cobalt precursor was synthesized by reacting cobalt powder with IPA and glycerol. Uniform Co precursor spheres were synthesized through a facile modified hydrothermal reaction. Under vigorous magnetic stirring, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.2 M) and glycerol (4 mL) were first dissolved into IPA (20 mL). The transparent solution was subsequently transferred to an autoclave (45 mL) and set aside in an oven. The reaction was conducted at 180 °C for 6 h. The product was then washed with ethanol five times and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

First, a homogeneous dark blue solution was prepared through dissolving 2 mg of FeCl3·6H2O and 2 mg of H4DOBDC in a mixed solvent consisting of 7 mL of DMF, 1.5 mL of absolute ethanol, and 1.5 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring at room temperature. Subsequently, the above solution was poured into a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a piece of as-prepared Co/CC (1 × 1.5 cm2) and maintained at 120 °C for 24 h. After the autoclave had been cooled to room temperature naturally, the obtained Co,Fe-MOF-74/Co/CC was rinsed with DMF and ethanol several times. The final products were then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. To obtain the optimum electrochemical performances of the Co,Fe-MOF-74/Co/CC, simultaneously, the dosage of FeCl3·6H2O (1, 2, and 3 mg) was optimized, and the corresponding products were marked as Co,Fe-MOF-74/Co/CC-n (n = 1, 2, 3). For the controls, the above process was repeated to synthesize the Co-MOF-74/Co/CC with 50 μL of HCl (2 M) instead of FeCl3·6H2O.

Prior to the synthesis, the carbon cloth with size of 2.5 cm × 3.5 cm were immersed into 50 mL concentrated HNO3 for 30 min, and then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with heat treatment at 120 °C for 3 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the treated carbon cloth was washed thoroughly with deionized water and absolute ethanol. For the synthesis of Co3O4 nanowires/carbon cloth, 4 mmol of Co(NO3)3·6H2O, 16 mmol of NH4F, and 20 mmol urea were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water and stirred for 10 min. The solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and a piece of pretreated carbon cloth was immersed into the solution. Then, the autoclave was maintained at 120 °C for 9 h. The obtained product was rinsed with distilled water three times, dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and annealed at 350 °C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 2 °C min–1 in air.

Typically, 1.75 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O and 1.75 mmol of Na2SO4 were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water and stirred for 10 min. The solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and a piece of Co3O4 nanowires/CC was immersed into the solution. Then, the autoclave was maintained at 120 °C for 2 h. The obtained product was rinsed with distilled water three times, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, the obtained Co3O4–FeOOH product was placed at two separate positions in a porcelain boat with NaH2PO2 at upstream side of the quartz tube, and annealed at 350 °C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 2 °C/min under slowly flowing argon atmosphere. For comparison, the CoP/CC and FeP/CC were synthesized via a similar method, using Co3O4/CC and FeOOH/CC instead of Co3O4–FeOOH/CC, respectively.

FeCl3 (0.4 mM) and NH4F (2.4 mM) are dissolved in 25 mL of H2O and hydrothermally treated at 160 °C in a 50 ml Teflon autoclave with activated CP as the substrate, with different reaction times.  The synthesized product grown on the CP is cleaned with deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol and dried overnight at 80 °C in a vacuum oven. The CP is sealed with polyamide tape during the hydrothermal synthesis except for 1 × 1 cm2 area.

The composite FeO(OH)–CoCeV-LTH/CP is prepared by taking different molar concentrations of CoCl2·6H2O, Ce(NO3)·6H2O, NH4VO3, and urea (CH4N2O), which are dissolved in 25 mL of H2O and stirred for 30 min to afford a homogeneous solution. The hydrothermally grown FeO(OH)/CP film is used as the substrate and hydrothermally treated at 120 °C for 12 h. For bare CoCeV-LTH, the same procedure is followed except for the use of bare CP as the substrate.

A series of CoMoNiS-NF-xy were prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method, by adjusting the feeding ratio of Co:Mo ranging from 5:1 to 1:3. In a typical synthesis, a piece of commercial NF (6 × 10 cm) was treated with HCl solution (1 M) for 20 min to get rid of the oxidation layer on the surface, followed by washing with water and ethanol. After that, the NF was weighed after being dried in a vacuum oven. Taking the CoMoNiS-NF-11 (Co:Mo molar ratio of 1:1) as an example, 0.562 g (2 mmol) of CoSO4·7H2O, 0.482 g (2 mmol) of Na2MoO4·2H2O, and 2 g (26.27 mmol) of CH4N2S were dissolved in 33 mL of deionized water and 15 mL of hydrazine hydrate under continuous magnetic stirring. The pretreated NF was immersed in the above-mentioned solution and reacted in an 80 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 24 h. After the mixture cooled down naturally to 25 °C, black product (named CoMoNiS-NF-11) was obtained, rinsed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried under vacuum at 50 °C for 12 h.

Typically, 400 mg of V2O5, 300 mg of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 20 mg of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 15 mg of Ni(NO3)3·6H2O, 280 mg of hexamethylenetetramine, and 500 mg of sodium sulfate were dissolved in 35 mL of distilled water under vigorous stirring for 0.5 h. The obtained solution in a sealed autoclave (50 mL) was then treated at 120 °C for 24 h in a traditional oven. When the autoclave was cooled to 25 °C, the white powder on the bottom was gathered and rinsed thoroughly with deionized water and ethanol. The sample was then dried in a vacuum oven for 12 h at 62 °C.

In a typical process, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1.5 mmol), Fe(NO3)3·6H2O (0.5 mmol), Al(NO3)3·9H2O (0.5 mmol), and urea (5 mmol) with a Ni/Fe/Al molar ratio of 3:1:1 were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water and then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with 1 × 2 cm Ni foam leaching against the wall. The autoclave was aged at 150 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained NiFeAl-CO32–-LDH on Ni foam was washed with deionized water several times, then dried at 70 °C for 12 h, and subsequently suspended in 100 mL of deionized water with vigorous agitation. Then the NaH2PO4 solution (1 M) was dropwise added until pH = 4.5 under a N2 atmosphere to obtain NiFeAl-PO43–-LDH grown on Ni foam, followed by drying at 70 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven.

Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (2.5 mmol) and urea (5 mmol) were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water and then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with 1 × 2 cm Ni foam leaching against the wall. The autoclave was aged at 150 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained Ni(OH)2 on Ni foam was washed with deionized water several times and then dried at 70 °C for 12 h. Finally, Ni(OH)2 and 1 g of NaH2PO2 were placed in two separate porcelain boats with NaH2PO2 at the upstream side, followed by heating at 300 °C for 1 h at a heating rate of 2 °C min–1 under an Ar-flow atmosphere. The final obtained Ni2P was washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol several times. The amount of Ni2P loading on Ni foam determined by the weight difference of Ni foam before and after material growth is approximately 1.0 mg cm–2.

The Ni foam was successively immersed into acetone, ethanol, and deionized water with ultrasonic treatment, and then dried at 80 °C overnight. In a typical run, 30 mL deionized water contains certain molar Na2S was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and a piece of cleaned Ni foam (1 × 3 cm) was placed into. Next, the autoclave was sealed and kept at 170 °C for 10 h. Finally, until cooling down to room temperature, the Ni foam was taken out and washed with deionized water repeatedly, then dried at 60 °C for 12 h; the resutl was denoted as NiS/NF.

column2: {'TARGET': 'NiCo2S4'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'nickel chloride hexahydrate'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'cobalt chloride hexahydrate'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'urea'}, {'SOLVENT': 'deionized (DI) water'}, {'SUBSTRATE': 'Ni foam'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'sodium sulfide nonahydrate'}
column3: The NiCo2S4 nanotube arrays on Ni foam were synthesized by a three-step hydrothermal reaction. First, to fabricate the precursor, 4 mmol nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), 8 mmol cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), and 12 mmol urea were mixed together in 60 mL of deionized (DI) water and then stirred for 15 min to obtained a clear solution. The above solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-cleaned 100 mL autoclave. Before the Ni foam was used as a substrate to in situ grow the arrays, it was washed with hydrochloric acid, acetone, ethanol, and DI water to remove the NiO layer from the surface. Next, the surface-cleaned Ni foam (2 cm × 4 cm) was immersed into the solution. The hydrothermal reaction was conducted at 120 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the Ni foam was taken out and rinsed with DI water to remove any unreacted residues. Second, 0.3 g of sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O) was added into 60 mL of deionized water with stirring for 10 min to obtain a homogeneous solution, and the above Ni foam was immersed into the solution. Next, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-cleaned 100 mL autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the Ni foam was washed several times with DI water and ethanol, respectively, followed by drying at 60 °C.

column2: {'PRECURSOR': 'Ferric nitrate nonahydrate'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'nickel nitrate hexahydrate'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'urea'}, {'SOLVENT': 'DI water'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'NiCo2S4'}, {'TARGET': 'NiCo2S4@NiFe LDH'}, {'SUBSTRATE': 'Ni foam'}
column3: Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (1 mmol, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), 3 mmol nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O) and 5 mmol urea was dissolved into 60 mL of DI water with stirring for 15 min; next, the as-obtained NiCo2S4 nanotube arrays on Ni foam were immersed into the solution and the solution transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The reaction was completed at 120 °C for 10 h. Finally, NiCo2S4@NiFe LDH arrays on Ni foam were cleaned by DI water and ethanol, respectively, and vacuum-dried overnight. To contrast with NiFe LDH arrays on Ni foam, a surface-cleaned Ni foam was immersed into the above solution, and the product was then washed with DI water and ethanol and then dried overnight.

Typically, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1.45 g) and hexamethylenetetramine (C6H12N4; 1.40 g) were dispersed into ultrapure water (35 mL) and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution and pretreated NF (2 × 3 cm) were placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed, and maintained at 100 °C for 10 h. The Ni(OH)2 nanosheets on NF were obtained. Finally, Ni(OH)2 was annealed in a tube furnace at 380 °C for 2 h under a continuous NH3 flow to get Ni3N.

The monodispersed silica spheres were first synthesized according to a reported method with some modification.  Synthesized SiO2 spheres (0.1 g) were dispersed in 100 mL of HMT solution (9 mM) and heated to 120 °C. Then Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1.5 mM) and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (0.5 mM) were fully mixed and then added dropwise (over 2 h) into the above suspension under stirring, followed by aging in an autoclave at 120 °C for 24 h. After thorough washing and drying at room temperature, the NiFe-LDH HMS product was finally synthesized. For the preparation of LDHs HMSs containing different Ni/Fe ratios, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was changed to 1.67, 1.60, 1.50, 1.33, and 1.00 mM, and the amount of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was 0.33, 0.40, 0.50, 0.67, and 1.00 mM accordingly, giving rise to a molar ratio of Ni2+/Fe3+ to be 5:1, 4:1, 3:1, 2:1, and 1:1, respectively.

NiCo2O4/NF was prepared by a simple hydrothermal calcination method. Before using, the NF (3 × 5 cm2) was ultrasonically washed with 3 M HCl solution, ethanol, and water sequentially for 15 min to remove the oxide layer on the surface. In a typical synthesis, 1.2 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 2.2 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and 14 mmol of urea were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water to form the precursor solution. The solution was then transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave, and one piece of treated NF was immersed in it. The autoclave was sealed and kept at 120 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the obtained sample was washed with deionized water several times followed by freeze-drying for 12 h. Finally, the as-prepared NiCo precursor was calcined at 350 °C in an air atmosphere for 2 h to obtain NiCo2O4/NF.

A simple microwave-assisted solvothermal technique was employed to synthesize different batches of Co-doped CuO. A fixed quantity of Cu(OAc)2 and varying quantities of Co(OAc)2 were used as host and dopant precursors, respectively. Typically, 0.2 g of Cu(OAc)2 salt (1 mmol) was dissolved in 40 mL of mixed solvent of ethylene glycol (EG) and water with a volume proportion of 3:1 and stirred for 15 min at 400 rpm, which resulted in a deep green solution. Then, a required amount of Co(OAc)2 salt (0.01–0.07 mmol) was added to the preceding solution for different proportions of cobalt doping followed by addition of 0.2 g of PVP as a capping agent and 0.6 g of urea as a reducing agent. The solution was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined vessel and sealed before the microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis was carried out with a microwave synthesizer (MultiwavePRO, Anton Paar). The reaction was performed at 200 W (ramping rate 20 W per min) for 15 min with a maximum chamber pressure limit of 60 bar. The temperature during the microwave synthesis was measured to be ∼140 °C. After completion of the reaction, the microwave synthesizer was cooled naturally to room temperature. The resulting reaction mixture was centrifuged to collect the precipitated product. Further washing was done with Millipore water and ethanol to remove the surface impurities and/or additional precipitated salts. Finally, the product was dried in an air-oven for 6 h at 60 °C.

In a typical synthesis, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and Co(NO3)2·6H2O at an appropriate molar ratio, x (x = 0, 1:4, 1:3, and 1:2), were mixed in 50 mL of distilled water at room temperature. Then, 10 mmol of NH4F and 12.5 mmol of CO(NH2)2 were added into the mixture under vigorous stirring. The as-formed homogeneous solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Titanium substrates were carefully washed with a 1 M HCl solution and then rinsed with absolute ethanol and distilled water. After immersing a washed titanium substrate in the homogeneous solution, the autoclave was sealed, maintained at 120 °C for 10 h, and allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The substrate was washed several times with distilled water, dried at 80 °C for 12 h, and calcined in air at 250 °C for 3 h.

The layered double hydroxides were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (molar ratio Ni/Fe was 95:5 and 91:9) and 2.4 g of urea were dissolved in 15 mL of water under sonication and transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Next, 15 mL of Ag NW suspension was added into the autoclave and heated to 95 °C under continuous magnetic stirring for 10 h. Afterward, Ag NWs@NiFe LDHs were dispersed in ethanol to the concentration of 1.8 mg/mL. The as-synthesized catalysts with the Ni/Fe molar ratio of 95:5 and 91:9 were denoted as Ag NWs@Ni0.95Fe0.05 LDHs and Ag NWs@Ni0.91Fe0.09 LDHs, respectively. The synthetic procedure for Ag NWs@Ni(OH)2 was similar except that only Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was used.

FeCoNi MOFs/C (MOF, metal–organic framework) was synthesized with the one-step hydrothermal method. First, 0.082 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.053 g of (Fe(CO2CH3)2·4H2O), 0.081 g of (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), and 0.069 g of 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (H4DOT) were dissolved in 22.5 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 1.35 mL of anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), and 1.35 mL of deionized (DI) water with vigorous sonication forming a homogeneous solution for at least 30 min. Following this, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave immediately. Then, it was hydrothermally heated to 120 °C for 24 h. Last, the collected FeCoNi MOFs/C was washed with DMF, DI water, and EtOH and dried in an electric oven for 12 h at 70 °C.

FeCoNiMo MOFs/C was synthesized with the one-step hydrothermal method. First, 0.082 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.053 g of (Fe(CO2CH3)2·4H2O), 0.081 g of (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), 0.067 g of (H24Mo7N6O24·4H2O), and 0.069 g of H4DOT were dissolved in 1.35 mL of EtOH, 22.5 mL of DMF, and 1.35 mL of DI water with vigorous sonication for at least 30 min forming a homogeneous solution. Following this, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave immediately. Then, it was hydrothermally heated to 120 °C for 24 h. Last, the collected FeCoNi MOFs/C was washed with DMF, DI water, and EtOH and dried in an electric oven for 12 h at 70 °C.

column2: {'TARGET': 'HE-MOF'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'nickel acetylacetone'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'obalt acetylacetone'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'iron acetylacetone'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'zinc acetylacetone'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'molybdenum acetylacetone'}, {'SOLVENT': 'DI-water'}, {'SOLVENT': 'EtOH'}, {'SUBSTRATE': 'NF'}, {'ADDITIVE': '2,6-naphthalenedicarboxylate tetrahydrate'}
column3: Herein, we have successfully proposed a strategy for in situ growth of two-dimensional (2D) HE-MOF arrays on NF substrates via a one-step solvothermal method (Figure 1a). Generally, 0.05 mmol of nickel acetylacetone, 0.05 mmol of cobalt acetylacetone, 0.05 mmol of iron acetylacetone, 0.05 mmol of zinc acetylacetone, and 0.05 mmol of molybdenum acetylacetone are first dissolved in a mixture of DI-water and EtOH with NF. Later, 0.25 mmol of organic ligand (2,6-naphthalenedicarboxylate tetrahydrate) and 10 mL of the organic ligand solution were added into the above solutions with stirring for 30 min and sonicated for 5 min. After that, the mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 20 h. After cooling to room temperature, the HE-MOF covering on NF was obtained after washing with DI-water 3 times. The synthetic illustration of the 2D HE-MOF array is displayed in the text. In detail, the crystal structure of HE-MOF is composed of alternating organic hydrocarbon (2,6-naphthalenedicarboxylic group) and inorganic metal–oxygen layers (MO6 units; M = Ni, Co, Fe, Zn, and Mo), and each metal ion (Ni, Co, Fe, Zn, or Mo) presents an octahedral coordination. In addition, each metal ion may be coordinated to two trans monodentate carboxylates and four equatorial water molecules, and the naphthalene dicarboxylate bridges two metal–oxygen layers.

The synthesis procedure was adopted from a previous report with some modification.  First, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (2.42 g, 0.006 mol), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (2.33 g, 0.008 mol), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (2.33 g, 0.008 mol), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (1.93 g, 0.008 mol), and Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (2.40 g, 0.006 mol) were ultrasonically dissolved in a mixed solvent (35 mL of DMF + 2 mL of ethanol+ 2 mL of water) at an ambient condition. The above solution was mixed with another solution containing 1, 4-BDC (0.0275 mol, 4.6 g). The homogeneous solution was then autoclaved at 150 °C for 48 h in an electronic oven. The final product was obtained by centrifugation and washing several times using water and ethanol, followed by vacuum drying at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, HEO was achieved after 800 °C temperature treatment of the obtained HE-MOF for 3 h in air atmosphere and denoted as HEO-800. The same sample was treated at different annealing temperatures (600, 700, and 900 °C) for comparison, and they are denoted as HEO-600, HEO-700, and HEO-900, respectively. Furthermore, single-metal oxides and HEO with fewer metal elements and six metal elements were prepared with the same synthesis protocol.

All chemicals were purchased in the analytical grade and were used without any further purification. In a typical preparation,  1.26 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 14 mL of methanol. Once Co(NO3)2·6H2O got dissolved completely, 0.2 mL of benzyl alcohol was added into the mixture. After stirring for another 1 h, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (25 mL in volume), which was subsequently heated to 180 °C and maintained at that temperature for 24 h. Then, it was cooled to room temperature naturally. The precipitates were collected, washed with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water three times, and dried at 60 °C overnight (8 h). A lilac powder was obtained and subsequently calcined in the air with a ramp rate of 5 °C min–1 to 400 °C with a duration of 4 h. These intermediate products were labeled as Co3O4. After the thermal treatment, 30 mg of the samples (i.e., Co3O4) was added into 5 mL of 0.5 M NaBH4 solution and immersed for 1 h. Finally, the products were filtered, washed, and dried as described above, which were labeled as Co3O4-B.

In a typical process, Pluronic P123 (2.0 g) was dissolved into the aqueous HCl solution (1.60 M, 75.0 g). Tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, 4.25 g) was added and stirred at 40 °C for 24 h, followed by the hydrothermal treatment at 100 °C for 24 h. After filtration and then drying under ambient conditions, the template-occluded SBA-15 (denoted as AS) was recovered. After calcination in flowing air at 550 °C for 5 h, the template P123 was removed to get the template-free SBA-15 (denoted as CS).

Co–Fe–O nanostructures were synthesized using a hydrothermal synthesis route, as optimized in our previous report.  Co nitrate and Fe nitrate were used as metal ion precursors, while urea and NaF were used as precipitating and morphology controlling agents, respectively. Briefly, metal ion salts (1 mmol), NaF (3 mmol), and urea (5 mmol) were dissolved in deionized (DI) water until a clear homogeneous solution was obtained. This solution was transferred and sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 2 h in an oven. After the reaction was completed, the precipitated product was removed, filtered, washed with DI water and IPA, and followed by drying in air at 80 °C. The concentrations of Co and Fe were varied in the samples by adjusting the molar ratios of the respective salts. Control samples of Co–O and Fe–O were also synthesized using a similar route, by using only Co and Fe salts, respectively. The powders obtained after drying are amorphous intermediary phases, as verified in our previous report.  These powders were heated in a furnace at 350 °C for 3 h, to convert them into crystalline products.

Similar steps with tiny variances were conducted to prepare Co-Cu-P-NW. In particular, the ingredients of reaction solution varied by increasing the amount of urea from 0.541 to 0.721 g and adding 1.093 g of cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB). Besides, the reaction time in the autoclave increased from 6 to 12 h. A similar phosphatization process was implemented to convert Co-Cu dihydroxide into Co-Cu-P-NW.

Typically, for the preparation of Co-Cu-P-NS, 0.242 g of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, certain amount of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and 0.541 g of urea were successively added into 70 mL of deionized water. The mixture was strongly stirred to produce homogeneous reaction solution. A piece of the as-washed nickel foam (1 × 3 cm) was settled into the reaction solution and then chambered in a Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave with volume of 100 mL. The reaction proceeded at 120 °C for 6 h in the autoclave, and then, the solution was cooled down to ambient temperature. The nickel foam substrate wrapped with Co-Cu dihydroxide nanosheet arrays was taken out and rinsed several times with deionized water. To convert the Co-Cu dihydroxide nanosheet arrays into Co-Cu-P-NS, the nickel foam with Co-Cu dihydroxide nanosheet arrays was further phosphatized as follows: the nickel foam and 0.6 g of NaH2PO2 powder were placed in two independent quartz boats, respectively. The two quartz boats were chambered in a tube furnace with a distance of 6 cm. The quartz boat with NaH2PO2 powder was placed near the air inlet, and the distance from it to the air inlet was 30 cm. The furnace was heated to 350 °C with a heating rate of 2 °C/min and kept for 1 h at an Ar atmosphere. The flow rate of an Ar atmosphere was 30 sccm.

The Ni–Co oxide hexagonal nanoplates were assembled onto the Ti3C2Tx sheets by a facile hydrothermal treatment well-distributed growth. With the addition of NaOH aqueous solution to aqueous solution containing CoCl·6H2O and NiCl2·6H2O with a certain Co/Ni molar ratio under an Ar flow, a pink suspension of β-NixCo2–x(OH)2 was generated. The β-NixCo2–x(OH)2, with the stacked layers, has positive charges.  Therefore, they tend to cover the surfaces or come into the interlayer space of Ti3C2Tx via an electrostatic force. After experiencing dissolution, recrystallization, and directed growth, the resulting β-NixCo2–x(OH)2 nucleus in the hydrothermal reaction was transformed into the form of hexagonal plates of β-NixCo2–x(OH)2. It is worth noting that Ti3C2Tx, particularly its surface in pure water, will be oxidized and transformed into TiO2 in the synthesis process. Nevertheless, under the protection of a concentrated NaOH solution as well as an Ar flow, this oxidation phenomenon did not happen.

A certain amount of CoCl2·6H2O and NiCl2·6H2O (4.834 + 0.125 g; 4.698 + 0.250 g; 4.548 + 0.375 g; and 4.398 + 0.499 g) were added into 70 mL of the Ti3C2Tx supernatant (5 mg/mL, the molar ratio of Ti3C2Tx to CoCl2·6H2O of 1:9), with the Ni/Co molar ratios being 1.5/98.5, 3/97, 4.5/95.5, and 6/94, respectively, under the protection of Ar flow. After the salt was completely dissolved, 28 mL of NaOH aqueous solution (2.5 M) was added dropwise into the mixed solution to yield a dark purple solution. It was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor (50 mL) and hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 6 h. The solids were collected, washed with deionized water as well as ethanol, vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and calcined at 350 °C for 3 h. The obtained samples were denoted Ni0.045Co2.955O4/Ti3C2Tx-HT, Ni0.09Co2.91O4/Ti3C2Tx-HT, Ni0.135Co2.856O4/Ti3C2Tx-HT, and Ni0.18Co2.82O4/Ti3C2Tx-HT, respectively. As a comparison, two composite materials Ni0.09Co2.91O4/Ti3C2Tx-S1 and Ni0.09Co2.91O4/Ti3C2Tx-S2, which have the molar ratios of Ti3C2Tx to CoCl2·6H2O being 1:6 and 1:15, respectively, were prepared.

The Rh2P NCs were synthesized using a solvo-thermal method. In a typical procedure, 0.01 mmol of Rh(acac)3 and 30 μL (0.067 mmol) of TOP were added to 5 mL of OAm. The mixture was stirred vigorously at room temperature for 10 min and then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h, and then cooled down to room temperature. The resulting black product was precipitated with ethanol (20 mL), separated by centrifugation with rate 10000 rpm for 10 min, and washed three times by precipitation/dissolution with ethanol/cyclohexane.

Fe–Co MOC was synthesized via a hydrothermal reaction. Specifically, 0.05 g of FeCl3, 0.05 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and 0.1 g of trimesic acid were dissolved in a mixture of 10 mL of DI water, 10 mL of ethanol, and 10 mL of DMF under vigorous stirring. The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 150 °C for 12 h. The final products were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min and washed three times with ethanol and water. The precipitate was collected and dried at 80 °C. Fe/Co MOC was prepared by dissolving 0.1 g of FeCl3 or 0.1 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O with 0.1 g of trimesic acid in a mixture of 10 mL of DI water, 10 mL of ethanol, and 10 mL of DMF following the same hydrothermal reaction condition as described for Fe–Co MOC.

A schematic illustration for the preparation of the Fe–Co–P catalyst is displayed in Figure 1. The Fe–Co metal–organic complex (MOC) was prepared by a hydrothermal reaction. To prepare the initial Fe–Co MOC, iron chloride (FeCl3) and cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O) with a stoichiometric ratio of 3:2 were dissolved in a solvent mixture of N,N-dimethylformamide, ethanol, and water with trimesic acid (BTC) as the ligand. The mixture was heated at 150 °C for 12 h, allowing the formation of an Fe–Co MOC structure through a self-assembly process (Figure 1a). The Fe–Co MOC was then annealed at 500 °C in Ar with a ramp rate of 2 °C/min. The organic ligands were carbonized, and metal (Fe/Co) oxides were formed. Sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2) was used as the phosphorus (P) source due to its low toxicity. Upon heating, NaH2PO2 thermally decomposed and released PH3, which converted the metal oxide/C structure into an Fe–Co–P alloy.  No surfactant or template was applied in the entire process, which avoided labor-intensive post-treatment and enabled a cost-effective scale-up.

A piece of 2 cm × 4 cm × 0.15 cm Ni foam (NF) was first dipped in diluted HCl to remove the oxide films on the surface, followed by sonication in acetone for 15 min, and finally cleaned by ethanol and deionized water several times before use. In a typical process, 0.5 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.5 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 10 mmol urea, and 4 mmol NH4F were dissolved in 30 mL of water at room temperature to form a yellow solution. The solution was subsequently transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, in which the as-prepared NF was immersed into the solution, heating at 120 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the NF was washed by ethanol and deionized water several times and then dried at 60 °C overnight in a vacuum drying oven.

To prepare FeCo-LDH@CoSx microtubes, the MoO3@FeCo-LDH@ZIF-67 microrods obtained in the last step were dispersed in 20 mL of ethanol under ultrasonic assistance. After continuous ultrasound for 15 min, 100 mg (∼1.33 mmol) of thioacetamide (TAA) was introduced. After the system was stirred for 30 min, the suspension was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 90 °C for 2 h. Finally, the black-gray precipitate was collected and washed with a 1 M KOH solution, deionized water, and absolute ethanol several times to remove the MoO3 template and the soluble impurities and then dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. Meanwhile, as a control, MoO3@FeCo-LDH microrods were also treated by the same method mentioned above to produce hollow FeCo-LDH microtubes. The corresponding reaction equation is as follows: MoO3 + 2OH– → MoO42– + H2O. The produced MoO42– is soluble in water.

Next, 20 mg of ZIF-67 and 100 mg (∼1.33 mmol) of TAA were introduced into 20 mL of ethanol; the mixed system was first ultrasonically dispersed for 15 min and then under vigorous stirring for 30 min. After that, the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 90 °C for 2 h. The reaction system was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The final products were washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol several times and then dried at 60 °C in vacuum for 12 h.

MoO3 microrods were obtained according to a previous report with minor modifications.  Typically, 700 mg (∼0.57 mmol) of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O and 2 mL of 98% HNO3 were added to 20 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring to form a uniform solution. After continuously stirring for 30 min at room temperature, the above solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 16 h. Subsequently, the system was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The white precipitates were washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol several times to remove the impurities and dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

First, NCF was ultrasonically degreased with acetone for 15 min, followed with immersion in 1 M HCl solution for 10 min. The cleaned NCF was then thoroughly rinsed three times with deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol and dried at 50 °C in an oven for further applications. The NiV-LDH nanospheres with different V/Ni ratios were synthesized on the NCF surface by a hydrothermal method, and the detailed preparation process is as follows: First, 1.0 mmol of VCl3 and NiCl2·6H2O with different molar ratios (Ni/V: 10/0, 9/1, 8/2, 7/3) and 0.4 g of CTAB were dissolved in a solution mixed with distilled water (4.8 mL) and methanol (24 mL). After magnetically stirring the solution, a transparent solution formed. A piece of cleaned NCF (1 cm × 1 cm) was immersed into the above solution and reacted in a 40 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 28 h. After that, samples were rinsed repeatedly with ethanol and distilled water several times, then dried at room temperature.

First, 4 mg of (NH4)2MoS4 was added to 4 mL of DMF and stirred for 15 min under ambient conditions. Then, 2 μL of N2H4·H2O was added to the above suspension. After further stirring for 15 min to completely dissolve solids, the mixed solution was poured into a 40 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and the above-synthesized NiV-LDH/NCF was placed into the above solution. Then, the sealed autoclave was held in a convection oven at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting Ni8V2(Mo3S4)11/NCF as indicated in Figure S1 was collected and rinsed with ethanol four times and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 6 h. As the Ni–V–Mo chalcogenide (Ni/V: 4/1) exhibited the highest electrocatalytic activity for HER and OER, it acted as the sample for characterization, unless otherwise specified.

Typically, Co3O4@CQDs-CN HPAs/CoF was synthesized via a facile yet simple two-step process. Typically, CoF was first treated with diluted hydrochloric acid (1.0 M HCl) and then washed with ethanol and water several times. DABCO (1.0 mmoL) and H2BDC (1.0 mmoL) were added to a mixed solvent of ethanol (1 mL) and DMF (4 mL). Then, a LiOH aqueous solution (1 mL, 3.7%) was slowly dropped into the mixture to form a transparent ligand solution. The CQDs (56 mg) were also dispersed in the ligand solution, where the CQDs concentration was 11.2 mg mL–1. CQDs@CoII-MOF were directly grown on conductive CoF in the ligand solution. A standard three-electrode system was used with a CoF (1 cm × 1 cm) as the working electrode and a Pt wire and Hg/HgO as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. CQDs@CoII-MOF/CoF was obtained by applying a constant oxidation potential (+1.5 V) for the solution for 10 min at 15 °C.

Typically, 45 mg of GO, 1 mmol of 2-methylimidazole (2-MeIm), and 2 mmol of cobalt acetate tetrahydrate were dissolved in 60 mL of DMSO while being stirred. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was held at 150 °C for 20 h. After being cooled down to room temperature naturally, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed with water and ethanol (3: 1) three times to remove the extra reactant, and then lyophilized and annealed at 800 °C for 2 h in an air atmosphere to obtain the hollow Co3O4 nanoparticles embedded in N, S-co-doped RGO (Ho-Co3O4/NS-RGO).

The BNC powder was coated on CIS/NF by a solvothermal synthesis using ethylene glycol as the solvent. BNC powder (12 mg) was transferred to a 10 mL ethylene glycol solution and ultrasonicated for 10 min. Then, the BNC solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave. The CIS/NF (1 cm × 2 cm) was then infused into the solution. The reactor was heated at 200 °C for 15 h and cooled at room temperature. The material was removed, rinsed several times in both water and ethanol, and dried in an oven at 60 °C. The mass of loading for BNC-CIS on NF was noted to be 5.51 mg cm–2. The sample was named BNC-CIS/NF hereafter. For comparison, we also prepared 5 wt % Ru/C (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), CIS, and BNC with the same catalyst loading on NF separately. The corresponding samples are denoted as Ru/C, CIS/NF, BNC/NF, and bare NF (no catalyst). The details of physical, chemical, and electrochemical analyses are provided in the Supporting Information.

CIS was grown on the surface of NF using a one-step hydrothermal method. Typically, 1 mmol of CuCl, 1 mmol of In(OOCH3)3, and 2 mmol of thiourea were added to three different beakers with 10 mL of DI water. These were stirred vigorously (700 rpm) for 10 min and rapidly transferred into a 100 mL Teflon cylinder in an autoclave. Then, cleaned NF (1 cm × 5 cm) was immersed perpendicular to the bottom of the Teflon cylinder, after adding 20 mL of DI water to the homogeneous solution. The autoclave was closed tightly and kept at 200 °C for 15 h in an oven and then naturally cooled at room temperature. CIS-coated NF was removed and washed several times in DI water and ethanol. The CIS/NF was dried in an oven at 120 °C for further use. The mass loading for CIS was estimated to be 3.57 mg cm–2, which was obtained by the NF mass difference of weighing two times before and after the growth of the sample. NF was cleaned by ultrasonication in 1 M HCl for 30 min to remove the thin surface oxide layer followed by washing with water and ethanol five times each and dried in an oven at 60 °C.

Flower-like nanostructures of manganese, cobalt, and copper on nickel foam (NF) were synthesized by one step hydrothermal treatment (Figure 1). Prior to the synthesis process, bare NF was cut and cleaned in bath sonicator by 1 M HCl, ethanol, and acetone for 10 min each. The cleaned NF piece was then dried at 70 °C for 6 h. The precursor solution was prepared by mixing the manganese acetate (3 mmol), cobalt acetate (8 mmol), copper acetate (1 mmol), urea (24 mmol), ammonium chloride (12 mmol) in 50 mL of DI water and placed on stirrer at 750 rpm for 30 min. The precursor solution along with cleaned piece of NF was poured in 100 mL of Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and place in muffle furnace for 6 h at 120 °C The deposited sample was carefully removed from the autoclave and cleaned several times with DI water and placed on hot plate at 90 °C for 6 h.

An earlier reported work was referred for this synthesis.  First, 15 mg of the as-prepared SiO2 powder was dispersed in 5.0 mL of deionized water via an ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. Then, 1.0 mL of DMDA, 10 mL of 0.548 M 2-MeIM, and 0.4 mL of 0.2 M CTAB aqueous solutions were introduced and magnetically stirred for 15 min. Finally, this mixed solution was transferred to a 30 mL autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 12 h. The final product was centrifuged and washed with ethanol three times.

For Zn5(2-pim)6(MoO4)2 as an example, a mixture of 2-propylimidazole (1.08 mmol, 0.1190 g), H2MoO4 (0.217 mmol, 0.0351 g), Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (0.5 mmol, 0.110 g), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (2 mL), and ethylene glycol (2 mL) was placed in a 20 mL Teflon vial and heated to 120 °C for 3 days. Colorless crystals were obtained in a pure phase in ca. 54% yield after cooling to room temperature. The synthesis of the other seven compounds is described in detail in the Supporting Information (SI).

For Zn5(2-pim)6(MoO4)2 as an example, a mixture of 2-propylimidazole (0.108 mol, 11.89 g), H2MoO4 (21.7 mmol, 3.51 g), Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (50 mmol, 10.98 g), DMF (150 mL) was stirred for about 10 min and then heated at 120 °C for 12 h. A white precipitate formed and was filtered out, washed with EtOH, and dried at 200 °C under a flow of N2. The final product mass was about 9.58 g (ca. 39% yield). Anal. Calcd. (%): C, 33.03; H, 4.58; N, 12.85; Zn, 25.00; Mo, 14.67. Found (%): C, 32.96; H, 4.67; N, 12.99; Zn, 28.89; Mo, 14.33. IR (KBr, cm–1): 3744(w), 2964(w), 2926(w), 1569(w), 1461(m), 1178(w), 1145(w), 1074(w), 881(s), 752(m). The synthesis of the other seven compounds is described in detail in the SI.

First, NF (4.0 cm × 3.5 cm2 in rectangular shape) was cleaned to remove impurities and surface oxides. Through hydrothermal reaction and postcalcining, a layer of vertically arranged NiCo2O4 NW array is first grown on NF.  A 0.582 g amount of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.291 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.222 g of NH4F, and 0.9 g of urea were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water to form a homogeneous pink solution. Then solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with the pretreated Ni foam immersed into the reaction solution. The autoclave was sealed and maintained at 120 °C for 6 h and then cooled to room temperature. The as-synthesized samples were taken out, ultrasonically cleaned at 40 Hz for 5 min in distilled water, rinsed with ethanol for several times, dried at 60 °C for 10 h, and annealed at 450 °C in Ar for 2 h.

To remove the surface nickel oxides, Ni foam (1 cm × 4 cm) was ultrasonically cleaned by acetone and HCl solution (3 M) for 15 min in turn and subsequently rinsed with water and ethanol for three times.  The cleaned Ni foam was immersed into 50 mL of deionized water in a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (60 mL), then sealed, and kept rotating at 160 °C for 24 h. The resultant sample was washed with ethanol three times and dried at 60 °C for 2 h. The weight increment (x mg) of Ni Foam can be directly calculated after the growth of Ni(OH)2. Ni(OH)2 loading = (x mg) × (MNi(OH)2/2MOH) = (x mg) × (93/34) = 2.7x mg, where M stands for the atomic or molecular weight. For as-prepared Ni(OH)2/NF electrode, the loading mass of Ni(OH)2 is about 2.9 mg·cm–2.

Compound 1 was synthesized from a mixture of cobalt chloride hexahydrate (0.213 g, 9 mmol), 1,3-diaminopropane (0.025 mL, 3 mmol), trans-cinnamic acid (0.132 g, 9 mmol), and distilled water (1.0 mL), which was sealed in a Pyrex tube and heated at 140 °C for 3 days, followed by cooling to room temperature at 20 °C/h. The pH of the solution before and after the reaction was ∼5 and ∼4, respectively. The solid products were recovered by vacuum filtration and washed with ethanol. Violet polyhedral-shaped crystals of 1 and an unidentified colorless powder were obtained. The yield of the product was ∼60% based on the cobalt content. The products were stable toward air, UV irradiation, and common solvents such as water, DMSO, THF, and acetonitrile (Figure S1). Compound 1 could not be obtained in the absence of 1,3-diaminopropane, in which the pH value before and after the reaction was ∼3. EDS analysis confirmed the presence of Co. Elemental Anal. Calcd (%): C, 60.32; H, 4.10. Found: C, 60.36; H, 4.10. IR (KBr): 3422 (br), 3082 (w), 3058 (w), 3027 (w), 1639 (m), 1577 (w), 1540 (m), 1496 (w), 1450 (m), 1384 (s), 1290 (w), 1279 (w), 979 (m), 875 (w), 722 (m), 713 (w), 684 (w), 597 (w), 485 (w) cm–1.

Rutile TiO2 nanorods with exposed facets were synthesized by a modified hydrothermal method.  In a typical synthesis, 1 mL of titanium butoxide (97% Aldrich) was added into a mixed solution containing 30 mL of deionized (DI) water and 30 mL of hydrochloric acid (37 wt %). Twenty milligrams of P25 nanoparticles was added as seeds and dispersed in the growth solution by ultrasonication. The hydrothermal reaction was conducted in a 100 mL autoclave at 170 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the product was washed several times with DI water and harvested by centrifugation.

The IrOx-commercial sample was synthesized by Alfa Aesar company, and the IrOx-FHI hydroxide was synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis. Aqueous KOH solutions were prepared from Milli-Q filtered water and KOH (AppliChem, p.a.). The solutions were kept under an inert atmosphere via constant Ar bubbling. K2IrCl6 (Alfa Aesar, Ir 39% min.) was then added in order to obtain a final Ir concentration of 10–2 mol/L and left stirring at RT for 1 h. On the basis of the above protocol, the precursor solution has a KOH:Ir ratio of 5:1. After 1 h of aging, 4 × 62 mL of a precursor solution was added to four 100 mL PTFE-lined vessels. The four vessels were then placed inside the microwave reactor (Anton Paar, Multiwave PRO). The solutions were heated up using a 10 K/min ramp from RT to a selected temperature of 250 °C, under constant stirring using a magnetic PTFE stirrer and maintained at the selected temperature for 1 h. After the treatment, the vessels were left to cool down to RT. The resulting black product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, further dissolved in Millipore-filtered water, sonicated for 5 min, and recentrifuged until the conductivity of the supernatant was measured below 0.05 mS/cm. The solid product was subsequently dried at 80 °C for 12 h and ground in a mortar.

The LCO NSs and NPs were both fabricated by a hydrothermal reaction followed by a calcination process.  For LCO NSs, we first synthesized Co(OH)2 nanosheets as precursor by a modified method reported previously. Briefly, 2 mmol of CoCl2·6H2O and 10 mmol of hexamethylenetetramine (HMT) were dissolved in 36 mL of H2O, which was stirred to form a clear solution. The aqueous solution was subsequently transferred to a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was sealed, maintained at 120 °C for 2 h, and then allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. Afterward, the resulting powder was isolated by centrifugation, washed three times with water, and then dried in an oven under 80 °C overnight. The LCO NSs can be obtained by simply mixing the as-prepared Co(OH)2 with LiOH·H2O and calcining the mixture at 800 °C for 4 h. The LCO NPs were synthesized by a similar procedure. We used CoCl2·6H2O (4 mmol) and urea (5 mmol) as the raw materials in the hydrothermal step (120 °C for 5 h) to fabricated Co2(OH)2CO3 nanowire precursor, which was then transformed into Co3O4 nanowires by heating at 500 °C for 3 h. The dark product was mixed with LiOH·H2O and calcined at 800 °C for 3 h to obtain LCO NPs.

A cobalt-hybrid Framework was synthesized by following a solvothermal method. In particular, 1 mmol of Co(SO4)·6H2O was taken in a 23 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave containing 5 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred for 2 h. To it, 5 mL of pyrazine solution in ethylene glycol was added slowly and left to stir for half an hour to form a homogeneous mixture. Thereafter, 70 μL of hydrofluoric acid (48%) was added dropwise to the homogeneous solution under constant stirring. The mixture was stirred for the next 15 min. The final mixture has the molar composition of CoSO4·6H2O/pyrazine/HF (1:4:2). The autoclave containing the mixture was sealed properly and placed in a hot air oven at 140 °C for 3 days. After they were cooled down to room temperature, the clear orange color needle-shaped crystals were collected and washed repeatedly with deionized water and isopropyl alcohol followed by drying at 60 °C and stored for characterization and electrocatalytic study. The crystals thus obtained here are named Co-MOF.

The bimetallic CoFe-hybrid frameworks were synthesized by following the same solvothermal method as used for the synthesis of Co-MOF and Fe-MOF. In particular, the method used the mixture of Fe(SO4)·7H2O and Co(SO4)·6H2O as the metal precursors, pyrazine as the linker, and HF in ethylene glycol solvent. Likewise, in the case of Co-MOF/Fe-MOF, the molar composition of precursors is maintained as 1:4:2 (CoSO4·6H2O/FeSO4·7H2O/pyrazine/HF). Here three bimetallic CoFe-hybrid frameworks were synthesized by varying the Co/Fe ratios as Co0.5Fe0.5-MOF (dark pink color plate-like crystals), Co0.25Fe0.75-MOF (violet color plate-like crystals), and Co0.75Fe0.25-MOF (pink color rod-shaped crystals) by changing the metal precursors is presented in Table S1.

PdO@Co3O4 was prepared with 5.4 mol % of Pd in relation to Co by hydrothermal synthesis with 2.8 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.815 g) and 0.15 mmol of Na2[PdCl4] (0.045 g) dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water under strong stirring.  In parallel, 0.2 mol of urea was dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water under strong stirring for 30 min. After that, the urea solution was added to the metal solution, transferred to an autoclave, and maintained at 150 °C for 12 h. After that, the solid was filtered in a 0.45 μm membrane under reduced pressure, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 10 h. The as-prepared solid was annealed at 300 °C for 3 h under air.

The spinel Co3O4 was prepared by a typical hydrothermal procedure according to the literature.  Co(NO3)2·6H2O (2.8 mmol, 0.815 g) and 10 mmol of urea (0.6 g) were mixed in 24 mL of 1:1 v/v ethanol/water solution and were submitted to vigorous stirring for 15 min. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 10 h. The resulting solid was filtered in a 0.45 μm poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE) membrane under reduced pressure, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C for 10 h. The as-prepared sample was converted to Co3O4 by annealing at 300 °C for 3 h under air.

IrOX catalysts with different crystallinity were synthesized by Bestaoui and Prouzet hydrothermal methods.  Iridium containing solutions were prepared by dissolving 5 g IrCl3·3H2O (99 wt %) in 100 mL of deionized water. The molar concentration of [OH–] was increased using a syringe pump to add 1 mol L–1 LiOH solution (99 wt %) to 10 mL of the iridium-containing solution at a rate of 200 μL h–1 until it was 5 times that of [Cl–]. The final amount was then increased with deionized water to reach 50 mL, which was transferred to a Teflon hydrothermal kettle and put in a high-pressure reactor for 12 h of reaction at 180 °C.

MoS2 was synthesized by a hydrothermal method.  In a typical method, 1 mmol of ammonium sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2H2O) and 30 mmol of thiourea were dissolved in 17 mL of water, placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave, and kept inside a hot air oven at 200 °C for 24 h. The obtained product was washed with DI water and ethanol until the pH of the supernatant liquid reached 7. The samples were dried overnight at 60 °C in a hot air oven.

All the materials in this experiment were used without any preprocessing. Nickel chloride hexahydrate (0.8 mmol of NiCl2·6H2O) and manganese chloride tetrahydrate (0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 mmol of MnCl2·4H2O) were dissolved in 20 mL of 0.1 M Na2S solution under vigorous stirring to form a homogeneous solution with a color of dark black. Then, the solution was transferred into a 25 mL autoclave followed by immersing a 2 × 4 cm–2 Ni foam into the above solution and maintained at 130 °C for 8 h. After the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally, the Mn-Ni3S2/NF was taken out, rinsed with water and ethanol for several times, and completely dried at room temperature. The prepared Ni3S2 was grown on the Ni foam with different concentrations of Mn2+ doping. (x represents the addition amount of MnCl2·4H2O in reaction precursors, x = 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4.)

Co(NO3)2·6H2O (1.5 mmol) and 1 mmol NH4H2PO4 were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water under stirring. The mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. A piece of NF (3 cm × 2 cm) treated by ultrasonication in hydrochloric acid and acetone was dipped in the solution before the autoclave was sealed. The hydrothermal reaction was conducted in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. When the autoclave cooled, the purple NF was cleaned in deionized water and ethanol and dried in vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

K3[Fe(CN)6] (1 mmol) was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water under stirring before being transferred into another Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave containing the as-prepared CoPi@NF. The hydrothermal reaction mixture was kept at 120 °C for 12 h. When it cooled, the product was cleaned in deionized water and ethanol and dried in vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

Typically, a light-brown solution containing 0.37 g of sulfur powder and 4 mL of n-butylamine was first obtained. Another 4 mL of n-butylamine solution containing 0.24 g of CoCl2·6H2O (∼1 mmol) and 0.27 g of FeCl3·6H2O (∼1 mmol) was then added under constant stirring. A homogeneous solution was initially observed, which was then stirred for another 30 min and loaded into a Teflon-lined autoclave (20 mL). The autoclave was sealed and heated to 100 °C and held for 12 h. The thus-formed sulfide product was centrifuged, washed with ethanol and deionized water in air, and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. The thus-prepared sulfide product was named as FeCo–S-370. With the same preparation process, other contrast samples were prepared just by changing the sulfur powder dosage, for example, to 0.15 g (4.7 mmol). The corresponding product was noted as FeCo–S-150. In addition, the contrast samples containing different Fe–Co molar ratios were also prepared with a sulfur dosage of 0.37 g.

The copper foam (size of 0.5 × 4 cm2) was first cleaned by ultrasonication in 3 M HCl for 30 min, washed thoroughly with water and ethanol, then dried at 60 °C under vacuum. 8-Hydroxyquinoline complex was then loaded on the copper foam through the coordination reaction between Co2+/Fe3+ ions and 8-HQ. In a typical synthesis, 1.4 mmol of 8-HQ (0.203 g), 0.1 mmol of CoCl2·6H2O (0.024 g), and 0.4 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O (0.108 g) were dissolved in 40 ml of ethanol. The solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. A treated copper foam was then added. The mixture reacted at 150 °C for 10 h and was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained sample was noted as Co0.1Fe0.4-HQ/CF. Contrast samples including those with only one kind of metal ions were also prepared with a similar method. The details of material characterization are present in the Supporting Information (SI).

As in the previous synthesis, Ni(OH)2/NF was synthesized by dissolving Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (2 mmol), urea (10 mmol), and NH4F (4 mmol) together in 35 mL of DIW with thorough stirring to form a clarified solution. The mixture was then poured into a 45 mL autoclave, and the NF was then placed into the autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 6 h.
Briefly, ethanol (25 mL) and 2-mercaptoethanol (2.5 mL) were mixed well and transferred into an autoclave, and then the prepared Ni(OH)2 was added. The mixture was heated at 150 °C for 5 h.

Then, 20 mL of 0.1 M aqueous solution of NiCl2·6H2O was added dropwise to an equal portion of 0.1 M Na2MoO4·2H2O under vigorous stirring for 15 min. The milky green suspension was then transferred to a 50 mL capacity Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated to 160 °C, and was held for 6 h. The greenish-yellow precipitate obtained was collected by centrifugation at 2600 rpm for 3 min. Thereafter, the product was washed thoroughly with distilled water and absolute ethanol in turns and dried under vacuum at 70 °C.

α-MoO3 nanobelts were synthesized by a hydrothermal process devised by Lou and Zeng  with slight modification. (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (1.25 g) was added to 20 mL of water under vigorous stirring. After 15 min of continuous stirring, 10 mL of nitric acid (2.4 mol/L) was added dropwise to the above solution. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL capacity Teflon-coated stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 18 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the white product obtained was washed several times with water and dried under vacuum at 70 °C.

In a typical synthesis of Fe-MoS2-1, 0.01 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.05 mmol of (NH4)2MoS4 were dissolved in 12 mL of DMF, and the resulting solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. After heating at 200 °C for 12 h, it was cooled to room temperature, and the resulting black solid was washed three times with 2 mL of deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol (v/v = 1:1) and then dried in vacuo.

FeCl3·6H2O (13.5 mg) and (NH4)2MoS4 (13 mg) were dissolved in 12 mL of DMF, and the mixture, together with pretreated Ni foam (2 cm × 2 cm), was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the resulting Fe-doped MoS2/NF was washed three times with 2 mL of deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol (v/v = 1:1).

The as-synthesized ligand L1 (10 mg, 0.02 mmol) was taken in a 15 ml Pyrex glass tube, and to this, 1 mL of DMF was added. 19.68 mg (0.08 mmol) of Mn(OAc)2·4H2O was dissolved in 3 mL of deionized water and added to it. The resulting solution in the sealed Pyrex glass tube was heated at 100 °C in an oven for 24 h, and the white-colored single crystals of Mn-MOF were obtained in a yield of 75%.

The as-synthesized ligand L1 (10 mg, 0.02 mmol), was taken in a 15 ml Pyrex glass tube, and to this, 1 mL of DMF was added. 19.68 mg (0.08 mmol) of Mn(OAc)2·4H2O was dissolved in 3 mL of deionized water and added to it. A piece of clean and activated NF (1 cm × 3 cm) was immersed in the resulting mixture in an inclined manner, and the Pyrex glass tube was sealed. The sealed Pyrex glass tube was heated at 100 °C in an oven for 24 h, and the white-colored single crystals of the Mn-MOF were successfully grown upon the surface of NF, visible by the naked eye.

The NiFe2O4 nanoparticles were synthesized by a traditional hydrothermal strategy.  First, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and NiCl2·6H2O powder (Fe/Ni molar ratio = 2) were mixed with 12 mL of distilled water. Subsequently, 12 mL of a 2 M KOH solution was added to the mixture and then placed in sealed Teflon vessels (inner volume 80 mL) at 160 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, a brownish powder is obtained by suction filtration. The product was named NFO.

MoS2/TS2/TMO hybrids were prepared via a simple low-temperature hydrothermal process. In a typical procedure, 150 mg of TMO or TMO nanorods and certain amount of l-cysteine (0.12, 0.25, 0.36, and 0.48 g) were dispersed in 60 mL of deionized water in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 18 h and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The resulting product was collected by a filter and washed by deionized water thoroughly. Finally, the black powder was dried under vacuum. For convenience, the powder was marked as TMS. T symbolizes transition metal, M is the initial letter of molybdenum, and S represents sulfidation.

MoS2 nanosheets were also prepared via a hydrothermal process. In a typical procedure, 1 mmol sodium molybdate and 5 mmol thioacetamide were dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 18 h. Reference samples CoS2 and NiS2 were synthesized using a similar procedure, except that Co(NO3)2·6H2O or Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was used to substitute Na2MoO4.

Ni-BDC was prepared by our previously reported method  with a few modifications. 0.237 g (1 mmol) nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) and 0.166 g (1 mmol) terephthalic acid were mixed in 35 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide to form a homogeneous solution after 3 min of stirring in a beaker. After that, distilled water and ethanol with a volume of 2.5 mL were added into the above solution slowly and stirred for 30 min. Then, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel and sealed. After the solvothermal reaction for 10 h at 120 °C, the green products were filtrated out and washed with distilled water and ethanol three times, respectively, and then dried in a vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

In the absence of metal salts, H4DOT (31.70 mg, 0.16 mmol) ligand was added to the solution of 10 mL of DMF, 0.6 mL of H2O, and 0.6 mL of EtOH. The above mixture and the as-prepared NF were transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 100 °C for 24 h to obtain Ni-MOF-74/NF.

Briefly, H4DOT (31.70 mg, 0.16 mmol), FeCl2·2H2O (47.73 mg, 0.24 mmol), CoCl2·6H2O (28.55 mg, 0.12 mmol), and MnCl2·4H2O (23.75 mg, 0.12 mmol) were dissolved in a mixture solution of 10 mL of DMF, 0.6 mL of H2O, and 0.6 mL of EtOH under magnetic stirring to form a homogeneous solution at room temperature. To make them clean and activate their surface, NFs were treated with ultrasound in 1 M HCl, acetone, H2O, and ethanol for 20 min, respectively, then dried quickly at room temperature. The above mixture and the as-prepared NF were transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 100 °C for 24 h. Especially, to gain the prominent electrochemical activity of the catalytic electrode, the feeding number and ratio between divalent metal ions (Fe2+, Co2+, Mn2+) are also optimized in this experiment. Subsequently, the similar synthetic approach was applied to fabricate FeCoMnNi-MOF-74/NF-1, FeCoMnNi-MOF-74/NF-2, and FeCoMnNi-MOF-74/NF-3 electrodes by using different molar salt ratios of 2:3:3, 3:2:3, or 3:3:2 for Fe, Co, and Ni with the total feeding mount remaining at 0.48 mmol.

H4DOT (31.70 mg, 0.16 mmol), FeCl2·4H2O (63.25 mg, 0.32 mmol), and CoCl2·6H2O (38.15 mg, 0.16 mmol) were added to the mixed solution of 10 mL of DMF, 0.6 mL of H2O, and 0.6 mL of EtOH with magnetic stirring. The mixture solution with the NF was allowed to react at 100 °C for 24 h to obtain the FeCoNi-MOF-74/NF. CoMnNi-MOF-74/NF and FeMnNi-MOF-74/NF electrodes were synthesized by keeping the ratio 2:1 for metal salts with the total feeding mount remaining at 0.48 mmol.

FeCl-3·4H2O (1.0 mmol) and CoCl2·2H2O (1.0 mmol) were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. To this solution, NaCl (2.0 mmol) and urea (2.0 mmol) were added and the mixture was stirred for 5 min at room temperature. After that, the mixture was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 100 °C for 12 h. The product was washed several times with absolute ethanol and distilled water and then heated for 2 h at 300–400 °C in an electric furnace to construct different CoFe2O4 hierarchical nanostructures. The final product was cooled naturally to reach room temperature.

CoFe-MOF was prepared on NF using the solvothermal method based on the literature with some modifications.  NF (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm) was immersed in HCl (1 M) for 20 min, washed with deionized water (3 times) and absolute ethanol, and calcined in air at 500 °C for 2 h to obtain oxidized NF. Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.785 g, 2.7 mmol), Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (0.218 g, 0.54 mmol), and nicotinic acid (0.665 g, 5.4 mmol) were dissolved in dry DMF (40 mL) and stirred for 15 min under 600 rpm (solution 1). This solution was transferred together with the oxidized NF into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 24 h. This resulted in a NF decorated with red-brownish crystals. After washing the modified NF with deionized water and ethanol (3 times), the formed product (NF/CoFe-MOF) was dried at 100 °C under air for 12 h. NF/CoFe-MOF was further calcined at 500 °C (heating rate = 6.4 °C min–1 under N2 atmosphere) for 2 h to yield NF/Co3O4/CoFe2O4.

CoCl2·2H2O (1.0 mmol) was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. To this solution, NaCl (2.0 mmol) and urea (2.0 mmol) were added, and the mixture was stirred for 5 min at room temperature. After that, the mixture was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 100 °C for 12 h. The formed powder was washed several times with absolute ethanol and distilled water and then heated for 2 h at 300–400 °C in an electric furnace to produce different Co3O4 hierarchical nanostructures. The final product was cooled naturally to room temperature.

A total of 1 mmol of CuCl2·2H2O was dissolved in 2 mL of ethanol, and then 10 mL of n-hexane was added to the solution. The CuCl2 solution and 1 × 2 cm2 Ni foam were transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then the autoclave was heated at 120 °C for 3 h. After it was cooled to room temperature, the Ni foam was washed by ethanol three times and dried in a vacuum at 70 °C overnight. To study the effect of the ratio of NiO to CuO on the catalyst, we also changed the addition amounts of CuCl2 to 0.48 or 2 mmol, and the other conditions remained unchanged.

First, a piece of NF (3 cm × 2 cm) was cleaned with acetone, 3 M HCl, and DI water in turn. Then, 5 mmol of potassium ferricyanide and 24 mmol of urea were mixed up in 35 mL of DI water. After stirring, a transparent kelly solution was formed. Next, the kelly solution along with the above-cleaned NF was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 10 h. When the autoclave cooled down naturally, the resulting sample was rinsed with DI water and ethanol several times and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, the NiHCF precursor catalyst was achieved. In addition, in order to explore the optimal amount of potassium ferricyanide and urea on the catalytic performance, NiHCF precursor controlled by urea and NiHCF precursor controlled by potassium ferricyanide were also prepared using the same procedure (see details in the Supporting Information), where the hydrothermal product synthesized under 5 mmol potassium ferricyanide with 0 mmol urea and 0 mmol potassium ferricyanide with 24 mmol urea in the same way were named as K3Fe(CN)6 + NF and urea + NF, respectively.

FeNi LDH nanosheets grown vertically on various metal foils (Fe, Ni, and FeNi alloy) can be achieved via a facile hydrothermal method by directly immersing the substrates into the corresponding reaction solutions, as illustrated in Figure 1. Briefly, the preparation of FeNi LDH nanosheets involved precipitation of iron and nickel nitrates on metal foil in aqueous solution, which is induced by urea hydrolysis in an alkaline solution upon hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C for 12 h (see the details in the Experimental Section in the Supporting Information). Because FeNi LDH is a typical LDH, ions and molecules can be present between the stacking layers along the c-axis.  During the reaction process, urea decomposed into ammonia and carbonate, which served as the intercalated anion.  CO32– generated by the decomposition of the urea is inclined to adsorb on the (001) surface, passivating this plane. As a result, the growth of planes along the [001] direction is suppressed.  Therefore, the FeNi LDH crystals are stacked in the direction vertical to the substrate surface.

A carbon fiber (CF) substrate was first cut into an appropriate dimension and sonicated in ethanol, acetone, and distilled water. To prepare the precursors solution, 1.7 g of Ni(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.3 g of SnCl2·2H2O were mixed well in 50 mL of ultrapure distilled water. To the obtained homogeneous solution, 0.9 g of urea and 0.2 g of NH4F were added by stirring the entire content. The volume of the solution was made up to 100 mL by distilled water. Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave containing the cleaned CF substrate standing vertically. The whole content was then heated in an oven at 120 °C for 6 h. The CF was then taken out, rinsed with ethanol/water, and dried in an oven at 60 °C.

Figure 1 shows the schematics for the preparation processes of NiNS and NiMoNS. To prepare NiMoNS, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (364.9 mg), (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (22.2 mg), and C4H6N2 (325.1 mg), with a Mo/Ni molar ratio of 0.1, were firstly dissolved in 36 mL DI water. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave which was subsequently heated via a hydrothermal process at 140 °C for 12 h. The solid sample from the hydrothermal process was rinsed with DI water for several times, followed by drying at 40 °C for 12 h in a vacuum drier, affording NiMoNS. The preparation process of NiNS was similar to that of NiMoNS, except that (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O was not used. MoO3 was prepared by a process similar to that of NiMoNS, except that Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was not used. A NiMo-P catalyst was prepared by physically mixing NiNS with MoO3. Catalysts, denoted by NiMo-0.02, NiMo-0.05, NiMo-0.08, NiMo-0.12, NiMo-0.2, and NiMo-0.3, were prepared by processes similar to that of NiMoNS, but the amount of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O was changed to obtain Mo/Ni molar ratios of 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, 0.12, 0.2, and 0.3, respectively.

Nanostructured FeNi LDH@NWSSF was synthesized through a urea-assisted hydrothermal method on NWSSF. In a typical procedure, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (19 mg), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (274 mg), and urea (99 mg) were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water and stirred to form a clear solution. The aqueous solution and SNWSSF (placed vertically) were transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was sealed and maintained at 150 °C for 8 h. The as-prepared FeNi LDH@NWSSF was obtained by rinsing with deionized water and ethanol and oven-dried. The weight of FeNi double hydroxides was measured by weighing the NWSSF before and after the hydrothermal process; a loading amount of 0.875 mg cm–2 was achieved. For comparison, the FeNi LDH loaded onto nickel foam (NF) and stainless steel foam (SSF) were prepared following the same procedure with the same Fe/Ni ratio and they were named as FeNi LDH@NF and FeNi LDH@SSF, respectively. The FeNi LDH@NWSSF-C1 and FeNi LDH@NWSSF-C2 were also prepared. FeNi-layered double hydroxide powder was collected from the bottom of autoclave, washed, and dried for characterization use.

(Ni–MoO2)@C/NF NWs was prepared via the two-step method (Figure 1). Specifically, a piece of the NF substrate (1.5 cm × 3.0 cm) was subjected to ultrasonic cleaning in 3.0 M HCl mixed with ultra-pure water and ethanol for about 15 min. Later on, the NF substrate was put in a steel autoclave that contains hybrid liquid of ethylene glycol, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, C19H42BrN, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, and kept in a drying oven for 24 h at 160 °C. After the temperature was dropped to about 25 °C, it was cleaned with ultra-pure water and C2H5OH for three times, respectively, and dried for 24 h at 60 °C. Afterward, it was kept for 1.5 h at three temperatures (350, 450, and 550 °C), respectively, under the 5% H2 + 95% Ar ambience, and the obtained sample was named (Ni–MoO2)@C/NF NWs. We removed the materials from NF by the ultrasonication method to test the mass loading of (Ni–MoO2)@C/NF NWs, which is 20.5 mg cm–2. Ni–MoO2/NF was obtained in pure water solution with the Mo and Ni source; Ni@C/NF and MoO2@C/NF were obtained by the same method without the Mo and Ni source, respectively.

NiFe–Cl LDH with Ni/Fe ratio of 3:1 was synthesized using a hydrothermal method adapted from Dang et al.  In a typical procedure, 0.45 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O was added to a centrifuge tube containing 900 μmol of TEA. Afterward, a solution with 1.35 mmol of NiCl2·6H2O and 1.2 g of TEA (8 mmol) dissolved in 90 mL of a mixture 1:1 (v/v) ethanol/water was added. The resulting solution was mixed thoroughly, until complete dissolution, and transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was placed in a preheated oven at 120 °C for 6 h. Finally, it was cooled to room temperature, and the final brown powder was filtered and washed with H2O and EtOH several times and dried during 24 h in vacuum.

A facile hydrothermal method was utilized to fabricate the Ni(OH)2 and Fe–Ni(OH)2 nanobelts. A certain number of moles of NiSO4·6H2O and Fe2(SO4)3·xH2O was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and keeping stirring continuously for 30 min. Dissolve 8 mmol NaOH in 20 mL deionized water to gain NaOH solution. Subsequently, the NaOH solution was slowly dropped into the above NiSO4·6H2O/Fe2(SO4)3·xH2O mix solution in 20 min. After it was stirred for 0.5 h, the mix solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 24 h. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed three times with DI water and ethanol, and then dried in vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight. The doping level of Fe atoms in the host structure of Ni(OH)2 was controlled by precisely regulating the molar ratio of Ni/Fe salts in the precursor solution, while with the same total amount of metal ions in the initial solutions (Ni2+ + Fe3+ = 7 mmol). The Fe–Ni(OH)2 samples that obtained by introducing 0.25 mmol, 0.5 and 2 mmol Fe2(SO4)3·xH2O were denoted as 0.25Fe–Ni(OH)2, 0.5Fe–Ni(OH)2 and 2Fe–Ni(OH)2 respectively (Table S1). The synthesis procedures of Ni(OH)2 nanobelts are as same as the above synthesis of Fe–Ni(OH)2 nanobelts but without adding Fe2(SO4)3·xH2O.

All samples were fabricated by a one-step solvent-thermal method. Typical procedure for Ni1Co1-LDHs-E1D1 as for instance: Urea (6 mmol), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (1.5 mmol), and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1.5 mmol) were firstly dispersed in ethanol (15 mL) to obtain a light purple solution. Then N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (15 mL) was poured into the transparent solution. After ultrasonic for 10 min, the above precursor solution was transferred into a 50 mL sealed Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the blue-grey powder was collected by centrifugal separation, then washed with ethanol for last three times, and dried in a vacuum oven under 60 °C overnight. For comparison, LDHs contained different molar ratios of metal salts (Ni:Co = 3:0, 2:1, 1:2, and 0:3, the total molar amount of metal precursor was kept at 3 mmol) and different volume ratios of solvents (VEthanol:VDMF = 1:0, 3:1, 2:1, 1:2, 1:3, and 0:1, the total volume of solvents were 30 mL) were synthesized by the same process.

Synthesis of Co3S4/MnS2: 160 mg Co(NO3)2∙6H2O and 160 mg MnN2O6∙4H2O were dissolved in 20 mL ethanol and stirred for another 20 min. After the solution was completely mixed, 320 mg TAA was added to the mixture, stirred for 15 min, transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, the treated NF was added to the autoclave, and then reacted at 160 °C for 3 h.

Synthesis of MIL-88B(Co)-NH2 or Synthesis of MIL-88B(Mn)-NH2: 320 mg Co(NO3)2∙6H2O (or 320 mg MnN2O6∙4H2O) and 160 mg 2-Aminoterephthalic acid were dissolved in 20 mL DMF and stirred for 20 min. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and the treated NF was added, and then reacted at 160 °C for 24 h.

Synthesis of MIL-88B(Co/Mn)-NH2: 160 mg Co(NO3)2∙6H2O and 160 mg 2-Aminoterephthalic acid were dissolved in 20 mL DMF and stirred for 20 min. After the solution was completely mixed, 160 mg MnN2O6∙4H2O was added to the mixture and stirred for another 20 min. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and the treated NF was added, and then reacted at 160 °C for 24 h. The synthesis of MIL-88B(Co/Mn)-NH2 is shown in Schemal S1.

Synthesis of MILN-based Co3S4/MnS2: 320 mg TAA was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol, stirred for 15 min, transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, the MIL-88B(Co/Mn)-NH2 grown on the foamed nickel synthesized in the previous step was also added to the autoclave, and then reacted at 160 °C for 3 h.

Synthesis of Fe2O3 catalyst. The Fe2O3 catalyst was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.24 g of iron(III) nitrate nonahydrate was added into 50 mL deionized (DI) water, and the pH of the solution was tuned to 7 by sodium hydroxide after sufficient stirring. Next, the light-yellow solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was then put into an oven and heated at 150 °C for 24 h. After the autoclave was cooled down, the precipitates were collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and DI water several times, and finally dried in a vacuum drying oven at 40 °C. The dried red powder was used for characterization.

Synthesis of α-FeOOH catalyst. The α-FeOOH catalyst was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Typically, iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (0.58 g) was added to 40 mL DI water and stirred to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 12 with 0.5 M sodium hydroxide solution under vigorous stirring. After that, the slurry was transferred into a 70 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was then put into an oven and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The precipitates were then collected by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and DI water several times, and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 40 °C. The dried powder was used for characterization.

First, dissolve 0.3 g Fe(NO3)3 and 0.05 g S in the mixture of 15 ml distilled water, 5 ml ethylene glycol and 30 ml toluene and stir the mixed solution at the rate of 400 r min−1 for 2 h at room temperature. The prepared solution was then transferred to the 100 ml PTFE reactor. Next, successively put 5 mm * 5 mm Ni foam into anhydrous ethanol, acetone, 2 mol L−1 dilute hydrochloric acid and distilled water for 15 min to obtain clean Ni foam. To ensure that the Co, S-NiFe2O4 grows evenly on the front and back of the Ni foam, the treated Ni foam needs to be welded to the nickel strip, and then the nickel strip is bent so that it acts as the upholder to make the nickel foam stand upright in the 100 ml PTFE reactor liner containing the reaction solution. After assembling the reaction kettle, put it into the muffle furnace, set the temperature to 150 ℃ with a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1, and heat it for 5 h; after cooling to room temperature, take out the reaction kettle. Co, S-NiFe2O4 was grown on nickel foam, and eventually rinse Ni foam repeatedly with ethanol and dry it.

NiMoO4 @NF precursor was in-situ grown on the surface of NF via a facile hydrothermal approach. Nickel foam (NF) (1 cm × 2 cm) was first immersed and then sonicated in 0.1 M H2SO4, acetone solution, and ethanol solution for 20 min to remove the oxide layer on the surface, then the cleaned NF were dried in a vacuum oven. Then, the same mole ratio of Ni(NO3)20.6 H2O and Na2MoO40.2 H2O were dissolved in 30 ml deionized water under stirring until completely dissolved. Afterward, the dried NF were subsequently immersed into the above solution, and then were transferred into 50 ml of Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling down, the as-obtained sample were taken out and washed with ethanol and DI water for several times, and the NiMoO4 @NF precursor were obtained after drying.

Fe foam (FF) was washed by 1 mol/L of HCl, acetone and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C under vacuum overnight. Then 8 pieces of cleaned FFs (1 ×1.5 cm2; Fig. S1), 5 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6 H2O and 25 mmol of urea were put into 50 mL deionized (DI) water with 30 min ultrasonication to promote salt dispersion. The mixture was then transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 90 °C for 12 h to obtain CoFe-LDH/FF, which was subsequently washed by DI water, ethanol and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

The synthesis procedure for Mo-Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN product is illustrated in Scheme 1. The hydrothermal synthesis process of Mo doped Ni, Fe precursor was performed at 120 °C for 12 h, followed by an annealing treatment at 380 °C for 3 h in NH3 atmosphere. The as-obtained product is denoted as Mo-Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN. For comparison, the same synthesis procedure is adopted for synthesis of Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN without the addition of Mo.

In a typical synthesis, 0.3982 g of Ni(acac)2, 0.0795 g of FeCl2, 0.05 g of MoNa2O4, and 0.5608 g of C6H12N4 were dissolved in 35 mL deionized water under magnetic stirring for 30 min. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. After hydrothermal treatment, the precursor was washed by deionized water and ethanol for three times and dried at 80 °C overnight. The as-obtained powder was calcined at 380 °C for 3 h under NH3 atmosphere with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The other catalysts can be also obtained with different molar ratios of Mo/Ni/Fe.

Ni3S4 hollow nanoprisms were synthesized via a solvothermal method. In total, the above Ni precursor nanoprisms (96 ​mg) was redispersed into ethanol (48 ​ml) with the addition of thioacetamide (TAA, 135 ​mg), which was averagely transferred into three sealed Teflon-lined autoclaves (20 ​mL) at 120 ​°C for 6 ​h. After cooling down to room temperature, the black precipitated product was harvested via centrifugation at 6000 ​rpm and washed 3 times with ethanol.

Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O (3 mmol) and FeCl3.6 H2O (0.2 mmol) were dissolved in an mixed solution of ultrapure water (25 mL) and methanol (15 mL) under stirring for 30 min.
