The obtained solution with a piece of cleaned NF were transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 8 h. The as-deposited NF was washed with ultrapure water and alcohol and dried at 60 °C, and then heated at 350 °C for 2 h in air to obtain the Fe doped NiO sample (named as Ni(Fe)O). 0.2 mmol Fe doping content was selected for the subsequent synthesis due to its best activity (see Fig. S1). Then, the Ni(Fe)O (1 * 1 cm) sample was employed as working electrode to conduct cyclic voltammetry (CV) activation for 60 cycles at a rate of 50 mV s−1 (see Fig. S2). The obtained sample was further treated by OER operation and directly named as Ni(Fe)OOH.

Ni(OH)2 self-supported on Ni foam (2 cm × 4 cm) was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. The Ni foam was cleaned in 20% HCl, methanol, and deionized water for 10 min sequentially under sonication and then 1 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 6 mmol urea were dissolved in 45 mL of water to form a homogeneous solution by vigorous stirring. The clean Ni foam was immersed in the solution, transferred to a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed, and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the Ni foam was rinsed with methanol and deionized water several times and dried at 40 °C for 6 h in the air to produce Ni(OH)2 nanosheets on Ni foam.

LaNiO3 nanoparticles were synthesized by hydrothermal method. Dissolve La(NO3)3· 6 H2O, Ni(NO3)2· 6 H2O and a moderate amount of glycine in deionized water (50 mL) according to the stoichiometric ratio. After stirring for 10 min, slowly titrate with NH3·H2O to pH = 8, continue to stir for 10 min, and then transfer the solution into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL), and heat for 12 h at 180 °C in the blast drying oven (DGG-90030 G). The solid product was washed three times with deionized (DI) water and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min (Cence, H1850), followed by further drying in a vacuum oven (DZG 6050) at 60 °C for 6 h. The La-Ni complex crystal precursors powder is obtained. Grind the precursor into a powder, and then, it was placed in muffle furnace (KSL-1200X), heated to 650 °C at 5 °C min−1 and annealed in O2 atmosphere for 2 h to obtain LaNiO3.

The Ir-NCT/CC electrocatalyst was synthesized via a two-step method, consisting of hydrothermal synthesis and heat-treatment procedure. First, 2 g of pyrene was dispersed into 160 mL HNO3. The mixture was subsequently heated to 80 ℃ for 12 h under refluxing. The yellow powder (1,3,6-trinitropyrene) was obtained after washing by DI water and drying under vacuum. Then, the 3D carbon cloth (CC) was activated by heating it in HNO3 at 100 ℃ for 2 h and washed with DI water. The above powder (0.3 g) was dispersed in 55 mL DI water and 5 mL ammonia aqueous ultrasonically. A piece of the CC was immersed in a para-polyphenylene-lined autoclave with the above mixed solution. The autoclave was tightly sealed and maintained at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After cooling down naturally, the as-obtained substrate was collected, and slightly washed with DI water. Next, the surface amino functionalized carbon substrate was immersed in IrCl3 (0.4 mg/mL) aqueous solution under ultrasonic condition for 15 min. Finally, the obtained carbon substrate with Ir atoms was freeze dried and further annealed with urea in a tubular furnace at 800 ℃ for 2 h under N2 atmosphere. The as-prepared catalyst was named Ir-NCT/CC.

NiFe LDH was prepared by a hydrothermal method. Typically, 1.5 mmol of hexahydrate, nickel chloride (99%), 0.5 mmol of hexahydrate, ferric chloride (99%) were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water under vigorous stirring (marked solution C), and 5 mmol of urea was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water (marked solution D). Then, solutions C and D were mixed under stirring and transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor. The reaction was kept for 6 h at 120 °C before cooling down. The yellow precipitate was collected and washed with deionized water repeatedly until the solution was neutral. Then, the product was dried overnight under vacuum.

MCo2S4 (M = Ni, Cu and Co) was prepared by a typical two-step hydrothermal process (Scheme 1). The nickel foam is pretreated before the synthetic NiCo2O4 material is prepared. A piece of Ni foam (3 cm × 5 cm) was treated by ultrasound three times by employing a 3 M hydrochloric acid solution, acetone and ultrapure water to eliminate the impurities and oxides from the material. In a typical preparation, Ni(NO3)2.6H2O (2 mmol), Co(NO3)2.6H2O (4 mmol), CO(NH2)2 (12 mmol) and NH4F (10 mmol) were added in 70 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 10 min to obtain uniform solution. The pretreated Ni foam and above clear solution were then put into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the reactor was taken out from the oven and cooled to room temperature. The Ni foam with the NiCo precursors were washed with ultrapure water and ethanol five times and then dried at 60 °C for 6 h. The NiCo2O4 nanorods were prepared by sequential annealing under air at 350 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The CuCo2O4 nanorods were prepared through similar methods by replacing Ni(NO3)2.6H2O with Cu(NO3)2.3H2O.

For a control experiment, Co3O4 nanorods were also synthesized through similar methods. In the next step, 0.05 mol/L Na2S.9H2O was dissolved into 80 mL deionized water to obtain a clear solution. Then the homogeneous solution and the Ni foam with NiCo2O4 were inserted in a 100 mL Teflon-lined reactor and heated at 110 °C for 4 h. The Nickel foam with NiCo2S4 was taken out from the reactor after naturally cooling down to 25 °C, washed with ultrapure water and absolute ethanol five times and kept under vacuum at 50 °C for 2 h.

The overall synthesis process was a simple solvothermal reaction. First, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.25 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.5 mmol Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O, 2.5 mmol urea, and 2 mmol NH4F were dissolved in a solution consisting of 25 mL ethylene glycol and 10 mL DMF. The amount of Co(NO3)2.6 H2O added, which was varied for obtaining different samples, was 0, 0.025, 0.125, 0.165, 0.245, 0.285, and 0.325 mmol. After thorough stirring and dissolution, the prepared homogeneous solution was loaded into a PTFE reactor and heated at 140 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature (~ 24 °C), the products were washed more than five times with water and ethanol to remove any impurities. Finally, the samples were vacuum dried overnight at 60 °C.

For the synthesis of high-entropy oxide (Mg0.2Fe0.2Co0.2Ni0.2Cu0.2)O containing five cations, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (99 % purity, Sigma-Aldrich), Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (98 % purity, Sigma-Aldrich), Co(NO3)·6H2O (98 % purity, Sigma-Aldrich), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (97 % purity, Junsei), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (99 % purity, Sigma-Aldrich), and NaOH (98 % purity, Sigma-Aldrich) were purchased. The five (for HEO) or four (for MEOs) metal nitrate precursors (each 0.2 mmol) were dissolved in distilled water (10 mL) in order to obtain homogeneous mixtures of solution. The pH of the solutions was raised by the addition of 6 M NaOH solution (10 mL), and the solutions were stirred with a magnetic stirrer bar at room temperature for 1 h. The solutions were then transferred into a Teflon autoclave and were kept in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The precipitates obtained were washed several times using distilled water and ethanol. After centrifugation, the collected powders were dried at 80 °C for 10 h and subsequently were calcined at 1000 °C for 12 h in a box furnace under an air atmosphere followed by natural cooling.

Firstly, the carbon cloth (2 × 2 cm) was ultrasonically washed with deionized water and ethanol, then be treated with KOH and hydrochloric acid reported previously . To enhance the interaction between active materials and carbon cloth, the treated carbon cloth (CC) was immersed in a concentrated HNO3 solution at 80 ℃ for 2 h, and then washed with deionized water to neutrality and dried for standby application. Then, the Co-S/CC was synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Dissolving 0.1 M Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.6 M CO(NH2)2, and 0.1 M CH4N2S in 15 mL of a mixed solution involving deionized water and ethanol (Vwater/Vethanol = 4:1)with vigorous stirring until obtained clear solution. The aforementioned mixture was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then, a piece of CC (1 cm * 2 cm) was placed in the above autoclave and kept at 180 ℃ for 18 h. After being cooled down to room temperature naturally, the CC turned to a pitaya color. Then, the as-prepared sample was rinsed with deionized water several times and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ˚C for 12 h. Finally, the sample was placed in a quartz boat and calcined at 350 ℃ for 3 h with 3 ℃ min−1 under N2. The synthesized material is recorded as Co-S/CC.

Nanostructured metal oxides such as MnCo2O4, FeCo2O4, Co3O4, NiCo2O4, CuCo2O4, and ZnCo2O4 were synthesized in two steps. In the first step, the required amounts of the starting chemicals were dissolved in a mixture of 6 ml DI water and 30 ml isopropanol (See Table 1 for details). In this solution, urea was added. The resulting solution was stirred for 30 min using a magnetic stirrer and then transferred into a 45 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The reaction was carried out at 120 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. After the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed with water and ethanol, and dried at 80 °C overnight. In the second step, the dried powder was ground and calcined at 350 °C in air for 2 h at a ramping rate of 5 °C/min to obtain the metal oxides.

Pretreatment of nickel foam: the nickel foam (NF) (2 × 3 cm2) was immersed in a 1.0 M hydrochloric acid solution and ultrasonically treat it for 15 minutes to remove the surficial oxide layer. The NF was thoroughly rinsed with ultrapure water, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. Using acid etching method, 20 mL 3.6 × 10−3 M HCl solution was added into a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined autoclave, and the dried NF was put in the autoclave, and heated it at 100 °C for 20 h. After that, the sample was taken out and rinsed with ultrapure water to obtain Ni(OH)2/NF. During the etching process, the following reactions occurred on the NF:Ni + 2 H+ = Ni2+ + H2Ni2+ + 2 OH−1 = Ni(OH)2

Zirconium-based MOF termed UiO-66 was synthesized by a simple method reported by Qiu et al. . Acid promoted solvothermal process was employed with slight alterations in quantities of reactants for optimized results. 150 mg of ZrCl4 and 115 mg of BDC were dissolved in 30 mL of DMF solvent, 2 mL acetic acid was introduced into the solution with constant mixing for 2 h and ultrasonication mixing for 20 min. This solution was then transferred to 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. After the completion of reaction time, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the solid product was separated by centrifuge. The product was rinsed thrice with DMF and Ethanol to get rid of the residual reactants and dried overnight at 70 °C.

Pure MoS2 was synthesized by using the hydrothermal method reported by Qiu et al.  with slight modification in temperature for optimized results. In a typical process, 1.5 g of ammonium molybdate (molybdenum precursor) and 0.75 g of thiourea (sulfur source) were dissolved in 70 mL DI water. The reaction mixture was stirred for one hour with help of a magnetic stirrer and further sonicated for 20 min. It was then poured into the Teflon-lined autoclave reaction vessel at 200 °C and 24 h. Black-colored MoS2 nanoparticles were obtained. The final product was rinsed with DI water followed by ethanol and dried at 70 °C overnight.

The ultrathin Fex(Co2Ni1)2.5-OOH-NS are prepared via one-pot solvothermal method, which can be prepared as described in Scheme 1. In brief, specific amount of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved into ethylene glycol. Then Urea, NH4F and Polyvinyl Pyrrolidone (PVP, 4000) were added into the former solution in turn. The Fe/Co/Ni stoichiometric proportion of the catalysts could be adapted easily by modifying the quantity of iron salt. After the solvothemal treatment, the final collected materials were washed and dried and served directly as OER catalysts. More information on the experimental process can be found in the Experimental Section. Interestingly, it was discovered that Fe salts play an essential role in the synthesis of Fex(Co2Ni1)2.5-OOH-NS elertrodes, which not only participate in the synthesis catalyst as an Fe source, but also control the structure formation of different morphologies.

The FeCoNi (Oxy)hydroxide nanosheets could be compounded via a facile solvothermal method. Fe1.25(Co2Ni1)2.5-OOH-NS as an example: Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (51 mg), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (146 mg) and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (73 mg) were dissolved into 35 mL ethyl alcohol. Then Urea (150 mg), NH4F (37 mg) and PVP (450 mg) were added into the prior solution in turn. The mixed solution was homogenized by stirring for 30 min before transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 100 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the product was gathered by centrifugation. The solid was subsequently washed separately with water and ethanol for several times and dried at 60 °C under vacuum. Other materials use the same method, except the Fe(NO3)3·9H2O content is 0, 10.1 mg, 25.3 mg, 75.2 mg and 101 mg, respectively.

Ni mesh (1 cm × 2 cm) used in all experiment was first dipped and sonicated sequentially in acetone and 0.1 M HCl for 10 min, then was washed several times by distilled water to remove the residual acid from its surface. In a typical preparation, 1.5 g H3BO4, 0.05 g SDS, and 0.75 g C6H5Na3O7·2H2O were dissolved in 100 mL ultrapure water. Then 4 mmol NiSO4 and 1 mmol CoSO4 were added into mixture. The as-prepared solutions were used with cleaned Ni mesh for cathodic deposition at 200 mA for 90 min. Afterwards, the Ni-Co mesh was put in a 45 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave within 30 mL of 0.08 M Na2S solution, then the hydrothermal reaction would maintain 150 °C for 9 h and naturally cool to room temperature. The final catalytic electrodes were collected after washed with deionized water (DI) several times and denoted as Co-Ni3S2-2. When the amount of Ni and Co was changed to 4.5 and 0.5 mmol, the obtained electrodes were denoted as Co-Ni3S2-1. For comparison, pure Ni3S2 electrodes were obtained with in the Ni amount of 5 mmol. The electrode sheet precursor with a size of 1 × 2 cm was synthesized by a hydrothermal method, and the loading amount of the catalyst was about 22.3 mg.

The Ni(OH)2 precursor was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis process, 0.02 mol of NiCl2 was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. Then 10 mL of 6 M NaOH solution was added under vigorous stirring. 0.6 mmol of Na2SO4 latter was added into the solution. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with distilled water several times. The Ni(OH)2 was obtained after drying for 12 h at 60 °C in the vacuum oven.

N-NiS was synthesized using a hydrothermal method, the process was as followed: Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.58 g), hexamethylenetetramine (1.13 g) and thioacetamide (0.62 g) were dissolved in 30 mL distilled water. The mixture was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. After cooling down to room temperature the product was separated and collected by centrifugation and drying in a vacuum oven overnight at 60 °C.

In a typical synthesis of Fe0.95S1.05, Fe0.9Co0.05S1.05, Fe0.88Co0.07S1.05, and Fe0.84Co0.11S1.05 catalysts, 6 g of Fe(CO)5 (99%, Acros) and 1.4 g of S (99.98%, Sigma-Aldrich) were uniformly mixed and dissolved in 150 mL of p-xylene (99%, Junsei), followed by addition of 0, 0.04, 0.08, and 0.16 g cobalt acetate (Co(Ac)2) (greater than 98%, Sigma-Aldrich)respectively and stirred for 30 min under N2 atmosphere. After being stirred, the solution was put into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 210 ℃ for 16 h. The precipitates were filtered, washed with acetone, and dried in a vacuum at 80 ℃ to get solid Fe0.95−xCoxS1.05 catalysts.

Before film deposition, NF (1 × 3 cm2) was cleaned via sonication in ethanol and deionized (DI) water for 10 min each. CoMoO4 nanoplates were grown on NF as follows. Co(NO3)2·6H2O (1 mmol) and Na2MoO4·2H2O (1 mmol) were dissolved in DI water (60 mL) under constant stirring to obtain a transparent pink solution. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing a piece of NF, and the reaction was conducted at 150 °C for 6 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the sample deposited on NF was washed with DI water and ethanol several times, followed by during in an air oven at 60 °C to obtain CoMoO4·H2O/NF. The CoMoO4·H2O/NF sample was subsequently calcined at 350 °C for 2 h to obtain CoMoO4/NF.

The as-prepared CoMoO4·H2O/NF was immersed in a growth solution comprising 60 mL of ethanol containing 25 mg of RuCl3. Next, the reaction mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and the reaction was conducted at 90 °C for 8 h. The obtained product was subsequently calcined at 350 °C for 2 h and the sample code is mentioned as CoMoRu0.25Ox/NF indicating that 25 mg of ruthenium chloride was used. For comparison, CoMoRu0.1Ox/NF and CoMoRu0.5Ox/NF named samples were fabricated using CoMoO4/NF and 10 and 50 mg of RuCl3, respectively using the same experimental method. The mass loadings of CoMoO4/NF and CoMoRu0.25Ox/NF were 1.3 and 1.6 mg, respectively.

The synthesis of Ni3S2/NF was carried out according to our previously reported method.  Typically, a piece of NF (1 cm×3 cm) was immersed into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave containing 10 mL of aqueous thiourea solution (1.445 mmol/L). The autoclave was then sealed and kept at 150 °C for 5 h. The material was taken out and washed with ethanol three times and dried in vacuum at room temperature, giving the Ni3S2/NF material as product.

NixCo3−xS4/Ni3S2/NF was synthesized via a facile cation exchange reaction between Ni3S2/NF and Co(II) ions. In a typical synthesis, a piece of Ni3S2/NF was immersed into a mixture solution containing cobaltous acetate tetrahydrate (0.622 g) and ethylene glycol (30 mL) in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The autoclave was then sealed and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. The resulting material was washed with water and ethanol several times and dried at room temperature, giving the NixCo3−xS4/Ni3S2/NF material.

In a typical synthesis, a piece of Ni3S2/NF was immersed into a solution containing cobaltous acetate tetrahydrate (0.622 g) and ethylene glycol (30 mL) in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The autoclave was then sealed and kept at 200 °C for 72 h. The material was taken out and washed with water and ethanol several times and dried at room temperature, giving the NixCo3−xS4/NF material.

A piece of NF (2 × 3 cm2) was washed with 3 M HCl, ethanol, and deionized water several times to ensure the surface of the NF was well cleaned before use. For the preparation of NaHSe solution, 0.118 g Se powder was added into 3 mL deionized water containing 0.130 g NaBH4. After gently stirring for several minutes, a clear NaHSe solution was obtained. The freshly prepared NaHSe solution was added to 30 mL ethanol under N2 flow. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a piece of pretreated NF maintained at 140 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. After the autoclave cooled down slowly at room temperature, the sample was collected and washed with water and ethanol several times and then dried at 60 °C for 4 h. The mass of NiSe catalyst on NF was calculated as follows. The weight increment (x mg) of NF can be directly weighted after the growth of NiSe. NiSeloading = x mg × (MNiSe/MSe) = x mg × (138/79) = 1.747 x mg, where M is the molecular weight or atomic weight. For NiSe/NF electrode, the loading mass of NiSe is about 7.8 mg cm−2.

A piece of NF (1 × 1 cm) was sonicated in acetone, 3.0 M HCl solution, and ethanol sequentially for 5 min each to remove the nickel oxide layer on the surface. The Fe3C nanosheets perpendicular grown on NF substrate was successfully synthesized via simple hydrothermal and calcination methods under the effect of SDBS and urea. A preparation process is as follows: A solution containing FeCl2·4H2O (0.0995 g), CO(NH2)2 (0.0902 g), C6H12O6 (0.0702 g), and SDBS (0.2100 g) was prepared in 30 mL deionized water. After stirring for 5 min, the solution turned green. Subsequently, the solution and NF were transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and the autoclave was sealed and heated at 120 °C for 6 h in an electric oven. After hydrothermal reaction, the NF was taken out from the autoclave, rinsed with distilled water and absolute ethanol to remove residual reactants, and dried in a vacuum oven overnight.

Ni foam (1 cm × 1 cm) was cleaned in the order of isopropanol, absolute ethanol, 0.1 M HCl and deionized water under ultrasound in 20 min, respectively. In a typical synthesis, 0.005 mol Na2WO4·2H2O, 0.01 mol NH2OH·HCl, 0.02 mol CH4N2S and some amount of PEG were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water. The solution was stirred for 1 h under 800 rpm at room temperature. Next, pH value of the solution was adjusted to 6.0 by dropping 0.1 M HCl. The final solution and cleaned Ni foam were put into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave which was kept at 180 °C for 24 h. The procedure adopted for the synthesis of 3D porous WO3/Ni electrode was depicted in Fig. 1. The obtained electrodes were washed using ultrapure water and absolute ethanol, and dried naturally under room temperature. The residual WO3 powder was also washed with the cleaning method for WO3/Ni electrodes. To better describe the electrodes, the precipitate using no additive was named as WO3/Ni, with the addition of PEG were marked as WO3/Ni-X (X = 0.18 and 0.54 g PEG). The as-prepared WO3/Ni-X electrodes and residual WO3 powder were analyzed by the combination of XRD, SEM, HR-TEM and XPS. The detail information was shown in Supporting Information (SI).

Synthesis of TPA/NF (Ni-MOF) electrode: The TPA/NF electrode was fabricated by a simple hydrothermal method. 200 mg of terephthalic acid was dissolved in a mixed solution of DMF, ethanol and water at a certain volume ratio. After ultrasonic dissolution, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave of 50 ml with the treated NF was placed in, which was heated at 125 °C and maintained for 12 h. The Ni-MOF was collected after washing a few times, and finally dried for further use.

Synthesis of MOF-V-Ni3S2/NF: 0.05 M of TAA and 0.004 M of Na3VO4·12H2O were dissolved into 60 ml deionized water, After continuously stirring, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave of 100 mL with TPA/NF in it, which was maintained at 160 °C for 16 h. Finally, the black electrode was washed for several times, and dried in the oven for further characterization.

Synthesis of the V–Ni3S2/NF electrode: 0.05 M of TAA and 0.004 M of Na3VO4 were dissolved in 60 ml deionized water, and it was placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave with the NF. Then, it was kept in an oven at 160 °C for 16 h. Finally, the V–Ni3S2/NF electrode was collected and dried for further characterization.

To prepare CoMoSeS, 16 mg of MoSe2–1, 16 mg of MoS2, 100 mg of CoCl2•6H2O and 500 mg of urea were weighed and mixed together, and 30 mL of ultrapure water was added to the mixture. The mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 20 min. After that, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon‒lined stainless‒steel autoclave and subjected to heating in a muffle furnace at a temperature of 180 °C for 24 h (Scheme 1). After it was cooled to room temperature, the mixture was washed with ultrapure water by centrifugation several times and dried at 40 °C in an oven for 12 h

NiCoMoSe was prepared by hydrothermal method. Specifically, 16 mg of MoSe2–2, 100 mg of NiCl2•6H2O, 100 mg of CoCl2•6H2O, 500 mg of urea and 30 mL of deionized water were mixed and kept under ultrasonic bath for 20 min. The dispersion was transferred into a Teflon‒lined stainless‒steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 24 h (Scheme 1). After cooling to room temperature naturally, the product was washed with ultrapure water several times by centrifugation, and finally dried in an oven at 40 °C for 12 h

Prior to use, a piece of Ni foam with a size of 3.0 cm × 3.0 cm was carefully cleaned by using a 3.0 M HCl solution, deionized water and absolute ethanol for several times. In a typical synthesis, x mmol of MnCl2·4H2O, y mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (x + y = 1.50 mmol, x = 0.10, 0.15, 0.20 mmol), 5.0 mmol of urea and 0.5 mmol of NH4F were dissolved in 25.0 mL distilled water and stirred for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. The above aqueous solution was transferred into a 50.0 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a cleaned Ni foam inside. Then the autoclave was sealed, and then kept at 120 °C for 6 h, and finally allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally. Afterwards, the Ni foam was taken out and washed with water thoroughly. After drying at 60 °C for 8 h, the MnNi precursor was obtained. To obtain Mn-Ni2P, the MnNi precursor and 1.0 g NaH2PO2 were placed at two separate locations in a porcelain boat. Afterward, the samples were heated at 300 °C in Ar atmosphere for 2 h, and then the Mn-Ni2P-X microflowers were harvested, where X is referred to the actual molar ratio of Mn/Ni (X = 0.038, 0.053, or 0.080) measured by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) analysis. For pure Ni2P microflowers, the similar procedure without adding MnCl2·4H2O was employed. The mass loading of Mn-doped Ni2P and Ni2P on such Ni foam was kept at 2.0 mg cm−2.

NF@Co-MOF nanoplate arrays (NF@Co-MOF NP) were prepared according to a previous report . First, 3.0 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 1.0 mmol of trimesic acid were dispersed in equal amounts of N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and ethanol mixture (solution A). Added 0.5 g of PVP to 10 mL of deionized water (DIW) (solution B). Then, solutions A and B were mixed and stirred for 5 min. After that, vertically placed NF (5.0 cm × 1.0 cm×0.1 cm) was submerged in 25 mL of the mixture, and then maintained at 180 °C for 10 h. The product (NF@Co-MOF NP) was washed with DIW several times and dried at 80 °C for further use.

6.0 mmol of thiourea were dispersed in 25 mL of absolute ethanol under ultrasonication, transferred to a stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave in which the NF@Co-MOF NP was held vertically and then treated at 180 °C for 5 h. The product NF@Co3S4/(α,β)-NiS was rinsed with ethanol and DIW in sequence and oven-dried at 80 °C. Replacing Co-MOF with Co(NO3)2·6H2O in NF@Co-MOF NP, NF@NiCoS was prepared by the same method. The above catalysts were synthesized with no binders.

Similar to synthesis of MOF-74-Co/Fe powders, a calculated amount of DOBDC (10 mg), FeCl2·4H2O (22.5 mg) and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (17 mg) were dissolved in the solvent of 10:2:1 (v/v/v) DMF-ethanol--water (13 mL). The resulting solution is magnetically stirred at room temperature, and the solution is completely dissolved and the solution turns dark blue. The treated CP and the resulting solution are hydrothermally reacted for 24 h at 120 ℃. Then the MOF-74-Co/Fe grown on PANI@CP was taken out. And the precipitate was centrifuged, then washed 3 times with ethanol, and dried in an oven at 60℃ for 12 h.

Co(OH)2/CFP was prepared as follows: 5 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 10 mmol HMT were dissolved in 35 mL distilled water under gentle stirring. Then the solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. A clean CFP was then immersed into the autoclave contained solution, sealed and heated at 100 °C for 12 h in an oven and then cooled down to room temperature. The CFP was taken out and rinsed with ethanol and water several times. To prepare the CoSe/CFP, fresh KHSe ethanol solution was prepared according to the previous method. Next, 30 mL KHSe ethanol solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a piece of prepared Co(OH)2/CFP and maintained at 140 °C for 10 h.

In a typical process, a piece of CC was initially cleaned with nitric acid (3.0 M), then washed with ethanol and deionized water several times before being dried at 60 °C overnight. Next, the cleaned CC was seeded with titanium chloride (TiCl4, 0.2 M) at room temperature for 12 h before it was dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Then, the seeded CC was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave, which was filled with a solution containing hydrochloric acid (37 mL), deionized water (37 mL), and titanium (IV) butoxide (0.75 mL) for a hydrothermal reaction at 160 °C for 14 h. After the reaction finished, the 1D TiO2 nanoarrays on CC sample was washed with DI water for 3 times and well dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The 1D TiO2 nanoarrays on CC was annealed at 900 °C under flow of NH3 (100 sccm) and Ar (100 sccm) for 2 h to convert TiO2 to TiN.

The CeFeCo(OH)x/NF was fabricated by an uncomplicated hydrothermal method. A commercial nickel foam (NF) was employed as the substrate. Firstly, the NF (10 ​mm ​× ​10 ​mm) was washed in 2 ​M HCl solution for 15 ​min. It can help remove the NiOx surface layer, subsequently washed with water and ethanol, then dried at 60 ​°C overnight. The NF treated in this way can be used in experiments. In addition, 5 ​mmol CO(NH2)2, 2 ​mmol CoCl2·6H2O, 2 ​mmol NH4F, 1 ​mmol FeSO4·7H2O, 0.5 ​mmol Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 35 ​mL water were mixed. The resultant mixture was stirred for 10 ​min and turned out into a uniform solution. Later, the solution was transferred to a 50 ​mL Teflon-lined autoclave and put NF into the solution, setting the temperature to 120 ​°C and keeping it for 7 ​h. Then most important thing is natural cooling to ambient temperature. In addition, in order to be able to obtain the product of the catalyst, the sample was removed and rinsed with deionized water and ethanol. Eventually, the samples were dried in a vacuum oven to obtain CeFeCo(OH)x/NF. Similarly, FeCo(OH)x/NF, CeFe(OH)x/NF and CeCo(OH)x/NF were synthesized under identical conditions.

Fifty mg of the resultant Co-Fe PBA was dispersed in 12 mL of ethanol under ultrasonication for half an hour to obtain a uniform solution. Then, 6 mL of Na2S solution (10 mg/mL) was dropped into the above suspension under magnetic stirring for a few minutes, followed by the addition of 4 mL of thioacetamide (0.1875 mM, ethanol) and 4 mL of Na2MoO4·2H2O (0.0625 mM, water) under stirring for 10 min for each solution. Afterward, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL) and kept at 120 °C for 6 h; the temperature was then increased to 200 °C and kept for another 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained black products were washed with DI water and ethanol several times and then dried at 60 °C overnight. Finally, they were annealed at 350 °C for 2 h under a N2 atmosphere to obtain a highly crystalline Co3S4@MoS2 heterostructure. The heating rate was 1 °C min−1.

Furthermore, pure Co3S4 and MoS2 were also prepared for comparison and to highlight the structural advantages of the Co3S4@MoS2 heterostructure. Co3S4 nanoboxes were synthesized as follows: 6 mL of Na2S (10 mg/mL) was added to the 12 mL of ethanolic solution that contained 50 mg of Co-Fe PBA particles under stirring; the mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (25 mL) and kept at 120 °C for 6 h. The Co3S4 sample was finally obtained by the same process of centrifugation, drying, and annealing. MoS2 nanosheets were prepared by mixing 20 mL of thioacetamide (0.1875 mM, ethanol) with 20 mL of Na2MoO4·2H2O (0.0625 mM, water) under stirring for 30 min; the mixture solution was then transferred to an autoclave (50 mL) and kept at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitates were washed with DI water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C overnight. Finally, the dried powder was heated at 350 °C for 2 h under a N2 atmosphere to obtain MoS2.

For a typical synthesis, 1.7 mmol of Na2MoO4·2H2O and 1.7 mmol of CoCl2·6H2O were added into 30 mL of distilled water under ultrasonication for 10 min. The obtained mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-stainless steel autoclave (50 mL) and heated for 12 h at 150 °C. The purple powder was collected by centrifugation, washed with distilled water and ethanol several times to obtain a powder of CoMoO4 nanorods after drying at 60 °C in a drying oven overnight. For the synthesis of MoO3 nanorods, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O powder (700 mg) dissolved in a mixture solution (30 mL) consisting of HNO3 (65%, 6 mL) and deionized H2O (30 mL) was transferred into a Teflon-stainless steel autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 20 h. Finally, the achieved white powder was collected by washing with distilled water and ethanol several times and then dried at 60 °C overnight.

For the synthesis of the CoMoO4@OA sample, the OA (C18H37N) was used to coat and insert into the nanostructure of CoMoO4. Typically, 200 mg of CoMoO4 nanorods were dispersed in ethanol (30 mL) solution under sonication for 30 min to form a suspension solution, then a certain volume of OA (200, 400, and 600 μL) was dropped into this suspension solution. After continuous stirring for another 30 min, the obtained mixture solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 100 °C for 12 h. The purple precipitation (CoMoO4@OA) was collected after washing with ethanol several times. The CoMoO4@OA samples with adding different volumes of OA synthesized by the similar procedure, and expressed as CoMoO4@OA200, CoMoO4@OA400, and CoMoO4@OA600, respectively. MoO3/OA sample with the additional OA (400 μL) was achieved through the above similar process.

The pre-treated titanium plate was placed in the Teflon container filled with 50 mL of 1 mol L−1 NaOH solution, which was then positioned in a stainless-steel autoclave. TiO2 nanowire arrays were produced by placing the autoclave in a preheated oven at 200 °C for 24 h. After washed with ethanol, the samples with TNW arrays were immersed in 0.5 mol L−1 HCl solution for 12 h to make the complete ion exchange between Na+ and H+. Finally, the samples were calcined at 500 °C for 2 h in a sintering furnace.

Fe-Ni-OH samples were synthesized with a facile hydrothermal synthesis method . In a typical synthesis experiment, designated amounts of Ni and Fe precursors together with 5 mmol urea were firstly dissolved in 40 ml DI water. The solution was stirred at room temperature for 1 h before being transferred to a 45 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and kept for 12 h at 90 °C for reaction, after which it was allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally. The product precipitate was collected and washed with DI water and ethanol five times and then dried at 80 °C for 6 h. The molar ratio of the two metal precursors, with a total molar amount of 1 mmol being kept, was controlled to synthesize Fe-Ni-OH samples with different compositions. Specifically, five Fe-Ni-OH samples with variant compositions, namely Ni(OH)2, Fe1Ni9(OH)x, Fe2Ni8(OH)x, Fe3Ni7(OH)x, and Fe5Ni5(OH)x, were obtained.

NiOF was solvothermally synthesized using a mixture containing deionized (DI) water (20 ml), 1,4-bezenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC, 0.33 g), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 10 ml), and nickel(II) chloride hexahydrate (0.48 g) . The mixture was poured into a stainless steel autoclave with Teflon-lined vessel, and then tightly sealed with a cap. The autoclave was heated to 180 °C by a programmable electric oven under a constant rate of 10 °C min−1, and then kept at that temperature for 12 h. Subsequently, the autoclave device was naturally cooled down to ambient temperature. The NiOF (Ni-BDC) powder was collected by high-speed centrifugation, and then dried at 70 °C for 10 h in a hot convective oven. Nickel/nickel oxide nanoparticles were obtained by calcining the NiBDC powder at temperatures above 400 °C in air for 2 h. Nickel/nickel oxide nanoparticles were coated onto commercial nickel foam (2 cm × 2 cm) with 1.7 mm thick, 110 pores per inch, and 380 g m−2 in areal density by electrophoretic deposition (EPD) method. EPD bath was composed of nickel/nickel oxide nanoparticle (50 mg), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 50 ml), and nickel nitrate hexahydrate (0.6 × 10−3 mol l−1) . In EPD process, a potential difference of −50 V was applied between the nickel foam and platinum (2 × 2 cm2) sheets with a separation of about 1 cm for 30 s employing a source meter (Keithley 2400). After EPD, nickel foam with attached nickel/nickel oxide nanoparticles was heated to 150 °C for 1 h in a hot oven. For comparison, unmodified nickel foam electrode was also subjected to heat treatment at 150 °C in air for 1 h before electrochemical measurements. The mass loading of nickel/nickel oxide nanoparticles was weighted to be approximately 1.50 mg.

All the chemicals used in this experiment were analytical grade with the high purity (99.99 %). Hydrothermal approach was used to prepare the nanosheet-shaped ZIF-67 nanomaterials. In a general procedure, 1 g of Co (NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 60 ml of a mixed solution of distilled (DI) water, ethanol, and N,N-dimethylformamide, where was added 0.5 g of 2-Methylimidazole. After stirring for 1 h, the reaction mixture was transferred to a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and it was kept at 160 °C for 12 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, purple precipitate was collected and washed multiple times with copious amounts of water and ethanol to eliminate any organic residuals. Finally, the filtered powder was dried overnight at 80 °C prior to further analysis.

α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles were directly grown onto carbon paper following the reported procedure . In a typical synthesis, 0.15M FeCl3 and 1M NaNO3 were dissolved in 60 mL distilled water in 100 mL Teflon lined vessel, concentrated HCl was added to maintain the pH at 1.5 under continuous stirring. Different atomic weight % of NH4VO3 was added to the above solution for different doping concentrations of vanadium and hydrothermally treated at 160 °C for 6 h in the presence of carbon paper.

The NDCDs (0.012 g) were sonicated for 1 h in 60 mL of DD water to disperse it. Further, Nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 0.2818 g), Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (0.1939 g) and NH4F (0.0148 g) were added to the dispersed solution. Then, the solution was stirred vigorously to make a clear solution. Eventually, a specific amount of Na2CO3 (1 M) and NaOH (1 M) were added to that stirring solution until it reached a pH of 10. The solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 120 °C for 15 h before cooling to room temperature. The final product was extracted by centrifugation and then washed many times with water and ethanol. The synthesized composite was denoted as the NiFe-LDH@NDCDs. For comparative study, similar procedure of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was followed for synthesizing NiFe-LDH without adding NDCDs.

1.50 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 1.50 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.90 g of NTA and 20 mL isopropyl alcohol (C3H6O) were mixed into 40 mL distilled water and stirred for 30 min into homogenous solution. Next, the solution was put into the Teflon tube. The Teflon tube was sealed in stainless steel and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling the stainless steel down to room temperature, the precipitant was washed three times with mixture of distilled water and ethyl alcohol, and subsequently dried under vacuum at 60 °C. Then, pure CoNi-NTA-3 was got. Co-NTA-1, CoNi-NTA-2, CoNi-NTA-4, and Ni-NTA-5 were synthesized in the same way but the mass ratios were different (Co:Ni = 3:0, 2:1, 1:2, and 0:3).

The Co-precursor on NF was manufactured by means of an improved hydrothermal . Firstly, 0.2910 g (1 mmol) Co(NO3)2‧6H2O, 0.2222 g (6 mmol) NH4F and 0.6006 g (10 mmol) urea were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water, and then stirred vigorously for ∼ 30 min. Then, the above solution and the pretreated NF were transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 120 ℃ for 6 h. After the reaction, the obtained products were washed by deionized water several times and then dried at 60 ℃ in vacuum for several hours.

For this process, 1 mM La(NO3)3·6H2O, 1 mM Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 394 mg Glycine were added into a 50 mL deionized water and stirred for 10 min. Next, slowly add ammonium hydroxide solution to pH ≈ 8, and continue stirring for 10 min. The mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL) and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h in the blast drying oven. The solid powder was washed, centrifuged, and then dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The precursor was ground and annealed for 2 h at 650 °C in muffle furnace at 5 °C min−1 to obtain LaCoO3.

pieces of Nickel Foams (NFs) were disposed with 3 M HCl, absolute ethylalcohol and deionized water (H2O) for 10 min, respectively, afterwards was used after drying at 60℃. Then, 145 mg Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 202 mg of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 600 mg CH4N2O and 148 mg NH4F was dissolved in 30 mL H2O. The dissolved brown solution was transferred to a 50 mL high-pressure the Teflon liner stainless steel autoclave, two pieces of NFs with an area of 1*2 cm2 were added, and kept at 120℃ for 6 h.

Ruthenium chloride (0.01 M) dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water was mixed slowly with 0.1 M citric acid solution under constant stirring. This mixture was then kept in a pressurized hydrothermal autoclave at 180 °C for 24 h . The autoclave was cooled to atmospheric conditions, and the precipitate was filtered and washed with double-distilled water and an organic solvent. The collected black-colored sample was dried overnight at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. The prepared P-doped rGO (90 mg) was then mixed with the prepared RuO2 (10 mg) nanoparticles (10 wt%) in 10 mL of ethanol and sonicated for 5 h. Finally, the prepared electrocatalyst was washed with ethanol and distilled water, and it was placed in an oven at 80 °C overnight.

Cobalt hydroxide carbonate (CoHC) nanoneedle arrays are grown on a copper foam substrate by hydrothermal method in the presence of F− following the method reported by Jiang et al. In a typical experiment, CoCl2⋅6H2O 2.86 g (12 mmol), NH4F (0.89 g, 24 mmol), CO(NH2)2 3.60 g (60 mmol) is dissolved in 190 mL deionized water to form a pink solution. 18.5 mL above solution and a 1 cm × 2 cm copper foam is loaded into a 50 mL Teflon lined autoclave. The autoclave is heated to 120 °C at 5 °C⋅min−1, maintained at that temperature for 5 h and allowed to cool down to room temperature. The copper foam is covered by a layer of pink solid, which is collected and washed with deionized water for 3 times and dried by hot air. The sample is designated as CoHC/CF.

The preparation procedure for CeO2 modified Co3O4 nanoneedle arrays on Cu foil (CeO2–Co3O4/CF) is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1. First, cobalt hydroxide carbonate (CoHC) nanoneedles are direct grown on Cu foil by a hydrothermal method. Then cerium hydroxide Ce(OH)3 is deposited on to the surface of the CoHC needles by an electrochemical precipitation method. The Ce(OH)3–CoHC/CF sample is then calcinated in air to give the CeO2–Co3O4/CF product, which remains an intact electrode and is readily available for electrochemical tests.

In an innovatively experimental method, 200 mg of Na2SeO3 and 1g CTAB were dissolved in 40 mL EDA under constant stirring for 20 minutes to dissolve uniformly, then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon kettle and followed by 120 ℃ for 3 hours. Once cooled down to room temperature, add 0.8 M CoCl2·6H2O to the homogeneous solution. Further treatment at 190 ℃ for 8 hours and cooling down to room temperature, the black powder was obtained by centrifugation and washed five times with ethanol and deionized water. The CoSe2 was dried at 60 ℃ for 8 hours in vacuum oven for characterizations.

Typically, to prepare NiSe nanorods on NF, Selenium powder (1 mmol) and NaBH4 (2 mmol) were dissolved in 25 mL diluted ammonia solution (20 mL deionized water and 5 mL ammonia solution) and stirred for 10 min to get homogeneous solution. The prepared solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave together with three pieces of pre-treated NF which served as nickel source. The autoclave was then heated to 120 °C for 6 h and naturally cooled to room temperature in an electric oven. After the hydrothermal reaction, the obtained NF was ultrasonically cleaned with deionized water five times and dried at 50 °C in a vacuum dryer. The as-grown materials on NF are NiSe nanorods

NiMoO4·xH2O/NF was synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Frist, nickel foam pieces (2 × 4 cm2) were treated in 5 % HNO3 to remove the oxide layer, rinsed with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried under N2 gas blowing. The cleaned NF was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave containing 1 mM of NaMoO4 and 1 mM Ni(NO3)2 in 25 mL deionized water. The enclosed autoclave was then heated at 160 °C for 5 h in an electric oven. The obtained product was rinsed with distilled water and ethanol sequentially and dried in air at 60 °C.

In a typical synthesis, the NiFe layered bimetallic hydroxides were synthesis by a hydrothermal method. Firstly, 1 mmol of NiCl2·6H2O was dispersed in 120 mL ultra-pure water, stirred to dissolve it, and then added 1.2 g of urea and 440 mg of NH4F respectively, stirred for 30 min. And finally added 1 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O and stirred for 30 min to dissolve it. Then mixed solution was dispersed in the Teflon-lined stainless autoclave of two 100 mL, 3 × 4 cm2 hydrophilic carbon paper was put into it, maintaining 120 °C for 12 h after sealing, and then the product was distilled with water and ethanol, respectively. Finally, all samples were dried at 60 °C in vacuum oven for 10 h.

The Ni(OH)2 Nanosheet arrays was fabricated by dissolving 4.45 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 8 mmol NH4F, 20 mmol urea in 80 mL deionized water. After stirring for several minutes, the transparent green solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and a piece of NF (Nickel Foam) was put into the solution. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 120 °C for 6 h and then the autoclave was taken out and cooled down to room temperature. And the NF with Ni(OH)2 Nanosheet arrays was taken out and washed with deionized water and alcohol for several times and dried in air at room temperature.

We choose a hydrothermal method to sulfurize Ni(OH)2 nanosheet arrays. Typically, 0.2 mol/L Na2S·9H2O water solution and a piece of NF with Ni(OH)2 was immersed into the solution and maintained at 100 °C for 8 h. And then the autoclave was taken out and cooled down to room temperature. And the NF with Ni3S2 Nanosheet arrays was taken out and washed with deionized water and alcohol for several times and dried in air at room temperature.

A piece of commercial NF (3 × 2 cm2) was respectively sonicated in 3 M HCl, deionized (DI) water, and ethanol for 20, 5, and 20 min to remove the nickel oxides and organic contaminants on the surface and subsequently left to dry. The NiMoO4 nanorods were synthesized on NF by a facile hydrothermal method. Typically, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O were dissolved in 50 mL DI water under stirring with their final concentrations as 40 and 10 mM, respectively. The resulting solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing a piece of NF (3 × 2 cm2). After a hydrothermal reaction at 150 ℃ for 6 h, NiMoO4 nanorods were vertically grown on the NF surface. The NiMoO4/NF catalyst electrode was obtained after being washed with ultrapure water and ethanol and dried at 50 ℃ overnight.

The modification of NiMoO4/NF with sulfur (S-NiMoO4/NF) was conducted by a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 150 mg of thioacetamide (C2H5NS) were dissolved in 35 mL of anhydrous ethanol in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Then a piece of NiMoO4/NF was placed in the anhydrous ethanol solution. The autoclave was then heated to 150 ℃ for 5 h. The S-NiMoO4/NF product was collected after cooling, washing, and drying at 50 ℃ overnight.

After electrochemical deposition of Ni–Fe nanosheets, molybdenum was doped into the nanosheets structure using the hydrothermal method shown schematically in Fig. 1. For the purpose of doping the molybdenum in the structure, 3 mM Na2MoO4.6H2O was dissolved in 60 mL distilled water. The Ni–Fe sample was immersed in the solution and then the solution was embedded in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and incubated for 6 h at 100 °C to form Mo-doped Ni–Fe nanosheets. The samples were then rinsed with distilled water and prepared for further testing.

nNFCS NTs/NBs were transformed from the as-prepared NFCS NTs/NBs through a reduction process under hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) steam. A 5 mL beaker with an SSM@NFCS NTs/NBs inside was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel high-pressure reactor containing 250 μL N2H4·H2O, and maintained at 100 °C for 12 h. The obtained samples were directly dried in vacuum at room temperature for further characterizations. The mass loading is estimated to be 0.868 mg. Similarly, nFS NTs, nNCS NSs and nSSM also converted from the as-deposited FS NTs, NCS NSs and bare SSM by the same modification method.

NiCoFeS/NF was prepared through a solvothermal process. 1 g thiourea was dissolved in 60 mL ethanol (degassed in N2 in advance) to form a homogeneous solution. The solution was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) with three pieces of the prepared CoFe/NF. The autoclave was maintained at 140 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained NiCoFeS/NF was rinsed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, followed by drying at 60 °C for 12 h in vacuum.

In a typical procedure, 0.239 g of Ni(Ac)2·4H2O and 0.086 g of FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in the mixture of 1 mL of water and 24 mL of absolute ethanol in a round bottomed flask, and then a freshly-prepared PANI on CP was immersed into above solution, followed by the addition of 1 mL of NH3·H2O under a mild stirring. The dispersion was refluxed under continuous stirring at 80 °C for 20 h. After that, the dispersion was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and treated at 150 °C for 3 h. After the reactor cooling down naturally, the NiFe LDH-PANI sample was taken out and washed with deionized water and ethanol.

The NF was sonicated successively with 0.5 M H2SO4, deionized water and ethanol for 15 min to remove impurities. In a typical synthesis, Cr(NO3)3⋅9H2O (2.5 mmol) and CO(NH2)2 (25 mmol) were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water and stirred for 15 min to obtain a clear solution. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave. The pretreated NF (3 cm × 3 cm) was placed obliquely in the autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the obtained product was washed with deionized water for several times and dried at room temperature for further use. In order to optimize the influence of hydrothermal time, CrOOH-10/NF, CrOOH-14/NF were also prepared under the same conditions but different processing times (10 h and 14 h).

Preparation of NiCo2O4/Ti catalysts: In a typical process, a piece of Ti mesh was subjected to ultrasonication in aqueous HCl (2.0 M) for 15 min to remove the surface oxide layer. Subsequently, it was consecutively sonicated in ethanol and deionized water for 15 min each. In a separate container, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1.46 g, 5 mmol), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (2.91 g, 10 mmol), and CH3COONa (2.46 g, 30 mmol) were dissolved in PEG-200 (30 mL). The homogeneous solution was stirred vigorously for 30 min and transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL capacity). Subsequently, the pre-cleaned Ti mesh was submerged in the solution. The autoclave was sealed and heated to 200 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained Ni–Co precursor/Ti was copiously washed with deionized water and ethanol and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the samples were calcined on air at 350 °C for 2 h (heating rate: 2 °C min−1) to furnish the NiCo2O4/Ti product with an average catalyst mass loading on Ti mesh of approximately 1.5 mg cm−2. Additionally, n-NiCo2O4/Ti (n = 1, 3, 5, and 7 mmol) electrodes were synthesized employing different NiCo2O4 loadings under otherwise identical conditions. The effects of temperature and solvent on morphology were also investigated.

The NiCoSe2 nanoparticles were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 0.5 mmol NiCl2·6H2O, 0.5 mmol CoCl2·6H2O, 1 mmol SeO2 were dissolved into 25 mL deionized water to form a homogenous solution, and then 5 mL hydrazine hydrate was injected into the above solution slowly via a pipette. After stirring for 20 min, a piece of Ni foam (1 × 2 cm2) was put into the solution, and next the solution was sealed and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave immediately, and kept at 150 °C for 1 h. After cooling down naturally, the black precipitate was collected and washed with deionized water and ethanol absolute several times, and then dried at 60 °C for 4 h.

In a hydrothermal method, the double hydroxide nanosheet arrays were synthesized in one-step. Firstly, nickel, iron and chromium precursors with the total amount of mole at 1.0 mmol while having different ratios of 1:0:0, 0.66:0.34:0, 0.66:0.27:0.07, 0.66:0.17:0.17, 0.66:0.07:0.27 and 0.66:0:0.34, were ultrasonically dissolved in pure water for 10 min. In the meanwhile, NF was carefully cleaned before use, it was immersed in the solutions followed the order of HCl, ethanol and pure water, in an ultrasonic bath to eliminate impurities. The solution containing reactants (36 mL) and NF with exposed nickel atoms were transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene lining. The reaction was sustained for 12 h in the stainless-steel autoclave at 120 °C. It was rinsed with ethanol and water for several times before being put to dry in vacuum oven at 60 °C for 8 h.

The method of synthesis Co-MOF was the same as V41.7wt%-Co-MOF, just without adding NH4VO3. 9 mL of DMF with Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.22 mmol) was added dropwise to 15 mL of DMF and 6 mL of EG with NH2-BDC (0.22 mmol), mixed up under continuous magnetic stirring (750 rpm) for 30 min at room temperature. After that, the above mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 140°C for 6 h. Then, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed with DMF and ethanol three times and dried at 40°C for 6 h.

To get V41.7wt%-Co-MOF, firstly, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.12 mmol) and NH4VO3 (0.10 mmol) were dissolved in 9 mL of DMF with vigorous ultrasound to form solution A, and NH2-BDC (0.22 mmol) was dissolved in 15 mL of DMF and 6 mL of EG in a 50 mL beaker and dispersed under vigorous ultrasound to form solution B. Subsequently, the solution A was added drop-wise to solution B, mixed up under continuous magnetic stirring (750 rpm) for 30 min at room temperature. After that, the above mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 140°C for 6 h. Then, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed with DMF and ethanol three times and dried at 40°C for 6 h.

5.0 mg of N-G was dispersed in 30 mL of DI water under stirring to form a uniform suspension, to which 20 μL of IrCl3·3H2O solution (25 mg mL−1) was subsequently added. The solution was stirred at room temperature for 12 h and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 12 h. The resulting product (denoted as N-G-Ir) was collected and washed with DI water for three times, and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. Subsequently, the N-G-Ir powder was placed in a tube furnace and annealed in Ar atmosphere at 600 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1, followed by natural cooling to the room temperature. As-prepared sample was denoted as Ir@N-G-600. Different annealing temperatures (400 and 800 °C) were also carried out to optimize the electrochemical performance and the corresponding samples were denoted as Ir@N-G-400 and Ir@N-G-800, respectively. For comparison, Ir@G-600 sample was also synthesized by a similar procedure to Ir@N-G-600, except for that the pristine graphene was directly used without N2 plasma treatment.

Trace-Ru-doped nickel sulfide electrocatalyst, Ru-Ni9S8, was generated by one-step hydrothermal procedure. Before the reaction, the Ni foam (1 cm × 3 cm) was ultrasonicated in acetone, ethanol, 3 M HCl solution, and deionized water successively to slough off surface impurities. Na2S2O3.5 H2O (75 mg) and RuCl3·xH2O (7 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and stirred continuously to obtain a homogeneous solution. A piece of pretreated Ni foam was placed in this homogeneous solution and transferred to a 50 mL Teﬂon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was heated at 140 °C for 6 h. When the reaction products are cooled to room temperature, the black nickel foam was washed for several times with distilled water and ethanol. The black nickel foam was dried in vacuum at 60 ℃ about 12 h and named as the Ru-Ni9S8. The vs-Ru-Ni9S8 with rich sulfur vacancies was synthesized in a similar way, except that the reaction temperature is 100 °C.

The precursors of the CuxCo1-x(OH)F NAs/CP (x = 0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.08, and 0.11) were prepared as follows. Firstly, the carbon paper was carefully pretreated with diluted HNO3, ethanol and deionized water to remove the surface impurities, and then dried in electric vacuum drying oven before use. Then, Co(NO3)2·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were weighed in the stoichiometric amounts, keeping the total molar mass 2 mmol. Subsequently, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 4 mmol NH4F, and 8 mmol urea were dissolved in 40 ml of distilled water. After continuously stirring for 30 min, the as-obtained homogeneous solution was then transferred into a 50 ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. A piece of cleaned CP (2 × 2.5 cm2) was immersed in the solution, sealed, and maintained at 120 °C for 24 h. After the autoclave cooled down naturally at room temperature, the obtained CuxCo1-x(OH)F NAs/CP were collected and washed with ethanol and deionized water for several times, and then dried at 60 °C for further use. The mass loading was about 3.95 mg cm−2.

Cabon paper (CP) was soaked in concentrated nitric acid (HNO3) overnight to obtain the hydrophilic CP, followed by washing with water, ethanol and water in sequence for 15 min in an ultrasonic cleaner, and stored in deionized water. 0.9 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.1 mmol Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, 4 mmol NH4F, and 5 mmol urea were added to 15 mL H2O in order under continuous magnetic agitation to produce a mixed pink solution. Subsequently, the above solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with a piece of cleaned CP (3 × 2 cm2), and kept at 120 °C for 6 h. Finally, the Cr-Co(CO3)0.5(OH)/CP precursor was prepared after CP was cooled to ambient temperature, washed by deionized water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The total mole of metal ions was controlled at 1 mmol and the ratio of Cr to Co was changed from 0 mmol to 0.2 mmol to synthesize Cr-Co(CO3)0.5(OH) with different content of Cr. When the metal ions of Cr is 0 mmol, Co(CO3)0.5(OH)-F was synthesized.

rGO@Fe–NiCH was firstly fabricated by a hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.73 g NiCl2·6H2O and 0.27 g FeCl3·6H2O were added to a 30 mL GO solution (2 mg/mL), and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Then, 0.50 g polyethylene glycol and 0.50 g ammonium bicarbonate, used as a surfactant and precipitant, respectively, were added to the solution. The resultant solution was poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was then transferred to an oven and heated at 120 °C for 12 h. Then, the product was centrifuged and washed several times with distilled water. The as-prepared rGO@Fe–NiCH was dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. For the synthesis of rGO@Fe–Ni2P, the obtained rGO@Fe–NiCH (50 mg) was deposited into the opening of a quartz cube with NaH2PO2 (500 mg) in the bottom of the quartz cube. Then, the cube was treated in a flowing argon atmosphere at 300 °C for 2 h with a fast ramping rate of 20 °C min−1. After cooling to room temperature, rGO@Fe–Ni2P was prepared successfully. For comparison, rGO@Fe–Ni2P samples with various doping contents of iron were obtained using different amounts of ferric chloride (0, 0.27, 0.54, and 1.0 g). The pure rGO@NiCH was fabricated using the same procedure as for rGO@Fe–NiCH without the addition of ferric chloride. The fabricating procedure is illustrated in Scheme 1.

A certain portion of PBA/CNT-A and thioacetamide were ultrasonicated (20 min) in a mixed solution of 64 mL of ethylene glycol and DI water (7:1 ratio) in order to obtain the homogeneously dispersed suspension. The resulting suspension was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (80 mL) and subjected to hydrothermal reaction (160 °C, 8 h). After the reaction vessel cooled down naturally, the solid black precipitate obtained was washed several times with DI water and absolute ethanol and subsequently died over freeze-dryer for overnight. The as-obtained powder was denoted as NiS/CNT-A and the other NiS/CNT-B and NiS/CNT-C samples were prepared in the same method, just by using PBA/CNT-B and PBA/CNT-C precursors, respectively. The corresponding reaction conditions and parameters of all the electrocatalysts fabricated in this study are shown in Tab. S1.

For the Co doped NiFe LDHs, in a typical process, first, 3.0 mmol urea, 2.5 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 1.0 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 0.5 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O were added in a beaker with 15 mL deionized water and then stirred 2 h. Next, the solution was put into 20 mL a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to heat at 120 °C for 6 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the product was isolated by centrifugation and washed by deionized water and ethanol several times, and finally dried at 60 °C in drying oven and then got Co-NiFe LDHs (0.5:2.5:1.0).

The transition metal doped NiFe LDHs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. In a typical process, first, 3.0 mmol urea, 0.1 mmol Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, 3.0 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.9 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O were added in a beaker with 15 mL deionized water and then stirred 2 h. Next, the solution was put into 20 mL a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to heat at 120 °C for 6 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the product was isolated by centrifugation and washed by deionized water and ethanol several times, and finally dried at 60 °C in drying oven. At last, the Cr doped NiFe LDHs with the mole ratio of Cr:Ni:Fe of 0.1:3.0:0.9 was obtained, which was named as Cr-NiFe LDHs (0.1:3.0:0.9).

For the Mn doped NiFe LDHs, in a typical process, first, 3.0 mmol urea, 0.5 mmol MnCl2, 2.5 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 1.0 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O were added in a beaker with 15 mL deionized water and then stirred 2 h. Next, the solution was put into 20 mL a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to heat at 120 °C for 6 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the product was isolated by centrifugation and washed by deionized water and ethanol several times, and finally dried at 60 °C in drying oven and then got Mn-NiFe LDHs (0.5:2.5:1.0).

Water-dispersible Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized according to a previous report . Briefly, a 20 mL aqueous solution of 1 mM L-ascorbic acid was added into a 60 mL aqueous mixture of 6 mM FeCl3·6H2O and 18 mM NaHCO3, under stirring for 20 min. The mixture was transferred to a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was kept at 150 °C for 6 h. The product was separated using a magnet, washed with ultrapure water more than three times, leading to Fe3O4 nanoparticles that could be re-dispersed in water for further using.

NiFe LDH/NF was synthesized on Ni foam by hydrothermal method. Cleaned Ni foam was immersed into solution containing 2 mmol Ni(NO3)2·9H2O, 2 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 10 mmol urea, and 4 mmol NH4F, then transferred the mixture into a 50 ml stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooled to room temperature, the sample was taken out and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, then dried at 60 °C to obtain NiFe LDH on Ni foam (NiFe LDH/NF).

NiFeZn LDH was synthesized on Ni foam by hydrothermal method as above, except the solution in autoclave was 2 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 2 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 1 mmol Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 30 mmol urea, and 6 mmol NH4F. The obtained sample was named as NiFeZn LDH/NF(1) in this work. In comparison, 2 mmol of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was added into solution instead that of NiFeZn LDH/NF(1), and the obtained sample was named as NiFeZn LDH/NF(2).

Firstly, Ni foam with the size of 2 cm × 4 cm was immersed in CH3COOH solution (2 mol L-1) under ultrasonic for 10 min to clear away the surface nickel oxide layer as fully as possible, followed by ultrasonic cleaning with deionized water and absolute ethanol each for 10 min in sequence, and then dried in air naturally. Subsequently, the cleaned NF and 40 mL of mixed solution involving FeCl3·6H2O (0.5 mmol), CO(NH2)2 (5 mmol) and NH4F (2 mmol) after sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave were treated with a hydrothermal process at 120 °C for 6 h, then settled in air to cool naturally. The resulted NF was rinsed with deionized water and absolute ethanol for several times to obtain oxygen vacancy-abundant Fe2+-contained NiFe-LDH growing on the NF. After drying in air naturally, the thus-prepared Fe2+-NiFe-LDH@NF was implemented with electrooxidation at the potential of 1.6 V versus reversible hydrogen electrode (vs. RHE) for 6 h in KOH solution (1 mol L-1) to finally obtain the resultant sample of Fe2+-NiFe-LDH-EO6 h@NF. In addition, conventional NiFe-LDH@NF sample without further electrooxidation was synthesized through the same hydrothermal routine except for replacing FeCl3·6H2O with Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (0.5 mmol) and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.5 mmol) as well as changing the reaction time of 12 h, and also the cleaned Ni foam was used as the control sample of NF.

The Zr(OH)4 hexagonal sheets were fashioned using the hydrothermal technique. In this procedure, 0.05 M solution of zirconyl chloride (ZrOCl2·8H2O) was prepared by dissolving in 100 mL deionized water (DI) at room temperature, and then 10.0 mL of 2.0 M ammonium hydroxide solution was mixed while vigorous stirring to make a clear solution. After continuous agitating the obtained mixture was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, sealed. The sealed autoclave was treated hydrothermally at 150 °C for 24 h. After that, the resultant precipitates were collected by employing centrifugation and, finally washed thoroughly with DI water which was finally dried at 70 °C.

Anion exchange transitions were employed to convert the hexagonal Zr(OH)4 into porous zirconium telluride. For this purpose, 0.120 g of pre-synthesized Zr(OH)4 were stirred in 50 mL of deionized water, followed by the further addition of 0.0765 g of Te powder and 0.0420 g of NaBH4 in the reaction mixture. The final solution was poured in a 100 mL Teflon covered with a stainless-steel autoclave. After that, the autoclave was sealed and stored for 12 h in an oven at 180 °C for hydrothermal treatment. To get the resultant powders, the reaction products were centrifuged, rinsed thoroughly with DI water and ethanol, and finally dried at 70 °C. The proposed experimental mechanism for Zr reduction and Zr-Te formation at the interface is described as:(1)Zr(OH)4+Te→ZrTe+H2O

For the activation of carbon fabric, deionized water was used for cleaning purpose, then treated with acetone, nitric acid (10.0 wt%), and sulfuric acid (10.0 wt%). Afterward, 50 mg of the synthesized product was mixed in 25.0 mL of deionized water before forming ZrTe/CC. Next, the activated carbon fabric was dipped in the previously prepared solution and was agitated for 10.0 min at room temperature. The resultant material was then poured into a Teflon-wrapped autoclave with a capacity of 100 mL and then placed in the oven for 12 h at 140 °C. After hydrothermal treatment, the autoclave was cooled at room temperature. Finally, the resultant product was thoroughly washed with DI water and let it to air dry naturally. Before and after hydrothermal treatment, the loaded ZrTe on CC was weighed.

The Ni precursor of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets array on Ni foam was synthesized through a hydrothermal method. A piece of Ni foam (2 × 3 cm2) was immersed in a HCl solution (3 M) for 3 min and cleaned subsequently with DI water several times. Meanwhile, 5 mM Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O and 10 mM HMT were successively dissolved in 36 mL DI water to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution together with the pretreated Ni foam were transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 110 °C for 10 h. After cooling down, the sample was taken out, washed with DI water and dried at room temperature to obtain the Ni(OH)2 NS/NF.

The TiRu nanorod array supported on Ti mesh (TiRu/Ti) was synthesized through a hydrothermal deposition and subsequent thermal annealing process reported before with some modification . Primarily, Ti mesh was pretreated by ultrasonic treatment in acetone and DI water for 30 min, then soaked into 50% HCl solution at 85 °C for 15 min, and rinsed with DI water. Then, 5 mM RuCl3·xH2O was dissolved in 60 mL DI water containing 6.77 mL of concentrated HCl to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution together with the pretreated Ti mesh were transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling down, the precursor was washed with DI water, dried at room temperature, and heated at 450 °C for 2 h in a tube furnace.

We also tested corresponding electrochemical performances of commercial Pt/C and RuO2 catalysts according to the loading masses of Ni2P NS/NF (4.5 mg cm−2) and TiRu/Ti (1.0 mg cm−2). The relevant contents are as follows: Pt/C powder (10%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. RuO2 powder was synthesized according to the formerly reported method. Typically, the 50 mL RuCl3·xH2O (0.1 M) was well blended with 15 mL 1.0 M KOH, then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 100 °C for 45 min. After cooling down, the sample was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, washed with DI water several times and then dried at 60 °C overnight. A certain mass of Pt/C or RuO2 powders were dispersed into a mixed solution with water, ethanol and Nafion 117 (5%) in a ratio of 12:12:1. Then, 20 μL of the dispersions were drop-coated on the pretreated Ni foam or Ti mesh.

The Ni/Ni0.2Mo0.8N/MoO3 nanorods were prepared as follows. A piece of NF (3 cm × 2 cm) was first pretreated with 3 M HCl solution to remove surface impurity and then washed with water to ensure clean surface of NF. In typical synthesis, 1 mmol NiCl2⋅6H2O and 1 mmol Na2MoO4⋅2H2O were dissolved in 40 mL water to form uniform solution under stirring. Then, the NF was immersed in 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave containing above precursor solution, which was heated at 150 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the NiMo-based precursor (denoted as NiMoO) was taken out and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Then, the as-prepared NiMoO precursor was annealed in a tube furnace under NH3 atmosphere at 500 °C for 3 h, and finally cooled down to room temperature to obtain Ni/Ni0.2Mo0.8N/MoO3. In control experiment, the NiMoO precursor was calcined at 500 °C under Ar atmosphere to obtained the NiMoO4.

The Co9S8/Ni3S2 bifunctional electrocatalyst was in situ grown on commercial Co/Ni alloy foam by a one-step sulfurization synthesis strategy . Firstly, the Co/Ni alloy foam was sequentially cleaned using 1 M HCl, acetone, deionized water and ethyl alcohol, respectively. Then the Co/Ni clean alloy foam (0.5 × 0.5 cm2) was immersed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with 30 mL of water solution containing 20 mM thioacetamide. The hydrothermal synthesis was carried out at varying temperature for 3 h. The Co9S8/Ni3S2 electrocatalysts with different hydrothermal temperature of 140, 160, and 180 °C were denoted as CNS-140, CNS-160, and CNS-180, respectively. After cooling, the obtained samples were washed with water and dried at 60 ℃ for 8 h.

The Ni3S2 electrocatalyst was synthesized similar to the preparation of Co9S8/Ni3S2 electrocatalyst. The Ni foam (0.5 × 0.5 cm2) was immersed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with 30 mL of water solution containing 20 mM thioacetamide. The Ni3S2 electrocatalysts were prepared at 140 ℃ and 160 ℃ for 3 h, and the as-prepared samples were denoted as NiS-140 and NiS-160, respectively.

For comparison with RMP developed Fe-Ni3S2/NiS, iron doped nickel sulfides were also prepared by treating Fe-NF through hydrothermal and CVD methods. To synthesize iron doped nickel sulfide hydrothermally, a piece of Fe-NF has been placed in an autoclave containing 0.3 g thiourea dissolved in 15 ml water and kept in an oven at 150⁰ C for 10 h. After the reaction, the sample was washed with water and ethanol and then dried under vacuum at room temperature.

Na2MoO4·2H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, KOH and urea were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Co., Ltd. Nickel foam (1.5 mm in thickness) is obtained from The Source of Power Battery Material Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents were directly used without any further purification. Firstly, the precursor was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Specifically, 2 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O, 2 mmol NiCl2·6H2O were dissolved in 30 mL deionized (DI) water under stirring for 5 min. Then, nickel foam (1.5 mm × 2 cm × 3 cm, prewashed in 5% HCl for 10 min) was put into the as-prepared mixture, and then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. After hydrothermal treating at 160 °C for 6 h, the product was collected and washed by DI water and ethanol for three times respectively. After that, precursor product was put in a beaker and dried in oven (60 °C, air) for overnight. Then, the as-obtained precursor was annealed under H2/Ar (5:95 sccm) environment at 450 °C for 1.5 h. After reaction, the product was collected without washing for the subsequent characterization and electrochemical measurements.

Synthesis of NF/CoN: The NF/CoN was prepared by the hydrothermal reaction and following nitridation process. In typical, 1 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 2 mmol NH4F and 5 mmol CO(NH2)2 were firstly dissolved into 50 mL ultrapure water. Then, a piece of clean NF (3 × 3 cm2) was immersed into above solution and left undisturbed for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and maintained at 110 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the NF/Co(OH)2 was rinsed with ultrapure water. Finally, the NF/CoN was synthesized through the nitridation of NF/Co(OH)2 under 400 °C for 2 h by using dicyandiamide as the nitrogen source.

All reagents were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. without any purification. Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and thiourea were mixed with the molar ratio of 1: 2: 3.9, and followed by dissolving in a 40 mL mixture solution (deionized water: ethanolamine=1: 1 (v/v)) and transferred to a 50 mL autoclave. After being maintained at 160°C for 10 h, the black sediment was centrifuged and washed alternately with water and ethanol. The product was dried at 60°C for 12 h (named CuCo2S4-0.95) and placed in a tube furnace. The final Cu1.96S/Co9S8 was obtained by heating at 700°C in an Ar/H2 atmosphere with a flow rate of 80 mL min−1 for 1 h. For comparison, the proportion of thiourea was increased to 1: 2: 4, and synthesized based on the above method (denoted as CuCo2S4-1). Additionally, Co9S8 was synthesized through a similar process by removing the copper salt.

0.25 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.107 mmol of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, and 26 mmol of CH4N2S were dissolved into 20 mL of water and sonicated to form a pale green transparent solution; Meanwhile, 50 mg of MWCNTs was ultrasonically dispersed into 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol to obtain a suspension. Then, adding the suspension into the above solution and ultrasonicated for 30 min, stirring with a glass rod at the same time to ensure the formation of homogeneous suspension. After transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL), the reaction suspension was heated at 200 °C for 9 h, which was then cooled to room temperature spontaneously. The obtained black product was washed several times with deionized water, dried in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C for 12 h, and named as NiS2/MoS2/CNTs-p.

In brief, the NiCoP nanowire arrays were synthesized by a typical by a two-step process involving a hydrothermal approach followed by phosphorization . Firstly, NiCo-precursor was synthesized by a typical hydrothermal process. A piece of Ni foam was carefully sonicated in acetone, 1.0 M HCl, deionized water and ethanol for 15 min to clean the surface, respectively. Then, 1.00 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 6.00 mmol of Co(NH2)2 and 1.00 mmol of NH4F were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water to form a pink solution. As-obtained pink solution with treated Ni foam was transferred to 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 6 h. Then, the precursor was collected, washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times and dried in vacuum overnight. And the NiCo precursor was converted to NiCoP by a phosphating process. The as-prepared precursor and 4.72 mmol of NaH2PO2·H2O were placed at both ends of a tubular furnace, and NaH2PO2·H2O was at the upstream side. The phosphating process was conducted at 300 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 under continuous Ar flow.

column2: {'PRECURSOR': 'Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O)'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'thiourea'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'Silver nitrate (AgNO3)'}, {'SOLVENT': 'deionized (DI) water'}, {'TARGET': 'Ag2S to NiSx'}
column3: Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O) of 2 ​g, and thiourea (CH4N2S) of 1 ​g were used as Ni and S source, respectively. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) of 0 ​g, 0.094 ​g, 0.187 ​g, 0.280 ​g, 0.373 ​g, and 0.467 ​g were respectively added to achieve different molar ratio of Ag2S to NiSx (0:1, 0.04:1, 0.08:1, 0.12:1, 0.16:1, and 0.20:1) in the target product. These salts were totally dissolved into 50 ​mL deionized (DI) water as precursor solutions, which were thereafter transferred into the autoclaves with PTFE inner reactors to experience hydrothermal reaction under 210 ​°C for 24 ​h. The suspensions were washed and filtered at least three times and finally collected and dried in an oven under 60 ​°C for 12 ​h. The solid products were ground into fine powders before using.

For synthesis of cobalt nanorod arrays, 4 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 10 mmol CO(NH2)2 and 8 mmol NH4F were firstly added in 60 mL deionized water, followed by 20 min stirring for making them totally dissolved. After transferring the solution to a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave, a small piece of pre-treated Ni foam (2 × 4 cm2) was further added in.
