Then, with the autoclave sealed, it was subjected to an oven at the temperature of 160 °C for 12 h. When the autoclave cooled down to room temperature naturally, the sample was taken out, washed with absolute ethanol and deionized water, respectively, and dried in vacuum at 70 °C over night.

column2: {'TARGET': 'CoSe plicated nanosheet arrays'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'Se'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'NaOH'}, {'SOLVENT': 'N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF)'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O)'}, {'SUBSTRATE': 'Ni foam'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'cobalt carbonate hydroxide'}
column3: The CoSe plicated nanosheet arrays were prepared by selenization of the cobalt nanorod arrays precursor with a hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 3.75 mmol Se powder and 7.5 mmol NaOH were firstly mixed in a solution with 50 mL N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and 150 μL Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) under stirring for 30 min. Then, the Ni foam supported precursors (cobalt carbonate hydroxide nanowires) was subjected into the solution, which was subsequently kept at 180 °C for 3 h. After it was cooled down to room temperature, the resulting Ni foam covered with plicated nanosheet-like CoSe/NF array was washed with absolute ethanol and deionized water for several times, respectively, and then dried in vacuum at 60 °C over night. For simplicity, the product of CoSe plicated nanosheets array grown on Ni foam was termed as CoSe/NF.

With a third hydrothermal procedure, NiFe-LDH could be ex-situ synthesized on the facet of CoSe/NF arrays. In detail, CoSe/NF was further added into 60 mL aqueous solution in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave, which contains Fe(NO3)2·9H2O (1 mmol), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (3 mmol), and urea (6 mmol). It was later heated to 100 °C for 10 h in order to make NiFe-LDH nanosheets being grown on the surface of the CoSe/NF arrays. When the reaction was completed, and the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, the as-obtained samples were washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, respectively. By identifying the difference in mass of the NF electrodes before and after, the catalyst loading was determined to be ∼1.5 mg cm−2.

All the chemicals are analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The CoMoO4 nanocolumns arrays were firstly synthesized by a solvothermal reaction according to the previous report. In a typical procedure, 1 mmol Co(NO3)2•6H2O and 1 mmol Na2MoO4•2H2O were dissolved in mixed solution of 10 ml DI water and 5 ml ethylene glycol with vigorous stirring for 10 min. As formed solution was transferred into a 25 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a surface-cleaned NF perpendicularly immersed into the solution, and then heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the NF was removed from the solution and washed with DI water and ethanol for several times, followed by drying at 60 °C in an oven for further use.

For the synthesis of porous CoMoS4 hollow nanosheets arrays, the CoMoO4 nanocolumns on NF were immersed into 1.8 mmol Na2S solution and hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C for 12 h. As formed products were taken out from the solution and washed several times with DI water and ethanol to remove unreacted residues. The mass loading of the catalyst is about 3.2 mg/cm2.

MoS2 and CoS2 film on NF were also prepared for comparison of their catalytic activities. MoS2 film was synthesized by directly hydrothermal treatment of 10 mM (NH4)2MoS4 at 150 °C for 12 h with NF immersed into the solution. CoS2 film was synthesize by a two-step hydrothermal strategy. Firstly, the solution of 0.8 M cobalt nitrate and 2 M urea was reacted at 120 °C for 10 h to grow the precursor on NF, then the film was sulfurizing treated at 120 °C for 12 h using thioacetamide as sulfide source.

Co-precursor/CF was prepared via a typical process: Co(NO3)2•6H2O (4 mmol), urea (5 mmol), and NH4F (2 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water by ultrasonic process forming a aqueous solution. A piece of CF (1 cm × 2 cm) which was separately rinsed by hydrochloric acid, acetone, ethanol and deionized water under ultrasonic treatment for 15 min was put into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (25 mL volume). Then the prepared aqueous solution was also transferred in. After that, the autoclave was maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally, and then the Co-precursor on CF was taken out and washed with ethanol, deionized water to remove residues, and finally dried at 60 °C for 10 h.

2 mmol Ni(NO3)20.6H2O, 0.25 mmol Cu(NO3)20.6H2O, 4 mmol NH4F and 10 mmol Urea were added to 35 mL deionized water (DIW). After being completely dissolved, it was transferred to the autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 6 h.

The nickel foam (3 × 3 cm2) is cleaned in 3.0 M HCl solution by sonication for 20 min. After washed with plenty of deionized water and ethanol, NF is vacuum dried at 80 °C. A facile hydrothermal treatment is used to grow Ni(OH)2 on the cleaned NF substrate. A bright green solution (30 mL) containing 1 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 4 mmol urea is transferred into an autoclave (50 mL), and then the cleaned NF is immersed in the solution as substrate. The sealed autoclave is placed in an oven and heated at 100 °C for 12 h. The obtained Ni(OH)2/NF is washed with water and dried at 80 °C. Then another hydrothermal process is used to prepare P-Ni3S2-NiS/NF. Thioacetamide (4 mmol) and sodium hypophosphite (2 mmol) are dissolved in 30 mL of water, which is transferred into 50 mL of autoclave. After the Ni(OH)2/NF is put inside, the autoclave is sealed and heated at 160 °C for 16 h. The resultant P-Ni3S2-NiS/NF is rinsed with water and dried at 80 °C in a vacuum.

Material synthesis: Before synthesis process, the commercial Ni foams are successively cleaned by diluted hydrochloric acid, absolute ethanol and deionized water. First of all, Cu–Co precursor arrays are prepared by the hydrothermal process. Typically, 6 mM CoCl2·6H2O, 3 mM CuCl2·2H2O and 28 mM urea are dissolved in 80 mL deionized water. The mixed solution with a piece of Ni foam is transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and keeps at 120 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, Cu–Co precursor arrays directly on Ni foam are obtained after washing and drying. Then, Cu–Co precursor arrays are annleaed at 400 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 4 °C min−1, which can obtain the CuCo2O4/CuO arrays directly on Ni foam. Finally, RuO2 nanoparticles are coated over the CuCo2O4/CuO arrays by the electrodepostion process. Typically, the electrodepostion process is conducted by the CHI 660D electrochemical workstation at a constant current density of −10 mA cm−2 in 10 mM RuCl3 electrolyte, where the CuCo2O4/CuO on Ni foam, Ag/AgCl and graphite rod are served as working electrode, reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. After electrodeposition for 8, 15 and 20 min, different samples are obtained, and denoted as CuCo2O4/CuO@RuO2-1, CuCo2O4/CuO@RuO2-2 and CuCo2O4/CuO@RuO2-3, respectivly. For comparison, pure RuO2 is also electrodeposited on Ni foam by similar process when the electrodeposition time is 15 min. The mass loadings of CuCo2O4/CuO, CuCo2O4/CuO@RuO2-1, CuCo2O4/CuO@RuO2-2 and CuCo2O4/CuO@RuO2-3 were 3.10, 3.85, 4.60, and 5.45 mg cm−2, respectively.

To synthesize FeS2-MoS2@CoS2-MOF, sodium molybdate (1.2 g, Na₂MoO₄·2H₂O), thiourea (3.2 g, NH2CSNH2), and iron (III) chloride (0.4 g, FeCl3·6H2O) were completely dissolved in 120 mL DI. This mixed solution was poured into a 150 mL Teflon vessel, and the CoS2-MOF (at NF) was dipped into it at 180 °C for 20 h for the solvothermal reaction.

Co(NO3)2 (8 mmol), Ni(NO3)2 (4 mmol) and urea (12 mmol) were added into a solution containing 30 mL of ethanol and 30 mL of water, followed by stirring at room temperature for 30 min. The solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave, a piece of Ni foam (2 × 3 cm2) was carefully placed inside, and the autoclave was heated at 150 °C for 6 h. The following reactions occurred:CO(NH2)2 + H2O → CO2 + 2NH3CO2 + H2O → CO32−+2H+NH3 + H2O → NH4++OH−2Ni2++4Co2++3CO32−+6OH-→Ni2Co4(CO3)3(OH)6

A piece of nickel foam was etched in 3.0 M HCl solution with sonication for 30 min and subsequently washed with deionized water and ethanol to ensure a clean surface. CoMoOx nanosheets (termed as CoMoOx NSs) were synthesized through a facial hydrothermal reaction. Typically, 2.5 mmol CO(NH2)2, 2.0 mmol NH4F, 0.25 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and 0.1 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O were dissolved into 40 mL deionized water to form a clear solution under constant stirring. Then, the pre-treated nickel foam was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave containing the mixed solution above and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the product was washed with deionized water and ethanol several times and finally dried at 60 °C under a vacuum for 24 h. CoMoO4 nanosheets (termed as CoMoO4 NSs) were obtained by annealing as-prepared CoMoOx nanosheets at 500 °C for 2 h under an air atmosphere.

The fabrication process for Co3Mo/CoMoO3 NPSs on nickel foam is illustrated in Fig. 1. First, CoMoOx NSs grown on nickel foam were synthesized by a hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 12 h in 40 mL aqueous solution containing 0.25 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.1 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, 2.5 mmol CO(NH2)2, and 2.0 mmol NH4F. SEM images clearly show that the obtained CoMoOx displays hierarchical flower-like microsphere morphology with an average diameter of 8.17 μm (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Information). XPS spectra of CoMoOx NSs (Fig. S2) indicate the formation of Co/Mo oxides. Subsequently, the obtained CoMoOx NSs were annealed in air at 500 °C for 2 h to form CoMoO4 NSs, which is confirmed by the SEM and TEM characterizations (Fig. S3) and XPS analysis (Fig. S4). Finally, the obtained CoMoO4 NSs were further annealed in H2/Ar (v/v, 5/95) atmosphere at 500 °C for 2 h to create Co3Mo/CoMoO3 NPSs.

The Ir NPs/C catalyst was synthesized via solvothermal method. Firstly, carbon was washed with diluted hydrochloric acid solution and ethanol under ultrasonication for 30 min, respectively, to remove the possible impurities. Then, 100 mg carbon, 0.02 M hexachloroiridium acid hydrate (dissolved in water,1.3 mL) and ethanol (18.7 mL) were put into a 40-mL polyphenylene (PPL) carrier, followed by the ultrasonic process for 30 min and magnetic stirring process overnight to evenly mix and then transferred into the Teflon-autoclave, sealed and maintained 160 °C for 5 h in an oven. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the solids were cleaned three times with ethanol and ultrapure water, centrifugally separated, dried and collected. Moreover, we synthesized the carbon supported Ir nanoparticles by using hydrazine hydrate as the reducing agent. The experimental results are shown in Fig. S11.

A series of Fe(SexS1−x)2 catalysts were successfully synthesized via a facile and scalable one-step hydrothermal method by tuning the proportion of S and Se powders. The aqueous solution of iron chloride (FeCl3) and the hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) solution of selenium (Se) and sulfur (S) were mixed in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 24 h to obtain the Fe(SexS1−x)2 catalysts.

The multicomponent Fe(SexS1−x)2 was synthesized using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. FeCl3 (0.1 mol, 162 mg) was added into deionized water (25 mL), and the mixed solution was stirred for 30 min under argon atmosphere, different ratios of sulfur and selenium (Supporting Information Table S1) was dissolved in 8 mL of hydrazine monohydrate under intense stirring to get a homogeneous solution, subsequently mixed the above two solutions and bubbling with argon gas for 5 min, the solution was transferred to 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 180 ℃ for 24 h in an electric oven. After cooling down to room temperature, The as-prepared samples were collected via filtration, washed with DI water and ethanol several times and and were freeze-dried overnight.

FexVy-MOF/NF was prepared by a simple strategy. Firstly, nickel foam (about 1 × 2.5 cm) was cleaned in an ultrasound bath for 30 min with 1 M HCl aqueous solution to clear the impurity, then washed by DI water and ethanol for three times. Briefly, the FexVy-MOF/NF with different molar ratios of Fe to V (x:y) was prepared by mixing FeCl3·6H2O and VCl3 in 15 mL DMF with the total mole of metal cations (Fe3+ + V3+) as 2 mmol. Afterwards, 1 mmol of NH2BDC and 2 mL acetic acid was respectively added to the above solution and dispersed by ultrasonication for 30 min, then the solution was added to a 50 mL stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave and was placed one piece of NF, then, reacted at 120 °C for 15 h. When cooling down to the R.T., the FexVy-MOF/NF were obtained by rinsing with DMF and ethanol, then dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 12 h.

Specifically, 2.5 mmol CeO2, 0.75 mmol NH4F and 1.25 mmol CO(NH2)2 were dissolved in the 30 mL deionized water. Then the above solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and a piece of NF (2 cm × 3 cm) was immersed into the solution. The autoclave was kept at 130 °C for 8 h in an electric oven. After the autoclave cooled down to room temperature, the as-obtained product was washed with deionized water and ethanol several times. The so prepared sample was calcinated at 300 °C for 2 h. After natural cooling, the CeO2/NF was obtained.

PristineMnCo2O4 nanostructures were synthesized using hydrothermal process by procedure reported in our previous works. In the typical synthesis process, 1 M Mn(Ac) and 2 M Co(Ac) was mixed in 30 mL of DW to form a homogeneous mixture. 2 mM of CTAB was incorporated in the above solution and stirred for 2 h. It was then hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 12 h. The formed product was washed and dried at 60 °C. It was then annealed at 600 °C for 2 h. The obtained final product was denoted as pristineMnCo2O4 nanostructures.

Synthesis of Co-MOFs precursor: in a typical preparation process, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.864 g), 4-(2,4,6-tricarboxylphenyl)-2,2’:6′,2″-terpyridine (0.63 mg) and PVP (3.0 g) were added to a uniformly mixed solution of ethanol (20 mL), deionized water (20 mL) and N,N-dimethyl formamide (20 mL) under magnetic stirring at room temperature. After 1 h stirring, the suspensions were transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with hydrothermal treatment at 150 °C for 10 h. The brick-red sample was obtained after centrifugation following washing with deionized water (one time) and anhydrous ethanol (three times) and dried at 60 °C for 6 h.

0.10 g CoCl2·2H2O and 0.02 g thiourea were mixed in 60 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred for 30 min. A solution containing Co and S precursors was added dropwise to the g-C3N4 solution and stirred for 30 min. A Teflon-lined autoclave was used to heat the solution for 24 h at 180 °C after stirring, after which the solution was cooled to room temperature. A solution of CoS/g-C3N4 was dissolved in distilled water and ethanol and dried at 60 °C overnight. In order to conduct a comparison, CoS alone was synthesized under identical conditions without the addition of g-C3N4. For the synthesis of NiCu-LDH/g-C3N4 and NiCu-LDH/CoS, equal molar ratios of NiCu-LDH with CoS or g-C3N4 were added to beaker with water and subsequently stirred for 3 h. The obtained products were washed with ethanol and water, and place in a 60 °C oven overnight. The obtained NiCu-LDH/g-C3N4 and NiCu-LDH/CoS nanostructures are denoted as NiCu-LDH/g-C3N4 and NiCu-LDH/CoS, respectively. To fabricate the NiCu-LDH@CoS/g-C3N4 nanostructures, the prepared NiCu-LDH and CoS/g-C3N4 were added to distilled water, which was stirred for 3 h. The obtained products were washed with ethanol and water and then place in a 60 °C oven overnight.

60 mg of PF were dispersed in 36 mL of DMF and 9 mL of methanol by sonication for 30 min. In this reaction, DMF and methanol act as stabilizing and dispersing agents, respectively. Then a mixture of n mg of RuCl3 (n = 30, 20, 15, 0) and m mg of K2PtCl4 (m = 0, 10, 15, 30) was introduced into the above solution under vigorous stirring. The resulting mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel and heated for 15 h at 160 °C. The products (PtmRun/PF) were separated by centrifugation, rinsed with methanol (three times) and dried overnight at 60 °C.

To remove the surface oxides, Ni foam was in turn washed with 3.0 M HCl, ethanol, and water under sonication for 10 min. The preparation of Ru–MoO3-x NF followed a one-step route. 0.045 mmol of RuCl3·xH2O and 1.5 mmol H4MoNa2O6 were dissolved in 30 ml of water, and then 3.8 mmol of NH4F and 7 mmol of urea were added into the above solution under magnetic stirring for 30 min (pH is about 6.11). Next, the mixture was transferred to a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and the cleaned Ni foam (2.5 cm × 5 cm) was immersed vertically in the autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 6 h. Finally, the product was washed with water repeatedly, and dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 12 h to obtain the Ru–MoO3-x NF sample. The mass loading of the final material on Ni foam was almost 1 mg cm−2.

The Ni foam (3 cm × 3 cm) was cleaned with 3.0 mol L−1 HCl solution and absolute ethanol with an ultrasound for 10 min each. 484.8 mg Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 872.4 mg Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 303.0 mg urea and 18.5 mg NH4F were dissolved in a solution comprised of 30 mL distilled water and 20 mL absolute ethanol under vigorous stirring for 10 min. Then, the above solution was transferred into an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, in which contained a piece of cleaned Ni foam. The autoclave was heated at 120 °C for 6 h and allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The product was taken out, washed with distilled water, and dried at 60 °C.

Typically, 300.5 mg thioacetamide (TAA) was dissolved in a solution consisted of 40 mL distilled water and 10 mL absolute ethanol. The above solution was transferred into an 80 mL autoclave, containing a piece of as-prepared FeNi-LDH Ss/Ni. The autoclave was maintained at 140 °C for 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the product was taken out and severally washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol for four times. Finally, the (Fe0.2Ni0.8)0.96S TSs/Ni was obtained after drying at 60 °C overnight. The mass loading is 2.5 mg cm−2.

Iron (III) nitrate (Fe (NO3)3·9H2O), cobalt (II) nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), ammonia (NH3·H2O) and polyethene glycol (PEG, Mn = 400) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). The CoFe2O4 nanoparticles were prepared by dissolving 3.2 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 1.2 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.2 g of polyethene glycol (PEG, Mn = 400) in 40 mL of ultrapure water (0.055 μS cm−1) under vigorous stirring for 30 min. For Co2FeO4 nanoparticles, 1.6 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 2.3 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.2 g of PEG were mixed in 40 mL of ultrapure water. 5 mL of ammonia diluted with 5 mL of ultrapure water was slowly added dropwise into the solution mixture. The obtained suspension was subsequently transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, maintained at 180 °C for 3 h. The product was washed several times with ultrapure water via centrifugation, dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h.

3.6 mg Cl3H18N6Ru (11.6 µmol), 6.0 mg K2TeO3 (23.6 µmol) and 65.0 mg PVP (1.1 µmol) were dissolved in 2 mL H2O. After a few minutes of sonication, 2.0 mL NH3·H2O and 1.0 mL N2H4·H2O were quickly injected into the above mixture. The mixture solution was then transferred into Teflon-sealed autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 3 h. The RuTe2 PNRs were obtained by washed several times with ethanol/acetone solution.

A total of 20 mg of (NH4)2MoS4 was dispersed in 10 ml of DMF. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 10 min until a homogeneous solution was achieved. After that, 0, 0.05 ml, 1 and 2.5 ml of HZH were added to four of the above mixture respectively. The reaction solution was further stirred for 10 min before transferred to 40 ml Teflon-lined autoclaves. Then they were heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After the system was cooling down, the suspension was centrifuged and washed with ethanol for three times. Then the precipitant was obtained and re-dispersed in ethanol for further characterizations.

NiV-LDH was synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, a piece of Ni foam (2 cm × 3 cm) was ultrasonically cleaned in HCl solution for 10 min to dissolve the surface oxide layer and then washed with deionized water for several times. Then, 0.6 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.48 mmol of VCl3, and 4 mmol of urea (the role of urea can be seen in Supplementary Note 1) were dispersed in 30 mL deionized water and stirred for 30 min. The cleaned Ni foam was submerged into the solution and transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 120 °C for 12 h in an electric oven and then cooled to room temperature. The product was washed with deionized water for several times and then dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain NiV-LDH. The synthesis processes of NiVRu-LDH and NiVIr-LDH were the same as that of NiV-LDH, except for the addition of RuCl3 and IrCl3·xH2O, respectively. To synthesize NiVRu-LDH and NiVIr-LDH with different Ru and Ir contents, the dosages of RuCl3 (IrCl3·xH2O) were 0.06, 0.12, 0.18, 0.24, 0.3, and 0.36 mmol for samples 1~ 6, respectively. The mass loadings of NiV-LDH, NiVRu-LDH, and NiVIr-LDH were 2.6, 1.2, and 0.7 mg cm−2, respectively. The contents of Ru and Ir were determined by XPS. NiV-LDH, NiVRu-LDH with 1.11 at% Ru, and NiVIr-LDH with 0.62 at% Ir were used for material characterizations.

Fe/V co-doped Ni (oxy)hydroxide NS array on CFP was prepared by a hydrothermal method. In a typical fabrication process of Ni3Fe0.5V0.5/CFP, the solution of NiCl2·6H2O (0.6 mmol, 142.62 mg), FeCl3·6H2O (0.1 mmol, 27.03 mg), and VCl3 (0.1 mmol, 15.73 mg) in deionized water (40 mL) was magnetically stirred for 10 min to form a homogenous solution, to which urea (4 mmol, 240.24 mg) was added with subsequent stirring for 10 min. Afterwards, the prepared solution was transferred to a 50 mL stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave and a piece of the pretreated hydrophilic CFP (3 × 4 cm) was placed upright in the middle of autoclave. Next, the autoclave was sealed and heated in an electric oven at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling the system to room temperature naturally, the resulting CFP with Ni3Fe0.5V0.5 (oxy)hydroxide NS array was washed with deionized water and ethanol by the assistance of ultrasonication for three times to remove the loosely attached materials, and then dried in vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight. A series of reference electrodes, Ni(OH)2, Ni3Fe, Ni3V, Ni3Fe0.33V0.67, and Ni3Fe0.67V0.33 (oxy)hydroxide NS arrays on CFP, were prepared by the essentially identical procedure. The doping level of Fe and V atoms in the host structure of Ni (oxy)hydroxide was controlled by precisely regulating the molar ratio of Ni/Fe/V salts in the precursor solution, while with the same total amount of metal ions in the initial solutions (Ni2+ + Fe3+ + V3+ = 0.8 mmol). For each hydroxide catalyst, at least three electrodes were prepared and used for the spectroscopic and catalytic measurements.

The Co-Fe and Ni-Fe PBA hollow cage, designed as CFHC and NFHC respectively, were synthesized through a solvothermal treatment. Experimentally, 20 mg of the as-synthesized CFC/NFC were dispersed in 20 mL of EtOH with the assistance of ultrasonication. This dispersion was then added into 20 mL of EtOH solution containing 100 mg of PVP under vigorously stirring. The obtained transparent solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave capped with a stainless steel vessel. After heating at 180 °C for 24 h, the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature. The precipitates were collected by centrifuging at 8500 rpm for 15 min and washed with DI water and EtOH three times and dried at 70 °C for 12 h.

The LDH-based electrode was synthesized from hydrothermal method according to previous report. Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.3 g, 1 mmol), Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (0.4 g, 1 mmol) and urea (0.3 g, 5 mmol) were dissolved into deionized water (80 mL) under vigorous stirring. Then a piece of cleaned Ni foam (0.5 cm × 5 cm) and the as-prepared solution was transferred into an autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. The resulting material was washed with ethanol three times and dried in vacuum at room temperature, giving the LDH-based electrode.

All samples were prepared by the hydrothermal method. Firstly, C14H21O14Ru3 (0.07 mol, 5.02 g), WCl6 (0.002 mol, 0.79 g), and ErCl3 (0.001 mol, 0.27 g) were added into ultra-pure water with vigorous stirring. Then, EDTA (0.01 mol, 2.92 g) and citric acid (0.01 mol, 1.92 g) was added into ultra-pure water with regulating pH to 9 by NH3·H2O. Finally, the solution containing EDTA and critic acid was added drop-wise into the metal salt solution for reaction for 12 h at 353 K. The obtained precursors were tiled in a treated porcelain boat and heated to 573 K for 4 h and 673 K for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 K/min in muffle furnace, respectively. The porous W0.2Er0.1Ru0.7O2−δ nanosheets were obtained after the muffle furnace cooling to 303 K. The methods for preparation of the RuO2−δ, W0.1Ru0.9O2−δ, W0.2Ru0.8O2−δ, W0.3Ru0.7O2−δ, Er0.1Ru0.9O2−δ, and W0.2Er0.2Ru0.6O2−δ nanosheets were the same as that of W0.2Er0.1Ru0.7O2−δ nanosheets.

The chemicals and materials used in this work are listed in Supplementary Note 1. The NiFe hydroxides nanosheets were grown on the NF via a facile hydrothermal approach described as follows: FeCl3•6H2O (540.6 mg, 2 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL of H2O, which was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave of 50 mL. A piece of NF was immersed into the Fe(III) solution and reacted under ultrasonic for 30 min. Urea (900 mg, 15 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL H2O and mixed with the Fe(III) solution, and then 20 mL of ethanol was added into the solution. The autoclave was subsequently sealed and heated to 160 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, the NF was washed with H2O and ethanol several times to obtain the NiFe hydroxide nanosheets (denoted as NiFe(OH)x-NF). The NiFe(OH)x-NF was then heated in air or NH3-Ar gas mixture (20:80) at 300 °C (heating rate 2 °C per min) for 3 h to obtain Ni-Fe oxides (denoted as NiFeOx-NF) or nitrides (denoted as NiFeNx-NF), respectively.

The MXene/CF was first prepared by immersing copper foam pretreated by 1.0 M HCl into Ti3C2Tx MXene colloids (5 mg mL–1), followed by drying in vacuum at 40 °C for 12 h. A piece of MXene/CF (2 × 3 cm2) and 2, 6-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid dipotassium (0.2 mmol) were added into a aqueous solution (25 mL) of Ni (CH3COO)2·4H2O (0.1 mmol) and Co (CH3COO)2·4H2O (0.1 mmol). The reaction was conducted in a sealed autoclave at 80 °C for 3 h. The obtained product was annealed at 400 °C for 2 h at a ramp rate of 5 °C min–1 in NH3 flow to yield NiCo@C/MXene/CF. As a control sample, the NiCo@C/CF was also prepared in a similar way in the absence of MXene. The Pt/CF electrode with an average mass loading of ca. 1.0 mg cm–2 was also made by casting the catalyst ink (125 μL) of commercial 20% Pt/C (1.0 mg) in ethanol, DI water and Nafion (5.0 wt.%) with a volume ratio of 100: 97: 3 on the CF (1 × 1 cm).

0.07 M Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.01 M (NH4)Mo7O24·4H2O and 0.30 M thiourea were dissolved into 15 mL deionized water and stirred for 10 min under room temperature. Then the solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave with nickel foam. After hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 24 h, NiMoS precursor was obtained through washing with deionized water and then dried in an oven at 60 °C. As-obtained NiMoS precursors were irradiated by RF plasma under an oxygen flow (RF power, 50~150 W) for the oxidation treatment. Afterward the arrays were annealed up to 300–500 °C in H2/Ar (0.05/0.95) for the typical hydrogenation regulation, thus resulting into the synthesis of NiMoOx/NiMoS heterostructure array by oxidation/hydrogenation-induced surface reconfiguration strategy. In comparison, as-obtained NiOx/Ni3S2 and MoOx/MoS2 heterostructure arrays were synthesized in parallel by the same procedure as that of NiMoOx/NiMoS array expect for in absence of (NH4)Mo7O24·4H2O or Ni(NO3)2·6H2O in hydrothermal reaction.

(i) Synthesis of the MIL-101 Fe precursor: typically, FeCl3·6H2O (0.675 g) and terephthalic acid (0.206 g) were fully dissolved in 15 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide by vigorous ultrasonic vibration. Next, the solution was transferred into a 45 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After the autoclave was sealed and heated to 110 °C and then maintained at this temperature for 20 h, it was cooled down to ambient temperature naturally. The brown product was isolated from the resulting reaction solution by centrifugation at 8000 r.p.m. and washed three times with 20 ml of ethanol at 60 °C to remove any unreacted starting materials. Finally, the product was dried at 60 °C in an oven for 8 h to obtain the red brown fine powder.

In a typical procedure for synthesizing α-MnO2 nanofibres, 0.02 mol of MnSO4‧5H2O (analytical reagent, AR), 0.02 mol of (NH4)2S2O8 (AR) and 0.06 mol of K2SO4 (AR) were dissolved in 70 ml H2O. After vigorous stirring for 1 h, the mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the as-obtained dark brown powders were washed five times with deionized water, separated via vacuum filtration and dried overnight in an oven at 80 °C.

Terephthalic acid (83 mg, 0.5 mmol) were dissolved in 5 mL N,N- dimethylformamide (DMF). Then, 1 mL 0.4 M NaOH was added under stirring. The solution above was slowly mixed with 5 mL cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate (145 mg, 0.5 mmol) DMF solution in a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a piece of NF (1 cm × 3 cm) in it. After that, the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave was heated for 15 h at 100 °C. The resulting MOF film on NF was washed with DMF and ethanol three times and dried naturally. The loading amount of the MOF on NF was determined to be about 2.1 mg cm−2.

Terephthalic acid (166 mg, 1 mmol) and cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate (291 mg, 1 mmol) were dissolved in 5 mL N,N-DMF, respectively. Then, the solution above was slowly mixed in a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After that, the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave was heated for 12 h at 100 °C. The products were washed with DMF and methanol and dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

Typically, 0.188 mmol NiCl2·6H2O and 0.375 mmol BDC were dissolved in a mixture solvent containing 16 mL DMF, 1 mL ethanol and 1 mL water to form a clear solution. The NF was cut into small pieces (2 cm × 3 cm) and sonicated in 2 M HCl for 15 min to remove surface oxides. After washed with copious water and blown dry with pure nitrogen gas, it was then transferred into a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor with the above-mentioned solution. Subsequently, the hydrothermal reactor was heated in an oven at 140 °C for 48 h to grow Ni-MOF on NF. Finally, the sample was rinsed with ethanol and ultrapure water thoroughly and dried in air for later use.

To synthesize the Ni–Mo–N/CFC electrocatalyst, NiMo-precursor was first constructed on carbon fiber cloth (NiMo-pre/CFC) through a hydrothermal reaction. First, the CFC (5 cm × 4 cm × 0.32 mm) was pretreated in H2SO4 and HNO3 mixed solution and washed with DIW by ultrasonication. Second, Ni (CH3COO)2·4H2O (0.7475 g), (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (0.5319 g), and PVP (0.2538 g) were successively dispersed in DIW (80 mL) under stirring for 40 min. The mixture (75 ml) was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (ANHUI CHEMN, HT-100H-316L). Then the CFC was immersed into the solution and placed against the wall of autoclave. Third, the autoclave was maintained at 180 °C for 15 h. The CFC with green precipitates on the surface was taken out and washed with DIW to remove any unreacted residues. The NiMo-pre/CFC was dried at 60 °C for overnight. Finally, the as-constructed NiMo-pre/CFC was heated at 500 °C for 2.5 h in a NH3 atmosphere, and then, the Ni–Mo–N/CFC electrocatalyst anchored on the CFC was obtained. The mass loading of Ni–Mo–N/CFC on CFC is ≈2.9 mg cm−2, which is obtained by dissolving the material in nitric acid. The Ni–Mo–N/CFC-400 and Ni–Mo–N/CFC-600 were fabricated after the NiMo-pre/CFCs were annealed in an NH3 atmosphere at 400 and 600 °C, respectively, for 2.5 h. The pure Ni nanoparticles on CFC was also synthesized by changing precursor and calcination atmosphere ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O is replaced by urea in precursor synthesis and NH3 by H2/Ar in calcination atmosphere).

column2: {'TARGET': 'WO3'}, {'SUBSTRATE': 'CFP'}, {'PRECURSOR': 'tungstic acid powder'}, {'SOLVENT': 'hydrogen peroxide'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'anhydrous sodium sulfate'}, {'ADDITIVE': 'hydrochloric acid'}
column3: WO3 nanoarrays on CFP were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. With magnetic stirring, tungstic acid powder (0.625 g) was dissolved in 20 ml 12 wt.% hydrogen peroxide solution by heating at 95 °C for 3 h. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon reaction kettle with 0.2 g anhydrous sodium sulfate, 115 μl hydrochloric acid, and a piece of 2 cm × 2 cm CFP added in. WO3 nanoarrays were obtained after being heated for 12 h at 180 °C. WO3 powder without nanoarray structure was also synthesized by the same method without CFP.

NiMoO4 nanorods were synthesized on nickel foam through a hydrothermal method. A piece of commercial Ni foam (2 × 5 cm2) was cleaned by ultrasonication with ethanol and DI water for several minutes, and the substrate was then transferred into a polyphenyl (PPL)-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 ml) containing a homogenous solution of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.04 M) and (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O (0.01 M) in 50 ml H2O. Afterward, the autoclave was sealed and maintained at 150 °C for 6 h. The sample was then taken out and washed with DI water and ethanol several times before being fully dried at 60 °C overnight under vacuum.

Both two pyrochlores are synthesized by using hydrothermal method. This method doesn’t need rigorous preparatory conditions and is time efficient. First, the feeding mole ratio of Pb:Ir is 1:1 by using simple precursors of Pb(NO3)2 and IrCl3 aqueous solution. Then the mixture is transferred into 40 mL Teflon-lined pressure vessel with adding 10 mL deionized water and 10 mL 0.5 M NaOH aqueous solution. Subsequently, the reactor is loaded into an oven heating to 250 °C for 720 min; then, the vessel is cooled naturally at room temperature. The precipitates are suction filtered and washed with deionized water twice to remove other ions. The remaining solid on the filter was dried to dehydration in an oven at 80 °C for 1 h. The dried solid was transferred to a crucible and annealed at 600 °C for 6 h to produce excellent crystallinity. For Bi-Ir case, as Bi(NO3)2 dissolutes in 1 M HNO3, thus to neutralize acid more alkali (1.2 M NaOH) was added. We also prepared different compositions of Pb-Ir oxides in order to investigate deeply the nature of Pb-Ir pyrochlore in OER.

Typically, Ni(NO3)2 and Mn(COOH)2 in a 3:1 molar ratio was dissolved into 20 mL of deionized water (metal-ion concentration 40 mM) with 560 mg of HMTA by continuously stirring. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h. Once cooled to room temperature, the obtained precipitate was collected and washed several times with deionized water and subsequently dried at 60 °C to yield the hierarchical NiMn LDHs. Then, the resulting NiMn LDHs were placed in a tube furnace and annealed in air at 600 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. After natural cooling to room temperature, the porous Ni6MnO8 hierarchical microspheres were collected.

The Ni(OH)2 was prepared through an alcohothermal method. Typically, 0.4 g of NiCl2 was added into a 40 -mL ethanol solution. The mixture was stirred until a transparent solution was observed. The solution was then moved into a 50 -mL autoclave. The autoclave was then placed into an oven and treated in flowing air and heated at 150 °C for 12 h. Green powder of Ni(OH)2 was treated by 24 h ultrasound in an ethanol solution and then was collected by centrifuging the reaction mixture at a speed of 9000 r.p.m. for 10 min. The product was washed by water and ethanol four times and then was dried at 80 °C overnight. The obtained pure Ni(OH)2 was used as the reference sample and named as Ni(OH)2-r.

The w-Ni(OH)2-e was prepared through an alcohothermal method. At first, 1 g of WCl6 was added into 100 mL of ethanol to form a transparent solution. Then a certain amount of WCl6–ethanol solution was added into the NiCl2–ethanol solution (0.4g–40mL) by a pipette. After stirring for 15 min, the mixture was moved into a 50 -mL autoclave, which was then treated at 150 °C for 12 h in an oven. The product was collected by centrifuging the reaction mixture at a speed of 9000 r.p.m. for 10 min. After that, the powder was washed by water and ethanol four times and then dried at 80 °C overnight. The obtained W-doped Ni(OH)2 sample was named as w-Ni(OH)2-e.

In a typical synthesis, 121.2 mg Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 436.5 mg Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 450 mg (NH2)2CO, and 222 mg NH4F were dissolved into 40 ml ultrapure water and moved into a 45 ml teflon-lined autoclave with steel shell. A piece of Ni foam (1 × 3 cm, thickness: 1.5 mm, bulk density: 0.3 g/cm3) was cleaned ultrasonically first with ethanol (20 ml), then with 3 M HCl aqueous solution (20 ml) for 15 min, and washed subsequently with deionized water for three times. The as-prepared Ni foam above was immersed into the above autoclave and exactly stuck at the center of the autoclave vertically. The autoclave was sealed and then heated at 120 °C for 10 h. The obtained electrode was cleaned with ethanol three times and dried in vacuum at room temperature for further characterizations and next step reaction. The synthetic methods of NiCo-LDH-NWAs and NiFe-LDH-NSAs were the same as that of FeCoNi-LDH-NWAs, except that no adding iron source for NiCo-LDH-NWAs and no adding cobalt source as well as increasing amount of iron source to 606 mg for NiFe-LDH-NSAs, respectively. When focusing on the details of reaction process, fluoride ions were added into the reaction solvent to coordinate with the metal ions, resulting in slow release of the metal ions and promotion of nucleus formation on the substrate. Then the released metal ions combined with CO3 2− and OH− generated from the hydrolysis of urea forming polymetal carbonate hydroxides.

First, 15 mg of (NH4)2MoS4 were dissolved in 38 ml of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and stirred for 10 min to make solution homogeneous. Then 2 ml HZH (volume fraction, 85%) was introduced dropwise into the foregoing solution with vigorously stirring. The mixed solution for growing hybrid nanotubes was transferred into a 45 ml teflon-lined autoclave with steel shell and FeCoNi-LDH-NWAs was submerged into the above solution and exactly stuck at the direction of autoclave diameter vertically. The autoclave was sealed and then heated at 200 °C for 10 h. When the system was cooled down, this obtained electrode was cleaned with ethanol three times and dried in vacuum at room temperature for further characterizations. The synthetic methods of CoNi-HNTAs and NiFe-HNSAs were the same as that of FeCoNi-HNTAs, except that the precursor of FeCoNi-LDH-NWAs was replaced by NiCo-LDH-NWAs and NiFe-LDH-NSAs, respectively.

Generally, bulk MHOFs were prepared by the reaction between M(OH)2 and a carboxylate linker under solvothermal conditions at 120 °C. Taking Ni2(OH)2(L4) as an example, freshly prepared Ni(OH)2 precursor (50 mg, 0.54 mmol), H2L4 (70 mg, 0.26 mmol), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF; 16 ml), water (1 ml) and ethanol (1 ml) were charged into a Pyrex vial. The mixture was heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed with DMF (two times) and acetone (two times) and dried at 100 °C under vacuum (95 mg, 87 at.% yield). More details are provided in the Supplementary Information.

The NiCl2·6H2O (100 mg, 0.42 mmol), H2L4 (100 mg, 0.37 mmol), DMF (16 ml), water (1 ml) and ethanol (1 ml) were charged into a Pyrex vial. The mixture was heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation, dispersed in ethanol, sonicated for 30 min and dried at 100 °C under vacuum (75 mg, 85 at.% yield).

Generally, metal-substituted MHOF nanosheets were prepared by the reaction between NiCl2·6H2O, H2L4 and respective heterometal salts under solvothermal conditions at 120 °C. Taking Fe/Ni2(OH)2(L4) as an example, NiCl2·6H2O (90 mg, 0.38 mmol), FeCl2·4H2O (10 mg, 0.05 mmol), H2L4 (100 mg, 0.37 mmol), DMF (16 ml), water (1 ml) and ethanol (1 ml) were charged into a Pyrex vial. The mixture was heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation, dispersed in ethanol, sonicated for 30 min and dried at 100 °C under vacuum (70 mg, 78 at.% yield). The Fe/(Ni + Fe) ratio was determined to be 12.5 at.% by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy.

A piece of nickel foam (NF) (2 cm × 1 cm × 1.6 mm) or stainless steel mesh, was immersed into a vial containing 1 ml of DI-water and 8 mg of Ni(Ac)2·4H2O and 2 mg of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O. Next, 10 mg of organic ligand 2,6-naphthalenedicarboxylate tetrahydrate was added into the above solution, and the vial was sealed for reaction at 60 °C for 20 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the nickel foam was taken out, bath ultrasonicated for 1 min and then rinsed with copious DI-water.

The Ni-doped iron oxide precursors were prepared according to a modified method reported previously. Typically, 68 mmol of NaCl was added to a mixed solution of 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 20 mL of deionized water. Then NiCl2·6H2O and anhydrous FeCl3 (total amount of 10 mmol) were added to the previous solution under magnetic stirring, and the amount of the Ni salt was controlled at 3%, 5%, 7%, 10%, 12%, and 15%. After complete dissolution was achieved, 25 mmol of HMTA was added and kept at constant magnetic stirring for 30 min. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 110 °C for 12 h. Finally, the Ni-doped iron oxide precursor was obtained by centrifugation, washed several times with deionized water, and then dried at 60 °C for 10 h.

A two-step fabrication strategy for Ni(PO3)2–MoO3/NF is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1. The Ni(PO3)2–MoO3/NF was synthesized through a facile hydrothermal process, followed by an in situ phosphorization approach. Specifically, bare NF was immersed in an (NH4)2Mo2O7 and Ni(NO3)2 aqueous solution and heated to 150 °C. After the hydrothermal process, the NF turned yellow. Then the synthesized NiMoO4/NF with sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2) on the upstream side was calcined at 400 °C for 2 h under an argon atmosphere, resulting in the product Ni(PO3)2–MoO3/NF. The color of NF turned from yellow to black, implying a successful phosphorization reaction.

V–Ni3N/NF was synthesized through the following process. Typically, 1.9 mmol Ni(NO3)2, 0.1 mmol VCl3, 10 mmol urea and 5 mmol NH4F were dissolved in 40 mL water with magnetic stirring to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution and a piece of NF were transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After a hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 6 h, V-doped Ni(OH)2 was uniformly grown on the surface of NF (V–Ni(OH)2/NF). Then, the V–Ni(OH)2/NF precursor was placed in the center of a quartz tube and calcined at 400 °C for 2 h to synthesize V–NiO/NF under an N2 flow. Finally, the V–Ni3N/NF product was fabricated after annealing the V–NiO/NF in an NH3 atmosphere at 400 °C for 2 h. For comparison, the V-doped Ni3N/NF samples with different ratios of Ni and V were prepared by a similar process except for adding different initial ratios (14 : 1 and 9 : 1) of Ni(NO3)2 and VCl3 and named V–Ni3N/NF-1 and V–Ni3N/NF-2. The Ni3N/NF was obtained using the same method without adding VCl3.

Firstly, Ni(OH)2 nanosheets were fabricated by a simple hydrothermal method. In detail, 120 mg NiCl2·6H2O and 168 mg hexamethylenetetramine were dissolved in a mixed solution of 27 mL water and 3 mL ethanol under vigorous stirring for 20 min. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature, the product was collected via centrifugation, washed with water four times, and dried by vacuum freeze-drying.

A simple hydrothermal approach was used for synthesizing Co–N-Ni3S2/NF, Co-Ni3S2/NF, N-Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF. Ni foam was cleaned by sonication sequentially in HCl aqueous solution (3 M), acetone, ethanol and water for 15 min each. In a typical synthesis, the cleaned Ni foam was immersed in 60 mL solution containing 0.75 g thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2) (10 mmol), 0.2 g urea, 0.582 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O (2 mmol) and 0.1 g C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, and then the above solution and Ni foam was sealed in an autoclave, and reacted for 12 h at 160 °C. After this, the Co–N-Ni3S2/NF samples were collected, rinsed repeatedly with water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C. Using the same method and parameters without the addition of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, N-Ni3S2 was synthesized. The thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2) was replaced with Na2S·9H2O, and the above experiment was repeated, and Co-Ni3S2/NF was successfully obtained. The thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2) was replaced with Na2S·9H2O and without the addition of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Ni3S2/NF was successfully obtained.

5 mmol CoCl2·6H2O (Guangdong Chemical Reagent Engineering Technology Research and Development Center, Analytical grade, 99%) and 25 mmol thiourea (Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd, analytical grade, 99%) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water and sonicated for about 10 min. Then, this solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon autoclave. Successively, the as-prepared OGFs were put into the mixed solution, leaning against the wall of the autoclave. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. During this process, CoS2 nanosheets were grown on hydrothermally reduced OGFs, and the product is termed as CoS2/GF composite films. These CoS2/GF composite films were sonicated in deionized water for 2 min to remove the physically adsorbed CoS2 on their surfaces. Finally, they were dialyzed with deionized water for 6 h, and kept in water before use. The loading mass of CoS2 on GF was measured to be 2.70 mg cm−2.

First, the substrate of hydrophilic carbon cloth (2 cm × 4 cm) was cleaned ultrasonically in nitric acid (1 M) for 1 h, and then washed with deionized water and ethanol several times. After that, 2.0 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 10 mmol NH4F and 20 mmol urea were added to 38 mL of deionized water under vigorous stirring. Next, the obtained homogeneous solution was transferred into a 40 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with one piece of hydrophilic CC placed against the wall of the autoclave. Finally, the solution in the autoclave was maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. After the temperature was cooled to room temperature, the sample was taken out and washed with water and ethanol several times. The final electrodes were put into a vacuum oven for drying.

First of all, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (291 mg), NH4F (296 mg), and urea (600 mg) were co-dissolved in deionized water (30 mL) under sonication and kept stirring for 10 min. Mo2C MXene powder was then added into the solution and the mixture was kept stirring for 20 min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave (45 mL) and heated at 120 °C for 10 h. The as-obtained Co(OH)F/Mo2CTx was washed several times with deionized water and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. Co(OH)F was produced in the same manner without adding the MXene.

(H3O)2·[Co8(HPO3)9(CH3OH)3]·2H2O (CoHPO-CJ2) was synthesized by a single-step solvothermal method as per a report published elsewhere. In detail, 0.238 g of CoCl2·6H2O was added to a mixture of H2O (1 mL) and EG (7 mL) followed by addition of 0.164 g of H3PO3 under constant stirring. The pH of the mixture was maintained between 5 and 6 by adding 0.08 mL of DETA and continued to stir for the next two hours. The homogeneous gel thus obtained was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in a preheated hot air oven at 130 °C for 3 days. After cooling down, the crystals thus obtained were separated from the mother liquor, washed repeatedly with deionized water and stored in desiccator for further use.

In a typical procedure, 3 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O and excess urea were dissolved in 60 mL distilled water and stirred uniformly at room temperature. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected after washing/centrifugation and drying at 60 °C for 12 h, and then calcined at 300 °C for 4 h in air.

In a typical procedure, 5 mmol of NH4VO3 was dissolved in 60 mL distilled water and stirred at 80 °C, resulting in a bright yellow solution, 1.0 M HNO3 aqueous solution was added into the solution, until the pH was 3. Then, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered, washed with a copious amount of distilled water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and then calcined at 350 °C for 2 h in air.

In a typical synthesis, Ni foam with dimensions of 1 × 2 cm2 was immersed firstly in 3.0 M HCl solution for 15 min to remove the oxide layer and contaminants from its surface and then washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol several times in sequence. Next, 0.25 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (99.99%), 0.75 mmol of Cu(NO3)2·6H2O (99.99%), 3 mmol of NiCl2·6H2O (99.9%) and 1.5 mmol of carbamide (99.999%) were dissolved in 40 mL of methanol (99.9%) and then the solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, into which a piece of Ni foam was placed in advance. The autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 24 h and then cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained sample was rinsed with deionized water and ethanol several times and then dried in air at 50 °C for 6 h. Afterward, the sample was annealed at 350 °C in air for 2 h to acquire the final product of Co/Cu-modified NiO film on Ni foam. For other samples, the amounts of Co(NO3)2·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·6H2O are changed to 1 and 0 mmol, 0.5 and 0.5 mmol, 0.75 and 0.25 mmol, 1 and 1 mmol, respectively. The chemicals used in this paper were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd.

Ni foam (1 × 1 cm2) is totally immersed into 3 M HCl solution and subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, and is then rinsed with lots of water and ethanol in turn. The synthesis of NiFe LDH flowers on NF is via a hydrothermal approach. 30 mL of solution containing 0.446 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.183 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.14 g of NH4F, and 0.66 g of urea is poured into a 50 mL autoclave (Anhui Kemi Machinery Technology Co., Ltd), and then the clean NF is immersed. The sealed autoclave is heated at 120 °C for 6 h. After being naturally cooled, NiFe LDH/NF is washed with water and ethanol, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight. The mass loading of NiFe LDH on NF is about 0.9 mg cm−2. The anchoring of ultrafine Pd nanoparticles on NiFe LDH/NF is via a simple electrodeposition approach. In a conventional three-electrode system, the as-obtained NiFe LDH/NF serves as a working electrode, a parallel positioned graphite rod and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) are utilized as the auxiliary electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. The electrodeposition procedure is performed in the electrolyte of 0.05 M NaCl and 0.3 mM PdCl2 with an applied potential of −1.0 V vs. SCE for the optimized time of 300 s.

MnCo2O4/NFF was prepared by a facile hydrothermal and calcination process. Prior to the synthesis, Ni–Fe foam (3.0 cm × 8.0 cm) was cleaned through sonication consecutively in HCl solution (3 M), ethanol, and deionized water (30 min each), and then dried in a vacuum drying oven. 4 mmol MnCl2·4H2O (0.788 g), 8 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O (2.328 g), 12 mmol NH4F (0.444 g), and 24 mmol urea (1.454 g) were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water under vigorous magnetic stirring for 10 min. The above clear solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and a piece of pretreated NFF was vertically immersed into the as-prepared solution. The autoclave was sealed and kept in the oven at 150 °C for 12 h then cooled naturally to room temperature. NFF loaded with the MnCo precursor was washed several times with ethanol and DI water to remove the residual reactants, and then dried in the oven. Subsequently, the resultant composite was annealed at 350 °C for 2 h (heating rate: 5 °C min−1) in air to convert into MnCo2O4/NFF. For comparison, Co3O4 was also directly grown on NFF under the same conditions without MnCl2·4H2O.

A piece of NF (2 × 5 cm2) was cleaned with 3 M HCl, acetone, ethanol and water under ultrasonication in sequence for 15 min each time to remove the nickel oxides and organics before use. FeCl3·6H2O (0.6 mmol) and 1,4-BDC (0.6 mmol) were dissolved in 70 mL DMF. After stirring for several minutes, the yellow solution together with a piece of cleaned NF was poured into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 150 °C for 15 h. After cooling to room temperature, the NF was taken out, and rinsed with ethanol and water, and dried overnight at 50 °C.

N-rGOs were prepared according to the literature . 10 mg of graphene oxide were sonicated in 35 ml of water to obtain a deep brown solution followed by the addition of 10 mg of urea. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 ml autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12 h. The system was then allowed to cool to room temperature and the products were deposited at the bottom of the vessel. The final products can be well-dispersed in 20 ml of N -methylpyrrolidone (NMP) by sonication.

For the preparation of iron sulfide electrocatalysts, four sets of solvents with a fixed volume of 80 mL, were prepared by varying the ratio of EG and DI water. The ratios between DI water : EG were 0 : 80, 20 : 60, 40 : 40 and 60 : 20. About 30 mmol FeCl3 and 60 mmolthioureawere dissolved in the mixed solvent using magnetic stirring. The solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and locked for 6 hours inside an oven preheated at 190 °C. The autoclave was kept inside the oven to cool down to room temperature. Thereafter, the materials were filtered through vacuum filtration and washed several times with DI water and absolute ethanol. Then the materials were collected and kept overnight at 60 °C inside a vacuum oven. The prepared materials were denoted as FS X (like FS 20, FS 40 etc.) where X stands for the amount of EG used in the mixed solvent. FS 0 was also prepared following the previously described procedure except for the use of EG solvent was avoided.

All reagents were used without additional treatment. Ni foams were cut into rectangular pieces of 2 cm×4 cm. The Ni foam pieces were then put into 20 mL acetone to be sonicated for 20 min, and washed by deionized water for three times. The Ni foam pieces were further put into 20 mL 2 M hydrochloric acid to be sonicated for 10 min and washed by deionized water and ethanol for three times in sequence to remove nickel oxides and grease, and then dried in a 60 °C vacuum oven for 12 h. About 60 mg thioacetamide (TAA), 45 mg anhydrous sodium molybdate and 110 mg non-ionic surfactants (P123/F127/L31) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water. The as-prepared rectangular Ni foam was then put into the above mixed solution and stirred magnetically at room temperature for 30 min. The prepared solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 24 h, where the Ni3S2/MoS2 was in-situ grown on Ni foams. The integrated foam was then washed by 20 mL deionized water and 20 mL ethanol for three times, and then dried in a 60 °C vacuum oven for 12 h.

Synthesis of IrO2/birnessite catalysts. In a typical procedure, Mn(NO3)2 (113 mmol L−1), Co(NO3)2 (170 mmol L−1) and H2IrCl6 (56.7 mmol L−1), with required volume, were dissolved in 15 mL of 1 M NaOH. After homogenization and shaking, the mixture was aged for 10 min. No assistant agent including surfactant or H2O2 was added, making the synthesis a greener and safer approach. After being tightly sealed, the autoclave was kept in an oven. The solution was slowly heated to 220 °C and was maintained at that temperature for 12 h to obtain fine crystals. The solution was then naturally cooled at ambient temperature. The brown-black precipitate was collected and transferred to a suction filter. After washing the solid several times with deionized water, the residual ions and unwanted species were almost completely removed from hydrothermally prepared crystals. The retentate on the filter was stowed in a 50 mL beaker and dried in an oven at 60 °C for dehydration. Then, the final sample was collected in a ceramic crucible and annealed at 400 °C for 6 h to achieve fine crystallinity of the as-synthesized composites. In the preparation of birnessite MnO2 (or Mn−Co(8 : 2)-Bir), a mixture of 0.5 mL of 30 % H2O2 and 15 mL of 1 M NaOH in the Mn(NO3)2 (or Mn(NO3)2 and Co(NO3)2 with required volume) precursor solution, and a dark brown suspension was immediately observed. The suspension was transferred to the autoclaves, which was later followed by the same procedure as IrO2/birnessite catalysts.

Synthesis of Ir1-2xMnxCoxO2 catalysts. In the Mn−Co doped IrO2 catalyst preparation, Mn(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2 and H2IrCl6, with required amount, were dissolved in 15 mL of 1 M NaOH. The mix solution was transferred to the autoclaves, and followed by the same procedure as IrO2/birnessite catalysts. In a synthesis where no Mn and Co precursors were added, IrO2 was the final obtained product.

Synthesis of NiCo-LHSs@NiCo-TEOA NA/NF Precursors: All chemicals used in the experiments are analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Prior to the hydrothermal treatment, the Ni foam was first cut into small pieces with a size of 2 cm × 3 cm, and then cleaned by ultrasonication in 2 m HCl for 30 min to remove the surface oxide layer, following the sequential wash with water and ethanol and drying at 60 °C for 10 min. To synthesize NiCo-LHSs@NiCo-TEOA precursors, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (1.5 mmol) and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1.5 mmol) were dissolved in the mixed solvent consisted of 25 mL deionized water and 5 mL triethanolamine in a 50 mL beaker, and the clean NF (1 cm × 3 cm) was then immersed in the resultant solution. This reaction system was then kept at 80 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the as-obtained NiCo-LHSs@NiCo-TEOA/NF precursors were washed with deionized water three times and dried at 60 °C. The large-sized current collectors were used for NiCo-LHSs@NiCo-TEOA precursor growth to achieve large-sized electrode materials. To grow NiCo-LHSs@NiCo-TEOA precursor on large-sized current collectors, we just need to replace small size of NF (1 cm × 3 cm) and beaker (50 mL) with a large size of NF (10 cm × 10 cm) and beaker (1000 mL) during synthesis.

Nickel foam (NF) was cut into small pieces with dimensions of 2.0 × 3.0 cm2. These small pieces were ultrasonicated for 10 min with an aqueous solution (3.0 m HCl), ethanol, and ultrapure water, respectively. Taking Co0.4NiS@NF as an example, a mixed solution of CoCl2·6H2O (0.4 mmol) and thiourea (1.5 mmol) dissolved in ultrapure water (30 mL) was prepared and transferred into a stainless steel autoclave with Teflon liner (50 mL). Then a piece of small NF was immersed vertically into the autoclave reactor. Subsequently, the autoclave was heated in a preheated oven at 140 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the autoclave reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The prepared Co0.4NiS@NF was taken out and rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water. Thereafter, it was dried inside a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The preparations of Ni3S2@NF, Co0.1NiS@NF, Co0.2NiS@NF, and Co0.6NiS@NF were similar to Co0.4NiS@NF except for varying the amounts of Co precursor (CoCl2·6H2O) from 0 to 0.1, 0.2, and 0.6 mmol.

Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O nanoneedle arrays were grown on carbon cloth fibers by a hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 70 mL solution of 0.05 m Co(NO3)2, 0.125 m NH4F, and 0.25 m urea was transferred in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. Three pieces of carbon cloth (3 cm×3 cm) were pretreated by O2 plasma for 40 min and then dipped into the solution. Polyimide tape was employed to fix carbon cloths vertically. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 120 °C for 6 h in furnace, followed by naturally cooling down to room temperature. Carbon cloths with pink Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O coating were then washed with water, sonicated for 5 min, and dried at 70 °C for 3 h. As-synthesized Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O nanoneedle arrays were used as precursors for the synthesis of CoP and Co3O4 nanoneedle arrays. Pink precipitates were also collected from the bottom of autoclave and used as precursors for the synthesis of CoP-based nanoneedle powders. CoP-based nanoneedle arrays and powders were synthesized by phosphidation of hydrothermally obtained Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O precursor with PH3. In a typical procedure, three pieces of carbon cloth with Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O precursor (3 cm×3 cm each) and 2.7 g of NaH2PO2⋅H2O were put in two porcelain boats.

Fabrication of NC–NiCu–NiCuN Nanowires Array: Nickel foam was put into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. 1 mmol NiCl2·6H2O, 2 mmol CuCl2·6H2O, and 0.15 m glucose/urea were dissolved into 15 mL deionized water. After hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 6 h, NiCu precursor was obtained thorough washing with deionized water and then dried at 60 °C. The NiCu precursor was annealed up to 400 °C in N2 atmosphere and then was treated in H2/NH3 atmosphere, resulting into the formation of NiCu–NiCuN nanowire array encapsulated in NC framework (noted as NC–NiCu–NiCuN). The structural characterization and electrochemical measurements of different samples have been shown in the Supporting Information.

The Fe2O3 hollow nanotubes were prepared by a facile hydrothermal reaction according to the work of Jia et al. Typically, 260 mg of FeCl3 and 6.62 mg of NH4H2PO4 were dissolved in 80 mL deionized water. After vigorously stirring, the mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 48 h. The suspension was centrifuged and washed several times with ultrapure water and ethanol. Finally, the scarlet Fe2O3 nanotubes were obtained by drying the precipitate at 60 °C overnight.

The schematic scenario in Figure S1 (Supporting Information) presents the preparation of porous Ni–Co–S nanoflakes by a two-step hydrothermal method. 3D porous Ni foam with outstanding electrical conductivity served as a growth substrate (Figure S1a, Supporting Information). In the first step, NiCo-layered double hydroxide (LDH) nanosheets were uniformly synthesized by high-temperature reaction in a methanol solution containing Ni2+ and Co2+ (Figure S1b, Supporting Information). Then, the NiCo-LDH on Ni foam was immersed in thioacetamide solution at 150 °C for 6 h to form NiCoS nanosheets (Figure S1c, Supporting Information). After the two-step reaction, the color of the Ni foam surface entirely changed to black, indicating the uniform distribution of nickel and cobalt sulfide on the Ni foam.
